female-45-postop.name: Linda Chen
female-45-postop.chiefComplaint: My left leg has been hurting for 2 days

missedConsequences.Deep Vein Thrombosis: Untreated DVT leads to pulmonary embolism, which can be fatal. Also causes chronic post-thrombotic syndrome.
missedConsequences.Pulmonary Embolism: Approximately 50% of untreated proximal DVTs lead to PE. Massive PE causes sudden death.
missedConsequences.Cellulitis: If misdiagnosed as cellulitis, anticoagulation is delayed and clot can propagate or embolize.

commonPitfalls: Not using Wells score to risk-stratify before testing
Ordering D-dimer in high-probability patients (will likely be positive regardless)
Accepting negative D-dimer without considering pre-test probability
Diagnosing cellulitis without considering DVT when risk factors present
Not asking about dyspnea - may already have subclinical PE
Delaying anticoagulation while waiting for confirmatory imaging

mcqs.question: The patient had knee surgery 10 days ago and has unilateral leg swelling. Her Wells score is 4 (recent surgery +1, calf swelling >3cm +1, pitting edema +1, entire leg swollen +1). What is the best diagnostic approach?

options.text: Check D-dimer first; if negative, DVT is ruled out
options.explanation: Wells score ≥2 is 'DVT likely'. D-dimer has poor specificity in post-surgical patients and should not be used to rule out DVT in high-probability patients.
With Wells score ≥2 ('DVT likely'), proceed directly to ultrasound. D-dimer is only useful to rule out DVT in low-probability patients (Wells <2).
This would miss DVT. Risk factors and unilateral swelling warrant DVT workup before assuming cellulitis.

options.text: CT venography is the gold standard and should be ordered
options.explanation: Compression ultrasound is first-line for DVT diagnosis. It's non-invasive and highly accurate for proximal DVT.

mcqs.question: Ultrasound confirms acute left femoral vein DVT. The patient has no contraindications to anticoagulation. What is the most appropriate initial treatment?

options.explanation: Warfarin alone is dangerous initially - it's prothrombotic in first few days. Need bridging with heparin or LMWH, or use DOAC.
Aspirin is not adequate anticoagulation for DVT treatment.
DOACs are first-line for most DVT patients. They don't require bridging (some have loading doses) and have better safety profile than warfarin.
IVC filters are only for patients with contraindications to anticoagulation or recurrent PE despite anticoagulation.

allDiagnoses.name: Deep Vein Thrombosis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Unilateral leg swelling, pain, and erythema with recent surgery. Classic presentation with clear risk factors.

allDiagnoses.name: Post-surgical edema
allDiagnoses.rationale: Swelling after knee surgery is common, but asymmetry and pain warrant DVT evaluation.

allDiagnoses.name: Pulmonary Embolism
allDiagnoses.rationale: Must assess for PE symptoms. DVT and PE are same disease spectrum (VTE).

allDiagnoses.name: Cellulitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Erythema and warmth overlap with DVT. However, cellulitis rarely causes significant swelling proximal to infection.

allDiagnoses.name: Necrotizing Fasciitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Rapidly progressive, pain out of proportion. Surgical emergency if present.

allDiagnoses.name: Superficial Thrombophlebitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Involves superficial veins with palpable cord. Less concerning than DVT but can extend.

allDiagnoses.name: Baker's Cyst Rupture
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can mimic DVT with sudden calf swelling. History of knee pathology helps differentiate.

allDiagnoses.name: Lymphedema
allDiagnoses.rationale: Usually chronic and bilateral, non-pitting. Unlikely to be acute post-surgical presentation.

allDiagnoses.name: Muscle Strain/Hematoma
allDiagnoses.rationale: Post-surgical hematoma possible but wouldn't cause diffuse leg swelling.

female-45-postop.oldcartsNarrative: "This started about 2 days ago. The pain is in my left calf and lower leg. It's been getting progressively worse - started as an ache and now it's more constant. The pain is like a deep soreness or cramping feeling, probably a 5 out of 10. It hurts more when I walk or flex my foot upward. Resting helps a little but it doesn't go away completely. The pain doesn't really go anywhere else. It's worse when I'm up and about. It's affecting my ability to walk normally - I'm limping to avoid putting weight on it."

historyQuestions.text: When did this start? How long has your leg been hurting?
historyQuestions.answer: It started about 2 days ago. First it was just an ache in my calf, but it's been getting progressively worse. Now the whole leg feels heavy and swollen.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Timeline important - DVT typically develops over days. Acute onset might suggest other causes.

historyQuestions.text: Have you noticed any swelling?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, now that you mention it - my left leg does look bigger than my right. My pants feel tighter on that side and my ankle looks puffy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Unilateral leg swelling is key finding for DVT - should be discovered through history/exam, not given in chief complaint

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any recent surgeries or been hospitalized?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, I had knee arthroscopy about 10 days ago for a torn meniscus. It was outpatient - went home the same day.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Recent surgery is major DVT risk factor (+1 Wells score)

historyQuestions.text: How active have you been since the surgery?
historyQuestions.answer: I've been mostly resting at home with my leg up. Haven't been moving around much - just hobbling to the bathroom and back to the couch.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Immobility is major DVT risk factor - prolonged rest increases venous stasis

historyQuestions.text: What medications do you take?
historyQuestions.answer: Just my birth control pills. And ibuprofen for pain after the surgery.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Oral contraceptives are significant DVT risk factor - estrogen increases clotting

historyQuestions.text: Have you noticed any changes to the skin on your leg?
historyQuestions.answer: It does look a little red and feels warm to touch, especially around my calf.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Erythema and warmth can indicate DVT or cellulitis - need to differentiate

historyQuestions.text: Any shortness of breath or chest pain?
historyQuestions.answer: No, my breathing is fine. No chest pain either.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Must screen for PE symptoms - DVT can embolize. Absence is reassuring but doesn't rule out

historyQuestions.text: Have you or anyone in your family ever had blood clots?
historyQuestions.answer: No, not that I know of. No one in my family has had clots.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Personal or family history of VTE suggests thrombophilia and increases risk

historyQuestions.text: Do you smoke?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I've never smoked.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Smoking increases DVT risk, especially combined with OCP use

historyQuestions.text: Any history of cancer?
historyQuestions.answer: No, never had cancer.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Malignancy is risk factor and affects anticoagulation choice/duration

historyQuestions.text: What other medical problems do you have?
historyQuestions.answer: I'm pretty healthy. Just seasonal allergies and sometimes migraines. I had my appendix out years ago, but otherwise no major health issues.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Complete medical history needed for anticoagulation decision and risk assessment

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any allergies to medications?
historyQuestions.answer: No medication allergies. Just seasonal allergies to pollen.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important before starting anticoagulation therapy

historyQuestions.text: Any other surgeries besides the recent knee procedure?
historyQuestions.answer: Just my appendix when I was 22 and a C-section with my daughter 8 years ago. Both went fine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prior surgical history and pregnancy history relevant for VTE risk assessment

historyQuestions.text: Any medical problems that run in your family?
historyQuestions.answer: My mom has high blood pressure and my dad had a stroke a few years ago. My aunt had breast cancer. No blood clots that I know of though.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Family history of VTE suggests possible hereditary thrombophilia

historyQuestions.text: How much alcohol do you drink?
historyQuestions.answer: A glass of wine with dinner sometimes, maybe 2-3 times a week. Nothing heavy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Alcohol use relevant for bleeding risk on anticoagulation

historyQuestions.text: Have you seen anyone else about this or been back to your surgeon?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I called the surgeon's office yesterday and they said some swelling was normal, but today it's so much worse I decided to come in.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prior evaluation and advice affects current management

historyQuestions.text: When did you last see your regular doctor?
historyQuestions.answer: About 3 months ago for my annual physical. Everything was normal.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Recent baseline health status and PCP relationship for follow-up

historyQuestions.text: What do you do for work?
historyQuestions.answer: I'm an accountant. Desk job, sit most of the day. I've been working from home since the surgery.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Sedentary occupation contributes to VTE risk, especially post-operatively

historyQuestions.text: Who do you live with?
historyQuestions.answer: My husband and our 8-year-old daughter. My husband's been helping with everything since the surgery.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Social support important for outpatient anticoagulation management

historyQuestions.text: Any recent long car rides or flights?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I've just been at home recovering. Haven't gone anywhere.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prolonged travel is additional VTE risk factor

historyQuestions.text: What are you most worried about?
historyQuestions.answer: I'm scared it might be a blood clot. I read online that those can be dangerous and travel to your lungs. I just want to make sure I'm okay.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Patient understanding and concerns important for shared decision-making

physicalExam.finding: BP 132/82, HR 88, RR 16, Temp 99.2°F, SpO2 98% on RA
physicalExam.reasoning: Low-grade fever can be seen in DVT. Normal SpO2 reassuring against large PE.

physicalExam.finding: Left leg visibly more swollen than right from thigh to ankle. Erythema over left calf. Superficial veins more prominent on left. Right leg normal.
physicalExam.reasoning: Unilateral swelling with erythema and prominent veins classic for DVT

physicalExam.finding: Left calf: 42 cm. Right calf: 36 cm. Difference of 6 cm (>3 cm asymmetry).
physicalExam.reasoning: >3 cm asymmetry in calf circumference is Wells criterion (+1)

physicalExam.finding: 2+ pitting edema left leg from foot to mid-thigh. Right leg no edema.
physicalExam.reasoning: Pitting edema in symptomatic leg is Wells criterion (+1)

physicalExam.finding: Tenderness to palpation along the left calf, particularly posterior compartment. No palpable cord.
physicalExam.reasoning: Calf tenderness along deep veins supports DVT

physicalExam.finding: Pain in left calf with passive dorsiflexion of foot. Negative on right.
physicalExam.reasoning: Homans sign - neither sensitive nor specific, but present here

physicalExam.finding: Left leg notably warmer than right on palpation.
physicalExam.reasoning: Increased warmth from venous congestion/inflammation

physicalExam.finding: Knee arthroscopy portals well-healed, no signs of surgical site infection.
physicalExam.reasoning: Rules out surgical site infection as cause of symptoms

physicalExam.finding: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes or crackles.
physicalExam.reasoning: Reassuring against clinically significant PE

physicalExam.finding: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1/S2. No murmurs or gallops.
physicalExam.reasoning: Baseline cardiac exam

labs.name: D-dimer
labs.result: Not indicated - Wells DVT score ≥2 ('DVT likely') means proceed directly to imaging. D-dimer only for Wells <2.
labs.reasoning: D-dimer rules OUT DVT only when pretest probability is LOW (Wells <2). For patients >50yo, use AGE-ADJUSTED threshold (age × 10 μg/L) to improve specificity. Here, Wells ≥2 = proceed to ultrasound regardless of D-dimer.

labs.name: CBC
labs.reasoning: Baseline for anticoagulation. Rules out thrombocytopenia.

labs.name: BMP
labs.reasoning: Renal function for anticoagulant dosing

labs.name: PT/INR, PTT
labs.reasoning: Baseline coagulation before starting anticoagulation

imaging.name: Compression Ultrasound of Left Leg
imaging.result: Non-compressible left common femoral and femoral veins with echogenic thrombus. Popliteal vein also involved. Findings consistent with acute extensive proximal DVT.
imaging.reasoning: Gold standard for DVT diagnosis. Non-compressibility is diagnostic.

imaging.name: CT Pulmonary Angiography
imaging.result: Consider if symptoms of PE develop
imaging.reasoning: Not indicated currently without PE symptoms. Would obtain if dyspnea/chest pain/hypoxia.

treatments.name: Direct Oral Anticoagulant (Rivaroxaban/Apixaban)
treatments.result: Rivaroxaban 15mg BID x21 days, then 20mg daily. Or Apixaban 10mg BID x7 days, then 5mg BID. Duration: 3 months for provoked VTE.
treatments.reasoning: Per CHEST 2021: DOACs are PREFERRED over LMWH→warfarin for most VTE patients. No bridging needed. Rivaroxaban/apixaban can be started immediately.

treatments.name: LMWH bridge to Warfarin
treatments.result: Enoxaparin 1mg/kg SubQ BID + Warfarin, continue LMWH until INR 2-3 x2 consecutive days. More monitoring required.
treatments.reasoning: Use if DOAC contraindicated: severe renal failure (CrCl <30), antiphospholipid syndrome, cost/access issues. Requires INR monitoring.

treatments.name: Compression stockings
treatments.result: 30-40 mmHg graduated compression stockings
treatments.reasoning: May help with symptoms and reduce post-thrombotic syndrome risk

treatments.name: Leg elevation
treatments.result: Elevate leg above heart level when resting
treatments.reasoning: Reduces swelling and discomfort

treatments.name: Early ambulation
treatments.result: Encouraged once anticoagulated - bed rest NOT recommended
treatments.reasoning: Early ambulation is safe and may improve outcomes vs bed rest

treatments.name: Pain management
treatments.result: NSAIDs generally avoided with anticoagulation. Acetaminophen preferred.
treatments.reasoning: Avoid NSAIDs due to bleeding risk with anticoagulation

consults.name: Hematology consult
consults.result: Not routinely needed for provoked DVT. Consider for recurrent VTE or unprovoked DVT for thrombophilia workup.
consults.reasoning: This is provoked DVT (surgery + OCPs) - routine hematology consult not needed

disposition.name: Discharge with outpatient anticoagulation
disposition.result: Appropriate for uncomplicated DVT with reliable follow-up
disposition.reasoning: Most DVTs can be treated outpatient with DOACs. No need for admission.

disposition.name: Admit to hospital
disposition.result: Consider for massive DVT, high bleeding risk, or unreliable follow-up
disposition.reasoning: Most DVTs managed outpatient. Admit for phlegmasia, concurrent PE, social concerns.

wellsDVT.name: Wells Criteria for DVT
wellsDVT.description: Clinical prediction rule for DVT probability

components.criterion: Active cancer (treatment within 6 months or palliative)
Paralysis, paresis, or recent cast immobilization of lower extremity
Bedridden >3 days or major surgery within 12 weeks
Localized tenderness along deep venous system
Calf swelling >3 cm compared to asymptomatic leg
Pitting edema confined to symptomatic leg
Alternative diagnosis as likely as or more likely than DVT

thisPatient.clinicalAction: With Wells ≥2, skip D-dimer and go directly to ultrasound. D-dimer only useful when Wells <2.

unlikely.action: D-dimer; if negative (<500), DVT excluded without imaging

likely.action: Compression ultrasound; D-dimer not useful at this probability

wellsDVT.source: Wells PS et al. N Engl J Med. 2003;349(13):1227-1235

actualDiagnosis.name: Acute Proximal Deep Vein Thrombosis (Left Femoral and Popliteal Veins) - Provoked
keyFindings: Unilateral left leg swelling, >3cm asymmetry in calf circumference
Pitting edema, warmth, erythema of affected leg
Calf tenderness with positive Homans sign
Clear risk factors: recent surgery + immobility + OCP use
Wells score = 4 ('DVT likely')
Compression ultrasound confirming non-compressible femoral and popliteal veins
No symptoms of PE (no dyspnea, chest pain, normal SpO2)
teachingPoints: VTE = DVT + PE are same disease spectrum. Always screen DVT patients for PE symptoms and vice versa.

Wells Score for DVT: Cancer(+1), Paralysis/immobilization(+1), Bedridden>3d or surgery<4wk(+1), Tenderness along deep veins(+1), Entire leg swollen(+1), Calf>3cm vs other(+1), Pitting edema(+1), Collateral veins(+1), Prior DVT(+1), Alternative diagnosis as likely(-2). Score≥2='DVT likely', <2='DVT unlikely'.
D-dimer: Only to RULE OUT when pretest probability is LOW (Wells<2). For age >50, use AGE-ADJUSTED threshold = age × 10 μg/L (e.g., 65yo cutoff = 650).
Compression ultrasound: First-line imaging. Non-compressibility = diagnostic for DVT.
Proximal DVT (above knee): Higher PE risk, ALWAYS treat. Distal DVT: Can consider serial imaging vs anticoagulation.
DOACs are FIRST-LINE per CHEST 2021: Rivaroxaban or apixaban preferred. No bridging needed. Better safety than warfarin.
Treatment DURATION: Provoked VTE (surgery, immobility, estrogen) = 3 months. Unprovoked = at least 3 months, consider extended if low bleeding risk.
Provoked vs Unprovoked: Provoked has clear transient risk factor → lower recurrence risk → 3 months treatment. Unprovoked → higher recurrence → consider indefinite anticoagulation.
Early ambulation is SAFE and recommended - old dogma of bed rest is WRONG.
IVC filter: ONLY if anticoagulation absolutely contraindicated. NOT for 'added protection' - increases DVT recurrence.
Approximately 50% of untreated proximal DVTs lead to PE. This is why we treat urgently.

evidenceBasedPearls.topic: DOACs vs Warfarin (EINSTEIN, RE-COVER, AMPLIFY, Hokusai-VTE)
evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: DOACs are non-inferior to warfarin for VTE treatment with significantly less bleeding
evidenceBasedPearls.source: Cochrane Review, Robertson L et al., 2017
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: DOACs are first-line for VTE per CHEST guidelines. Better safety profile, no monitoring needed
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: Major bleeding: DOACs 1.1% vs Warfarin 1.7% (RR 0.63). VTE recurrence similar (~2%)

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Rivaroxaban 15mg BID x21d then 20mg daily is as effective as enoxaparin/warfarin bridging
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Rivaroxaban can be started immediately without heparin bridging
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: Recurrent VTE: 2.1% vs 3.0% (non-inferior). Major bleeding: 0.8% vs 1.2%

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Apixaban 10mg BID x7d then 5mg BID was as effective as enoxaparin/warfarin with 69% less major bleeding
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Apixaban offers best bleeding safety profile among DOACs for VTE
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: Major bleeding: 0.6% vs 1.8% (RR 0.31). Recurrent VTE similar: 2.3% vs 2.7%

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Extended apixaban (2.5mg or 5mg BID) reduced VTE recurrence vs placebo after initial treatment
evidenceBasedPearls.source: AMPLIFY-EXT, Agnelli G et al., NEJM 2013
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: After 6-12 months, can continue apixaban 2.5mg BID for extended prophylaxis in unprovoked VTE
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: VTE recurrence: 1.7% (2.5mg) vs 8.8% (placebo). NNT 14 over 12 months

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Age-adjusted D-dimer cutoff (age × 10 μg/L for age >50) increases specificity without missing PE
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Use age × 10 μg/L cutoff for patients >50 years to reduce unnecessary imaging
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: Specificity improved from 34.4% to 46.0% in patients ≥75 years. False negative rate only 0.3%

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: If all 8 PERC criteria negative in low-risk patient, PE can be ruled out without D-dimer
evidenceBasedPearls.source: Kline JA et al., J Thromb Haemost 2004; Singh B et al., Ann Intern Med 2012
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: PERC: Age <50, HR <100, SpO2 >94%, no hemoptysis, no estrogen, no surgery/trauma, no prior VTE, no unilateral leg swelling
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: Sensitivity 97.4%, NPV 99.1% when gestalt pretest probability is low (<15%)

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Most DVT patients can be safely treated as outpatients
evidenceBasedPearls.source: Othieno R et al., Cochrane Database Syst Rev 2018
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Consider outpatient treatment for uncomplicated DVT with adequate home support
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: No difference in recurrent VTE, mortality, or major bleeding with outpatient vs inpatient treatment

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: CDT did not reduce post-thrombotic syndrome in proximal DVT
evidenceBasedPearls.source: ATTRACT Trial, Vedantham S et al., NEJM 2017
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Routine CDT not recommended for DVT. Reserve for severe iliofemoral DVT with limb threat
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: PTS at 24 months: 47% CDT vs 48% anticoagulation alone. Major bleeding higher with CDT (1.7% vs 0.3%)

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Women with unprovoked VTE and HERDOO2 score ≤1 can safely stop anticoagulation
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: HERDOO2: Hyperpigmentation, Edema, Redness, D-dimer >250, Obesity, Older age. If ≤1 = low recurrence risk
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: Annual VTE recurrence 3.0% if HERDOO2 ≤1 vs 8.1% if >1

evidenceBasedPearls.topic: Aspirin for Extended VTE Prevention (ASPIRE, WARFASA)
evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Aspirin reduces VTE recurrence by ~30% after completing anticoagulation
evidenceBasedPearls.source: Brighton TA et al. (ASPIRE), NEJM 2012; Becattini C et al. (WARFASA), NEJM 2012
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: If stopping anticoagulation after unprovoked VTE, aspirin 100mg daily provides some protection
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: VTE recurrence: 4.8% (aspirin) vs 6.5% (placebo) per year (RR 0.68)

patientBackground.recentEvents: Sarah had knee surgery 10 days ago and has been mostly bedrest since. Yesterday she and her husband drove 6 hours to visit family. She sat in the car most of the way with only one quick stop. This morning she noticed her left calf was swollen and tender.
patientBackground.recentIllnesses: Recovery from knee arthroscopy has been going well otherwise. She stopped taking the baby aspirin her surgeon recommended because it was upsetting her stomach.
patientBackground.fluidIntake: Did not drink much during the car ride because she did not want to have to stop for bathroom breaks. Maybe one bottle of water over 6 hours.
patientBackground.contextualFactors: Takes oral contraceptives. Her mother had a blood clot during pregnancy. Sarah is now very worried this runs in their family. She had been feeling good enough to travel and did not think the long car ride would be a problem.
patientBackground.supportSystem: Husband Michael is with her and is very concerned. They are visiting his parents who live nearby. Her own parents are calling for updates.
patientBackground.healthBeliefs: Sarah is usually healthy and proactive about her care. She feels guilty for not being more careful after surgery. She did not realize the car trip was risky. She wants to understand what happened and how to prevent it in the future.
patientBackground.previousERVisits: Last ER visit was in college for a sprained ankle. No previous clots or hospitalizations.
patientBackground.occupation: Elementary school teacher. Summer break so she had time for the surgery. Good insurance through school district.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient was pregnant?
whatIfScenarios.description: The patient is 28 weeks pregnant with the same leg symptoms

chiefComplaint.original: My left leg has been hurting for 2 days
chiefComplaint.modified: My left leg has been hurting for 2 days. I'm 28 weeks pregnant.

feedback.correct: Excellent! Pregnancy is a major VTE risk factor. Key differences: D-dimer normally elevated in pregnancy (less useful), DOACs contraindicated - must use LMWH.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of VTE risk. Remember: DOACs are contraindicated in pregnancy. What anticoagulant would you use?
feedback.incorrect: Pregnancy creates a hypercoagulable state - VTE risk is 5-10x higher. This changes diagnostic approach (D-dimer less useful) and treatment (LMWH only).

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Unlike non-pregnant patients, D-dimer is unreliable (often elevated normally in pregnancy). DOACs are contraindicated - use LMWH (enoxaparin) throughout pregnancy and postpartum. Compression ultrasound remains diagnostic test of choice.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the swelling was bilateral?
whatIfScenarios.description: Both legs are swollen equally over the past 3 weeks

chiefComplaint.original: My left leg has been hurting for 2 days
chiefComplaint.modified: Both my legs have been getting swollen over the past 3 weeks

expectedReasoning.topDiagnosis: Systemic cause of edema (CHF, cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome)

feedback.correct: Excellent! Bilateral symmetric edema shifts the differential dramatically from DVT to systemic causes - heart, kidney, or liver disease.
feedback.partial: Good recognition that bilateral changes the picture. Consider what systemic processes cause bilateral leg edema.
feedback.incorrect: Bilateral symmetric edema is rarely DVT. Think about systemic causes: right heart failure, nephrotic syndrome, cirrhosis, medications (CCBs, steroids).

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Unilateral edema = think DVT/cellulitis. Bilateral symmetric edema = think CHF (check JVD, orthopnea), nephrotic syndrome (proteinuria, hypoalbuminemia), cirrhosis (ascites, stigmata), or medications. Wells score doesn't apply to bilateral edema.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the clot was only in the calf veins?
whatIfScenarios.description: Ultrasound shows isolated calf vein thrombosis (peroneal vein)

chiefComplaint.original: My left leg has been hurting for 2 days
chiefComplaint.modified: My left calf has been hurting for 2 days

feedback.correct: Good understanding! Isolated distal DVT is controversial - can anticoagulate OR do serial ultrasounds to monitor for proximal extension. Lower PE risk than proximal DVT.
feedback.partial: Consider the difference between proximal and distal DVT. Not all DVTs require the same approach.
feedback.incorrect: Isolated calf DVT has lower PE risk and treatment is debated. Options: anticoagulate OR serial ultrasounds at 1 week to ensure no proximal extension.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Proximal DVT (above knee) = always anticoagulate due to high PE risk. Distal DVT (below knee) = can either anticoagulate or do serial imaging. If serial imaging chosen, repeat US in 1 week - if extending proximally, then treat.

questions.stem: Based on the clinical presentation, which is the MOST appropriate next step?

options.text: Systematic history and focused physical examination
options.explanation: A targeted history and physical exam is the essential first step in clinical reasoning, guiding hypothesis generation and differential diagnosis.
Empiric treatment before adequate evaluation risks missing the correct diagnosis and may cause harm.
Diagnostic testing should be hypothesis-driven, not shotgun; targeted tests based on clinical reasoning yield better outcomes.
Consultation may be appropriate later but should follow initial clinical assessment and diagnostic reasoning.

questions.stem: Which cognitive bias poses the greatest risk in this clinical scenario?

options.explanation: Availability bias (overweighting recent experiences) is common but not the most frequent source of error in this scenario.
Anchoring (fixating on initial impression) is a risk but can be mitigated by systematic differential generation.
Premature closure — accepting a diagnosis before it is fully verified — is the single most common cognitive error in clinical reasoning across all settings.
Confirmation bias (seeking only supporting evidence) contributes to error but often follows from premature closure.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old presents with petechiae, confusion, renal insufficiency, fever, and platelets of 18,000 with schistocytes on smear. What is the most critical immediate intervention?

options.text: Emergent plasma exchange (plasmapheresis) — do not wait for ADAMTS13 results
options.explanation: This pentad (thrombocytopenia, microangiopathic hemolytic anemia with schistocytes, neurologic symptoms, renal dysfunction, fever) is classic TTP. Per ASH guidelines, plasma exchange must be initiated IMMEDIATELY — mortality without treatment exceeds 90%. Do NOT wait for ADAMTS13 activity levels; start treatment on clinical suspicion.

options.text: Platelet transfusion to prevent intracranial hemorrhage
options.explanation: Platelet transfusion in TTP is CONTRAINDICATED (relative) as it can fuel thrombotic microangiopathy and worsen organ damage. Platelets are consumed in microvascular thrombi, not bleeding.

TTP is distinguished from DIC by NORMAL coagulation studies (PT, PTT, fibrinogen). DIC shows prolonged PT/PTT and low fibrinogen. Treatment differs fundamentally — TTP requires plasmapheresis, not anticoagulation.

options.text: Obtain ADAMTS13 activity level and treat based on results
options.explanation: ADAMTS13 testing takes 2-3 days. With suspected TTP, mortality approaches 90% without treatment. Plasma exchange must begin on clinical suspicion while awaiting confirmatory testing.

male-68-hfref.name: William Jackson
male-68-hfref.variantDescription: Acute decompensated heart failure with reduced ejection fraction

missedConsequences.Acute Decompensated Heart Failure: Untreated leads to respiratory failure, cardiogenic shock, and death.
missedConsequences.Acute Coronary Syndrome: MI can precipitate heart failure; missed ACS has high mortality.
missedConsequences.Pulmonary Embolism: Can mimic or cause right heart failure; missed PE is often fatal.
missedConsequences.Pneumonia: Common precipitant of HF decompensation; untreated worsens outcomes.

commonPitfalls: Not identifying the precipitant (dietary indiscretion, medication non-compliance, ACS, arrhythmia, infection)
Giving excessive IV fluids to a volume-overloaded patient
Not obtaining BNP/NT-proBNP to help differentiate cardiac from pulmonary dyspnea
Missing concurrent ACS - always get ECG and troponin
Inadequate diuresis - 'start low' mentality undertreats congestion
Not addressing underlying cause (new AFib, uncontrolled HTN, ischemia)

mcqs.question: The patient has bilateral crackles, elevated JVP, and peripheral edema. His BNP is 1,840 pg/mL. What is the MOST appropriate initial treatment?

options.text: IV normal saline bolus for hypoperfusion
options.explanation: This patient is volume OVERLOADED, not depleted. IV fluids would worsen pulmonary edema.
Acute decompensated HF with pulmonary edema requires diuresis (IV loop diuretic) and oxygen. Start furosemide 40-80mg IV (or 1-2.5x home dose).
SpO2 89% can often be managed with oxygen and BiPAP. Intubation is for refractory hypoxemia or respiratory fatigue.
Crackles here are from pulmonary edema, not pneumonia. No fever or focal infiltrate described.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is the MOST common precipitant of acute heart failure exacerbation?

options.explanation: ACS/MI is a common precipitant (~15-20% per OPTIMIZE-HF) but not the most common. Always check ECG and troponin as ischemia can precipitate decompensation even without classic chest pain.

Medication non-compliance and dietary sodium excess are the most common precipitants, together accounting for ~40% of HF hospitalizations. Other common precipitants include: arrhythmias (~20-25%), respiratory infections/pneumonia (~25-30%), uncontrolled hypertension (~15%), and ACS/ischemia (~15-20%).
Arrhythmias (especially AFib) are common precipitants (~20-25% of cases) as loss of atrial kick and rapid rate worsen cardiac output. Always check ECG.
PE can precipitate right heart failure but is less common than other precipitants. Consider in appropriate clinical context with risk factors.

mcqs.question: According to 2022 ACC/AHA guidelines, which medication class is now a Class 1 recommendation for ALL patients with HFrEF, regardless of diabetes status?

options.explanation: GLP-1 agonists have cardiovascular benefits but are not part of the core HFrEF GDMT recommendations.

MAJOR 2022 UPDATE: SGLT2 inhibitors are now the FOURTH PILLAR of GDMT for HFrEF, joining ACEi/ARB/ARNI, beta-blockers, and MRAs. They reduce HF hospitalizations and CV death regardless of diabetes status. Start before discharge!
Ivabradine is for symptomatic HFrEF patients already on maximized beta-blocker with HR ≥70. It's not a core GDMT pillar.
Hydralazine-nitrate is an alternative for patients who cannot tolerate ACEi/ARB/ARNI, particularly beneficial in African American patients. Not a universal Class 1 recommendation.

allDiagnoses.name: Acute Decompensated Heart Failure
allDiagnoses.rationale: Dyspnea worse when supine (orthopnea), progressive over days to week. Common in older adults with cardiac history. Need to identify precipitant (dietary, medication, infection, ischemia, arrhythmia).

allDiagnoses.name: Acute Coronary Syndrome
allDiagnoses.rationale: ACS can present as dyspnea without chest pain, especially in elderly and diabetics. Can precipitate HF.

allDiagnoses.name: Pulmonary Embolism
allDiagnoses.rationale: Acute dyspnea with hypoxia. Risk factors include immobility, malignancy, recent surgery.

allDiagnoses.name: Arrhythmia (AFib/flutter, VT)
allDiagnoses.rationale: New arrhythmia can cause or precipitate acute dyspnea. ECG essential.

allDiagnoses.name: Pneumonia
allDiagnoses.rationale: Infection is common cause of dyspnea, can precipitate cardiac decompensation. May have subtle presentation in elderly.

allDiagnoses.name: Tension Pneumothorax
allDiagnoses.rationale: Acute severe dyspnea with unilateral decreased breath sounds. Requires immediate intervention.

allDiagnoses.name: COPD Exacerbation
allDiagnoses.rationale: Progressive dyspnea with cough and wheeze. Would expect history of COPD, smoking.

allDiagnoses.name: Asthma Exacerbation
allDiagnoses.rationale: Acute wheezing and dyspnea. Usually younger patients with known asthma.

allDiagnoses.name: Interstitial Lung Disease
allDiagnoses.rationale: Chronic progressive dyspnea, dry cough. More insidious onset typically.

allDiagnoses.name: Pleural Effusion
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can cause dyspnea, may be secondary to HF, malignancy, or infection.

allDiagnoses.name: Severe Anemia
allDiagnoses.rationale: Anemia can cause dyspnea on exertion. Would expect pallor, fatigue. Check CBC.

allDiagnoses.name: Thyroid Disease (Hyperthyroidism)
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can cause tachycardia, dyspnea, and high-output heart failure. Check thyroid function.

allDiagnoses.name: Anxiety/Panic Disorder
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can cause acute dyspnea, but must rule out organic causes first in this age group.

allDiagnoses.name: Acute Kidney Injury
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can cause volume overload and dyspnea. Often complicates other conditions.

male-68-hfref.oldcartsNarrative: "This started getting worse about a week ago. The breathing trouble is mostly in my chest - feels heavy and tight. It's been constant but definitely worse at night and when I try to do anything. It's a feeling like I can't get enough air in. Nothing really makes it better - I tried my inhaler from years ago but it didn't help. Lying flat makes it worse. The breathing trouble is probably 7 out of 10 right now. A week ago I could walk to my mailbox fine; now I'm winded just going to the bathroom."

historyQuestions.text: When did this start? How long has this been going on?
historyQuestions.answer: It started getting worse about a week ago. A week ago I could walk to my mailbox fine; now I'm winded just going to the bathroom.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Establishing timeline is critical - subacute (1 week) progression suggests decompensation rather than acute event like MI or PE

historyQuestions.text: Have you noticed any swelling anywhere?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, my legs and ankles are really swollen. My shoes don't fit anymore, and when I press on my ankles, it leaves a dent.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Bilateral pitting edema indicates systemic volume overload - key finding students should discover

historyQuestions.text: How do you sleep at night? Can you lie flat?
historyQuestions.answer: I can't lie flat at all anymore - I feel like I'm drowning. I need 4 pillows now just to breathe. A week ago I was fine with 2.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Orthopnea (dyspnea when supine) is classic for left heart failure - fluid redistributes to lungs when lying down

historyQuestions.text: Do you wake up at night gasping for air?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, two nights ago I woke up suddenly feeling like I couldn't breathe. Had to sit up on the side of the bed for about 20 minutes before it got better.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea (PND) - waking from sleep with dyspnea - is highly specific for heart failure

historyQuestions.text: Have you noticed any weight gain?
historyQuestions.answer: I don't weigh myself regularly, but my pants are definitely tighter. I'd guess maybe 10 pounds heavier.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Rapid weight gain (fluid) is sensitive marker of HF decompensation. 1L fluid = 1kg = 2.2 lbs

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any heart problems?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, I had a heart attack 3 years ago. They told me my heart is weak - pumps at about 25%. I have heart failure and see a cardiologist.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Discovers known HFrEF (EF 25%) - establishes this is decompensation of known disease

historyQuestions.text: What medications do you take?
historyQuestions.answer: I take water pills, lisinopril, and carvedilol. But I'll be honest, I ran out of my water pill about a week ago and haven't gotten it refilled.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Patient on 3 of 4 GDMT pillars (ACEi, BB, diuretic) but missing SGLT2i and MRA. Diuretic non-compliance identified as precipitant - per OPTIMIZE-HF, medication non-adherence accounts for 8.9% of HF hospitalizations but has paradoxically LOWER in-hospital mortality (easier to stabilize) with HIGH readmission risk.

historyQuestions.text: Have you been taking your medications regularly? Any changes to your medications?
historyQuestions.answer: I ran out of my water pill - the furosemide - about a week ago and haven't been able to get it refilled. I've been taking the other ones though.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medication non-adherence is one of the most common and modifiable precipitants of HF decompensation

historyQuestions.text: Any changes in your diet recently?
historyQuestions.answer: It was the holidays... I ate a lot of ham, chips, and salty foods. I know I'm supposed to watch my salt but I couldn't resist.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Dietary non-adherence accounts for 5.2% of HF hospitalizations per OPTIMIZE-HF. Combined with medication non-compliance (~14% total), these modifiable factors are the most common precipitants. Sodium causes water retention → volume overload.

historyQuestions.text: How much salt have you been eating? Have you been following a low-salt diet?
historyQuestions.answer: I know I'm supposed to limit salt, but with the holidays I really didn't. Lots of ham, chips, canned soups. Probably way more salt than usual for the past couple weeks.
historyQuestions.reasoning: High sodium intake causes fluid retention and is a common HF precipitant

historyQuestions.text: Any chest pain or pressure?
historyQuestions.answer: No chest pain. This doesn't feel like my heart attack did.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Must rule out ACS as precipitant. Absence of chest pain is reassuring but doesn't exclude ischemia.

historyQuestions.text: Any fevers or chills?
historyQuestions.answer: No fever. No chills either.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CRITICAL: Pneumonia/respiratory infection is the MOST COMMON precipitant (15.3%) AND has the HIGHEST in-hospital mortality (OR 1.60) per OPTIMIZE-HF. Absence of fever here makes pneumonia less likely but doesn't exclude it in elderly.

historyQuestions.text: Any cough? Are you bringing up any phlegm or mucus?
historyQuestions.answer: A little bit of a cough, but it's dry. No phlegm or anything colored.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Dry cough can be from pulmonary congestion (HF) or ACE inhibitor. Productive cough would raise concern for infection.

historyQuestions.text: Any racing heart or palpitations?
historyQuestions.answer: My heart does feel like it's beating fast, but it's regular, not skipping around.
historyQuestions.reasoning: New arrhythmia (especially AFib) can precipitate HF. Regular rhythm here is reassuring.

historyQuestions.text: What other medical problems do you have?
historyQuestions.answer: High blood pressure and diabetes. Been on medications for those for years.
historyQuestions.reasoning: HTN and DM are risk factors for HF and common comorbidities

historyQuestions.text: How has your activity level been? What can you do now compared to before?
historyQuestions.answer: A week ago I could walk to the mailbox and back without stopping. Now I get winded just walking to the bathroom. I can barely do anything without feeling short of breath.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Functional decline quantifies severity of decompensation

historyQuestions.text: Do you smoke or have you ever smoked?
historyQuestions.answer: I quit 10 years ago after my doctor warned me. Smoked about a pack a day for 30 years before that.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Smoking history relevant for COPD as differential

historyQuestions.text: When did this shortness of breath start? How has it changed?
historyQuestions.answer: It's been getting worse over the past week or so. Started with just being more tired, then I noticed I couldn't walk as far without stopping, and the last 2-3 days it's really gotten bad.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Gradual progression over days to weeks is typical for HF decompensation vs acute onset for PE or MI

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any allergies to medications?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, I'm allergic to morphine - it makes me throw up really badly. No other allergies though.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important for treatment planning - morphine historically used for acute HF but now less recommended

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: I had a stent put in my heart after my heart attack 3 years ago. Before that, just my gallbladder out years ago.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prior PCI establishes known CAD as etiology of HFrEF

historyQuestions.text: Any heart problems in your family?
historyQuestions.answer: My father died of a heart attack at 65. My brother had bypass surgery. Heart disease runs in my family.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Strong family history of CAD consistent with ischemic cardiomyopathy etiology

historyQuestions.text: How much alcohol do you drink?
historyQuestions.answer: I used to drink more, but since my heart problems I've cut way back. Maybe a beer on the weekend now and then.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Alcohol can contribute to cardiomyopathy and cause HF decompensation

historyQuestions.text: Who do you live with? Who helps you at home?
historyQuestions.answer: I live with my wife. She helps me with my medications and keeps track of things. She's the one who made me come in today.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Caregiver support important for HF self-management and medication adherence

historyQuestions.text: Have you been hospitalized for your heart before?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, twice in the past year for the same thing - fluid buildup and can't breathe. This feels just like those times.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Recurrent hospitalizations common in HF; identifies need for improved GDMT optimization

historyQuestions.text: When did you last see your cardiologist?
historyQuestions.answer: About 2 months ago. He told me everything was stable and to keep taking my medicines. I was supposed to go back next month.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Recent cardiology follow-up establishes baseline and GDMT optimization status

historyQuestions.text: What are you most worried about right now?
historyQuestions.answer: I can't breathe. I'm scared I'm going to need to go back in the hospital. Every time this happens I wonder if this is the one that'll get me.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Patient understands severity; fear and anxiety common in HF exacerbation

physicalExam.finding: BP 158/94, HR 102, RR 26, Temp 98.2°F, SpO2 89% on room air
physicalExam.reasoning: Hypertension, tachycardia, tachypnea, and hypoxia consistent with acute decompensated HF

physicalExam.finding: Anxious-appearing male in moderate respiratory distress. Sitting upright, unable to lie back. Speaking in short sentences.
physicalExam.reasoning: Orthopnea evident - patient cannot tolerate lying flat. Respiratory distress apparent.

physicalExam.finding: JVP elevated to 12 cm above the sternal angle (normal <4 cm). Positive hepatojugular reflux.
physicalExam.reasoning: Elevated JVP is most specific sign of volume overload/elevated filling pressures

physicalExam.finding: Tachycardic, regular rhythm. S1 normal, S2 normal. S3 gallop present. Soft holosystolic murmur at apex. PMI displaced laterally.
physicalExam.reasoning: S3 gallop indicates volume overload/poor compliance. Displaced PMI = cardiomegaly. Murmur suggests MR (common in dilated LV).

physicalExam.finding: Bilateral crackles (rales) extending to mid-lung fields. No wheezes. Decreased breath sounds at bases.
physicalExam.reasoning: Crackles from pulmonary edema. Decreased bases may indicate small pleural effusions.

physicalExam.finding: 2+ pitting edema bilaterally to the knees. Warm extremities with intact pulses.
physicalExam.reasoning: Bilateral pitting edema = systemic venous congestion. Warm extremities = adequate perfusion ('warm and wet' HF).

physicalExam.finding: Soft, mildly distended. Liver palpable 3 cm below costal margin, mildly tender. No ascites.
physicalExam.reasoning: Hepatomegaly from hepatic congestion (right heart failure component). Tender = acute congestion.

labs.name: BNP or NT-proBNP
labs.result: BNP 1,840 pg/mL (markedly elevated). For BNP: <100 rules out HF, >400 rules in. For NT-proBNP in ED: age <50 use ≥450, age 50-75 use ≥900, age >75 use ≥1800 to rule IN. This patient (age 68) far exceeds threshold.
labs.reasoning: Class 1A recommendation in 2022 ACC/AHA guidelines. BNP/NT-proBNP should be measured in all patients with dyspnea when HF is suspected. Values >5000 pg/mL indicate poor prognosis requiring close monitoring.

labs.name: Troponin
labs.result: Troponin I 0.08 ng/mL (mildly elevated, normal <0.04). Common in acute HF - does not necessarily indicate ACS.
labs.reasoning: Elevated troponin in HF can be from demand ischemia, not plaque rupture. Per OPTIMIZE-HF, ischemia/ACS is precipitant in 14.7% of HF hospitalizations - always get ECG to evaluate.

labs.name: Basic Metabolic Panel
labs.result: Na 132 (hyponatremia), K 4.8, Cl 96, CO2 22, BUN 38, Cr 1.6 (baseline 1.2 = AKI), Glucose 186
labs.reasoning: Hyponatremia is poor prognostic sign in HF. Worsening renal function (creatinine rise) has OR 1.48 for in-hospital mortality and OR 1.46 for 90-day mortality per OPTIMIZE-HF - highest post-discharge mortality of any precipitant.

labs.name: CBC
labs.reasoning: Rules out anemia as contributor. Mild anemia common in chronic HF.

labs.name: Lactate
labs.reasoning: Normal lactate indicates adequate perfusion. Elevated would suggest cardiogenic shock.

labs.name: TSH
labs.reasoning: Thyroid dysfunction can precipitate or worsen HF. Hyperthyroidism causes high-output HF; hypothyroidism worsens systolic function.

labs.name: Procalcitonin
labs.reasoning: Helps distinguish HF from pneumonia. Low procalcitonin (<0.25) argues against bacterial infection.

labs.name: Arterial Blood Gas
labs.result: pH 7.38, pCO2 32, pO2 62, HCO3 22 on room air. Respiratory alkalosis with hypoxemia.
labs.reasoning: Can assess oxygenation and acid-base status, but pulse ox and clinical picture usually sufficient. May help in severe cases.

labs.name: D-dimer
labs.reasoning: D-dimer elevated in HF, DVT, PE, and many conditions. Not helpful when pre-test probability for PE is low. This patient has clear HF precipitants.

labs.name: PT/INR, PTT
labs.reasoning: Baseline coagulation studies. Would be important if considering anticoagulation or if liver congestion suspected.

labs.name: Liver Function Tests
labs.result: AST 48, ALT 42, Alk Phos 98, T. Bili 1.4 (mildly elevated)
labs.reasoning: Mild elevations from hepatic congestion (congestive hepatopathy). Would be more concerning if markedly elevated.

labs.name: Lipid Panel
labs.reasoning: Not urgent in acute setting. Can be checked as outpatient for long-term cardiovascular risk.

labs.name: Hemoglobin A1c
labs.reasoning: Helpful for long-term diabetes management but not urgent for acute HF presentation.

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.result: Cardiomegaly. Bilateral pulmonary vascular congestion with cephalization. Bilateral small pleural effusions. Kerley B lines present. No focal consolidation.
imaging.reasoning: Classic CHF appearance: big heart, congested lungs, effusions, Kerley B lines

imaging.name: Echocardiogram (bedside or formal)
imaging.result: EF 20-25% (severely reduced). Dilated LV. Moderate mitral regurgitation. Elevated estimated PA pressures. No pericardial effusion.
imaging.reasoning: Confirms HFrEF, quantifies severity, identifies MR, rules out tamponade

imaging.name: ECG
imaging.result: Sinus tachycardia at 102. Normal axis. Old Q waves in V1-V3 (prior anterior MI). No acute ST changes.
imaging.reasoning: Rules out arrhythmia and acute ischemia as precipitants. Old MI explains cardiomyopathy.

imaging.name: CT Chest (without contrast)
imaging.result: Cardiomegaly, bilateral pleural effusions, ground-glass opacities consistent with pulmonary edema. No PE on this non-contrast study.
imaging.reasoning: Can confirm pulmonary edema but CXR is usually sufficient and faster. Non-contrast CT cannot rule out PE.

imaging.name: CT Angiography Chest (PE protocol)
imaging.result: No pulmonary embolism. Cardiomegaly with reflux of contrast into IVC suggesting elevated right-sided pressures. Bilateral pleural effusions.
imaging.reasoning: Would consider if PE is on differential, but this patient has clear HF precipitants (dietary indiscretion, med non-compliance) and no PE risk factors. CTA adds contrast load to already compromised kidneys.

imaging.name: CT Abdomen/Pelvis
imaging.result: Hepatomegaly with periportal edema consistent with hepatic congestion. Bilateral pleural effusions. No acute abdominal pathology.
imaging.reasoning: Not indicated for typical HF presentation. Abdominal findings of congestion can be assessed clinically and with bedside US.

imaging.name: Lower Extremity Doppler Ultrasound
imaging.result: No deep vein thrombosis. Bilateral subcutaneous edema.
imaging.reasoning: Would consider if unilateral edema or concern for DVT/PE, but bilateral edema in known HF patient is from venous congestion, not DVT.

imaging.name: Bedside Lung Ultrasound (POCUS)
imaging.result: Bilateral B-lines (>3 per field) consistent with pulmonary edema. Small bilateral pleural effusions. No consolidation.
imaging.reasoning: Rapid bedside confirmation of pulmonary edema. B-lines correlate with degree of congestion. Can monitor response to diuresis.

treatments.name: Supplemental oxygen
treatments.result: 4L NC applied, SpO2 improved to 94%. Consider BiPAP if not improving.
treatments.reasoning: Treat hypoxemia. BiPAP helpful for severe pulmonary edema - reduces preload and work of breathing.

treatments.name: IV Furosemide
treatments.result: Furosemide 80mg IV x1 (patient on 40mg PO at home, so gave 2x home dose IV)
treatments.reasoning: IV loop diuretic is cornerstone of acute HF treatment. Give 1-2.5x home oral dose as IV. Goal: net negative fluid balance.

treatments.name: Nitroglycerin (if hypertensive)
treatments.result: Consider nitroglycerin paste or drip if persistent hypertension and pulmonary edema
treatments.reasoning: Venodilator reduces preload. Useful in hypertensive acute pulmonary edema. Avoid if hypotensive.

treatments.name: Hold beta-blocker acutely
treatments.result: Hold carvedilol during acute decompensation. Restart once euvolemic.
treatments.reasoning: Beta-blockers can worsen acute decompensation. Hold during acute phase, restart before discharge.

treatments.name: Fluid restriction
treatments.result: Restrict fluids to 1.5-2L/day
treatments.reasoning: Helps achieve net negative balance along with diuretics

treatments.name: Daily weights and I/O monitoring
treatments.result: Strict I/Os, daily weights. Goal 1-2 kg/day fluid loss.
treatments.reasoning: Best way to track diuresis. Weight more reliable than I/O charting.

treatments.name: Sodium restriction
treatments.result: Low sodium diet (<2g/day)
treatments.reasoning: Reduces fluid retention

consults.name: Cardiology consult
consults.result: Consult cardiology for GDMT optimization. Patient currently on ACEi + BB but missing SGLT2i (new Class 1 for ALL HFrEF) and MRA. Evaluate for ICD (EF ≤35%), CRT if QRS >150ms, and advanced HF therapies if refractory.
consults.reasoning: 2022 guidelines: All 4 GDMT pillars (ARNI/ACEi/ARB, BB, MRA, SGLT2i) should be initiated/optimized before discharge. SGLT2i reduces HF hospitalizations regardless of diabetes status.

disposition.name: Admit to telemetry/cardiac unit
disposition.result: Admit for IV diuresis, monitoring, and GDMT optimization. HF hospitalization TRIPLES 1-year mortality; nearly 1 in 10 patients die within 30 days of admission.
disposition.reasoning: Acute decompensated HF requires inpatient diuresis and monitoring. Use hospitalization as opportunity to optimize all 4 GDMT pillars before discharge.

disposition.name: ICU admission
disposition.result: Consider ICU if requiring BiPAP, inotropes, or hemodynamic instability (cold and wet or cold and dry profiles)
disposition.reasoning: This patient is 'warm and wet' (adequate perfusion + congestion) - floor appropriate. 'Cold and wet' (hypoperfusion + congestion) requires ICU for inotropes.

actualDiagnosis.name: Acute Decompensated Heart Failure (HFrEF) - Stage C, Volume Overload
keyFindings: Progressive dyspnea with orthopnea (4-pillow) and PND
Bilateral pitting edema to knees with weight gain
Known HFrEF with EF 25% (≤40% = reduced EF)
Clear precipitants: diuretic non-compliance + dietary sodium indiscretion (most common per OPTIMIZE-HF)
Elevated JVP (12 cm) with hepatojugular reflux
S3 gallop and displaced PMI
Bilateral pulmonary crackles (pulmonary edema)
BNP markedly elevated at 1,840 pg/mL (>400 = HF likely; age 68 threshold is ~900 for NT-proBNP)
CXR with cardiomegaly, pulmonary congestion, Kerley B lines, pleural effusions
ECG shows sinus tachycardia, no acute ischemia (ruled out ACS as precipitant)
Stage C HF: Structural heart disease WITH current symptoms (vs Stage B = asymptomatic structural disease)
teachingPoints: HF classification (2022 ACC/AHA): HFrEF (EF ≤40%), HFmrEF (EF 41-49%), HFpEF (EF ≥50%), HFimpEF (previously HFrEF, now >40%)
HF STAGES: Stage A (at risk, no disease), Stage B (pre-HF: structural disease OR elevated biomarkers, NO symptoms), Stage C (symptomatic HF), Stage D (advanced/refractory). This patient is Stage C.
NT-proBNP AGE-ADJUSTED THRESHOLDS (ED rule-in): <50 years: ≥450 pg/mL, 50-75 years: ≥900 pg/mL, >75 years: ≥1800 pg/mL. Rule-out: <300 pg/mL in ED.
BNP thresholds: <100 pg/mL rules out HF; >400 pg/mL rules in. NT-proBNP >5000 = poor prognosis requiring admission.

OPTIMIZE-HF PRECIPITANTS: Pneumonia/respiratory (15.3%), Ischemia/ACS (14.7%), Arrhythmia (13.5%), Uncontrolled HTN (10.7%), Med non-adherence (8.9%), Worsening renal function (6.8%), Dietary non-adherence (5.2%)

MORTALITY BY PRECIPITANT: Pneumonia has HIGHEST in-hospital mortality (OR 1.60). Worsening renal function = highest 90-day mortality (OR 1.46). Hypertensive crisis actually has BETTER prognosis (OR 0.74).
PROGNOSIS: NYHA Class IV = 30-40% one-year mortality. Hospitalization TRIPLES 1-year mortality. Nearly 1 in 10 die within 30 days of admission.
'Warm and wet' = adequate perfusion + congestion = most common presentation. Treat with diuretics.
'Cold and wet' = poor perfusion + congestion = may need inotropes.
IV loop diuretic dose: give 1-2.5x home oral dose as IV bolus or start 40-80mg if diuretic-naive. Target 1-2 kg/day weight loss.
GDMT FOUR PILLARS (2022): ACEi/ARB/ARNI + Beta-blocker + MRA + SGLT2i. NEW: SGLT2i (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin) is now Class 1 for ALL HFrEF regardless of diabetes!
Hold beta-blocker during acute decompensation; restart before discharge once euvolemic. Optimize all 4 pillars before discharge.

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Sacubitril/valsartan reduced CV death/HF hospitalization by 20% vs enalapril
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: ARNI preferred over ACEi/ARB in HFrEF. Switch from ACEi requires 36-hour washout period
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: CV death/HF hosp: 21.8% vs 26.5% (HR 0.80). CV death alone: 13.3% vs 16.5%. NNT 21

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Dapagliflozin reduced worsening HF/CV death by 26% in HFrEF regardless of diabetes status
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: SGLT2i now Class 1 for ALL HFrEF patients, not just diabetics. Add on top of GDMT
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: Worsening HF/CV death: 16.3% vs 21.2% (HR 0.74). NNT 21 over 18 months

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Empagliflozin reduced CV death/HF hospitalization by 25% in HFrEF
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Both dapagliflozin and empagliflozin have Class 1 indication for HFrEF
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: CV death/HF hosp: 19.4% vs 24.7% (HR 0.75). Renal benefit also demonstrated

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Empagliflozin reduced HF hospitalizations in HFpEF (EF >40%)
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: First drug class to show benefit in HFpEF. Consider SGLT2i for all HF phenotypes
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: CV death/HF hosp: 13.8% vs 17.1% (HR 0.79). Driven mainly by HF hospitalization reduction

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Beta-blockers reduce mortality even in severe (NYHA III-IV) HFrEF
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Do NOT withhold beta-blockers in severe HF. Start low, go slow, once euvolemic
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: All-cause mortality: 11.2% vs 16.8% (RR 0.65). Trial stopped early for overwhelming benefit

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: MRA (eplerenone) reduced death/HF hospitalization by 37% in mild HFrEF
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: MRA is pillar 3 of GDMT. Monitor K+ closely (avoid if K+ >5.0 or CrCl <30)
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: CV death/HF hosp: 18.3% vs 25.9% (HR 0.63). Trial stopped early for efficacy

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: High-dose IV furosemide (2.5x oral dose) provided greater decongestion without worsening renal function
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Start with 1-2.5x home oral dose as IV. Higher dose = more net fluid loss without harm
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: High dose: greater fluid loss (4.9L vs 3.6L), more dyspnea relief. Transient Cr rise similar and benign

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Non-invasive ventilation reduces dyspnea and need for intubation in acute HF
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Use BiPAP early for severe pulmonary edema. Reduces work of breathing and preload
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: Intubation rate: 2.9% (NIV) vs 5.1% (standard). No mortality difference but faster symptom relief

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Rapid uptitration of GDMT before discharge reduced death/HF readmission
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Don't wait - initiate and uptitrate all four GDMT pillars before discharge when possible
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: 180-day death/HF readmission: 15.2% (rapid) vs 23.3% (usual care). NNT 12

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: IV iron (FCM) reduced HF hospitalizations in iron-deficient HFrEF patients
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Check ferritin and TSAT in all HF patients. Treat if ferritin <100 or 100-300 with TSAT <20%
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: HF hospitalizations: RR 0.74 (26% reduction). Improved symptoms and exercise capacity

nyha.name: NYHA Functional Classification
nyha.description: Classifies heart failure severity based on symptoms and physical activity limitations

classes.description: No limitation. Ordinary physical activity does not cause symptoms.
Slight limitation. Comfortable at rest. Ordinary activity causes symptoms.
Marked limitation. Comfortable at rest. Less than ordinary activity causes symptoms.
Unable to carry on any physical activity without discomfort. Symptoms at rest.

thisPatient.interpretation: Marked to severe limitation - symptoms at rest (orthopnea, PND)

hfProfiles.name: Heart Failure Hemodynamic Profiles (Forrester/Nohria-Stevenson)

patientBackground.recentEvents: Evelyn ran out of her furosemide 5 days ago and did not realize how important it was. Her grandson was visiting and she ate more salty foods than usual - she made his favorite ham and potato soup. She has gained 8 pounds in the past week.

patientBackground.fluidIntake: Has been drinking more fluids than usual because she was thirsty. Does not understand the fluid restriction well.
patientBackground.contextualFactors: Managing multiple medications is overwhelming. Her husband passed away 2 years ago and he used to help manage her health. She is fiercely independent but struggling. Limited income on Social Security.
patientBackground.supportSystem: Lives alone in the house where she raised her children. Daughter Linda lives 30 minutes away and tries to help but works full time. Home health nurse visits weekly. Grandson visits when he can.
patientBackground.healthBeliefs: Evelyn follows doctor recommendations as best she can but finds the diet restrictions very difficult. She wants to enjoy her remaining years and food is one of her pleasures. Does not fully understand why salt and fluid are restricted.
patientBackground.previousERVisits: Multiple hospitalizations for heart failure in past 2 years. Was in the hospital 4 months ago for the same thing. Has had goals of care discussions - she wants to continue treatment but does not want to be on a ventilator.
patientBackground.occupation: Retired schoolteacher. Worked for 35 years in elementary education. Active in her church community when she feels well enough.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient was 'cold and wet'?
whatIfScenarios.description: Same presentation but with cool extremities, hypotension, and altered mental status

chiefComplaint.original: I can't catch my breath and my legs are swelling
chiefComplaint.modified: I can't breathe and I feel very weak and confused

feedback.correct: Cold and wet = cardiogenic shock. Diuretics alone won't work - need inotropes (dobutamine) +/- vasopressors. ICU mandatory. Consider mechanical support.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of shock. How does this change treatment from 'warm and wet'?
feedback.incorrect: 'Cold and wet' = poor perfusion + congestion = cardiogenic shock. Can't just diurese - need inotropes (dobutamine, milrinone) and possibly vasopressors. ICU level care. Consider IABP/Impella.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Warm and wet (most common) = good perfusion, congested = diuretics work well. Cold and wet = cardiogenic shock = need inotropes, vasopressors, ICU, possibly mechanical circulatory support.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient couldn't speak in full sentences?
whatIfScenarios.description: Severe respiratory distress, pink frothy sputum, tripod positioning

chiefComplaint.original: I can't catch my breath and my legs are swelling
chiefComplaint.modified: [Patient gasping, unable to speak full sentences, pink frothy sputum]

expectedReasoning.topDiagnosis: Flash pulmonary edema / Acute respiratory failure

feedback.correct: Flash pulmonary edema = BiPAP immediately (reduces preload and intubation rates). IV nitroglycerin for afterload reduction. High-dose IV diuretics. May still need intubation.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of severity. What's the immediate respiratory intervention?
feedback.incorrect: Severe pulmonary edema with respiratory failure = BiPAP immediately (if patient can protect airway), IV nitro for BP/afterload reduction, high-dose IV diuretics. Prepares for intubation if no improvement.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Mild/moderate CHF = oxygen, IV diuretics, oral meds. Flash pulmonary edema = BiPAP, IV nitro, IV diuretics, possible intubation. This is the unstable end of the spectrum.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if there was a new loud murmur?
whatIfScenarios.description: Acute HF with new loud holosystolic murmur at apex

chiefComplaint.original: I can't catch my breath and my legs are swelling
chiefComplaint.modified: I can't breathe - this started suddenly after my heart attack last week

feedback.correct: New murmur after MI = mechanical complication! Papillary muscle rupture (MR) or VSD. STAT echo, urgent surgical consultation. This is a surgical emergency.
feedback.partial: Good concern about the new murmur. What's the implication in post-MI setting?
feedback.incorrect: New loud murmur + acute HF + recent MI = mechanical complication (papillary muscle rupture, VSD, free wall rupture). This is SURGICAL EMERGENCY. STAT echo, emergent surgery consult. High mortality without intervention.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Chronic known murmur in HF = manage as usual HF. NEW murmur especially post-MI = mechanical complication until proven otherwise. Completely different urgency and management.

questions.stem: Based on the clinical presentation, which is the MOST appropriate next step?

options.text: Systematic history and focused physical examination
options.explanation: A targeted history and physical exam is the essential first step in clinical reasoning, guiding hypothesis generation and differential diagnosis.
Empiric treatment before adequate evaluation risks missing the correct diagnosis and may cause harm.
Diagnostic testing should be hypothesis-driven, not shotgun; targeted tests based on clinical reasoning yield better outcomes.
Consultation may be appropriate later but should follow initial clinical assessment and diagnostic reasoning.

questions.stem: Which cognitive bias poses the greatest risk in this clinical scenario?

options.explanation: Availability bias (overweighting recent experiences) is common but not the most frequent source of error in this scenario.
Anchoring (fixating on initial impression) is a risk but can be mitigated by systematic differential generation.
Premature closure — accepting a diagnosis before it is fully verified — is the single most common cognitive error in clinical reasoning across all settings.
Confirmation bias (seeking only supporting evidence) contributes to error but often follows from premature closure.

questions.stem: An ECG shows ST elevation in leads II, III, and aVF with reciprocal ST depression in I and aVL. Which coronary artery is most likely occluded?

options.explanation: ST elevation in inferior leads (II, III, aVF) with reciprocal changes in lateral leads (I, aVL) localizes to an inferior STEMI, most commonly caused by RCA occlusion (~85% of cases). Check right-sided leads (V4R) to assess for RV involvement.
LAD occlusion causes anterior STEMI with ST elevation in V1-V4 (± V5-V6), not inferior leads.
LCx causes lateral STEMI (I, aVL, V5-V6). While LCx can cause inferior changes in ~15% of cases (dominant LCx), RCA is far more common.
Left main occlusion causes widespread ST depression with ST elevation in aVR — a pattern of diffuse subendocardial ischemia, not isolated inferior STEMI.

missedConsequences.Atrial Fibrillation: Uncontrolled rate leads to tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy and heart failure. Failure to anticoagulate results in stroke.
missedConsequences.Pulmonary Embolism: Missed PE leads to right heart failure or sudden death.
missedConsequences.Thyrotoxicosis: Untreated thyroid storm causes multi-organ failure.
missedConsequences.Acute Coronary Syndrome: Underlying MI triggers AF; missed diagnosis leads to cardiogenic shock.

commonPitfalls: Cardioverting new-onset AF without anticoagulation when duration >48 hours - stroke risk!
Not looking for underlying triggers: infection, PE, MI, thyroid disease, alcohol
Forgetting to calculate CHA2DS2-VASc score for stroke risk
Over-aggressive rate control causing hypotension
Not checking TSH in new-onset AF

mcqs.question: The patient's AF started 2 days ago. What is the MOST important consideration regarding cardioversion?

options.explanation: DANGEROUS - AF >48 hours requires anticoagulation 3-4 weeks OR TEE before cardioversion to prevent stroke.

options.text: Cardioversion is contraindicated; rate control only
options.explanation: Cardioversion is possible but requires appropriate anticoagulation strategy.

options.text: Rate control first; cardioversion requires anticoagulation strategy given duration
options.explanation: AF >48 hours requires 3-4 weeks anticoagulation OR TEE showing no LAA thrombus before cardioversion.

options.text: Chemical cardioversion is safe regardless of duration
options.explanation: Any cardioversion carries stroke risk if AF duration >48 hours without proper anticoagulation.

mcqs.question: The patient's CHA2DS2-VASc score is 4 (age >75, female, HTN, diabetes). What does this indicate?

options.text: Low stroke risk - aspirin alone is sufficient
options.explanation: Score ≥2 indicates high stroke risk requiring anticoagulation.

options.text: High stroke risk - oral anticoagulation is indicated
options.explanation: CHA2DS2-VASc ≥2 (men) or ≥3 (women) indicates high stroke risk. Anticoagulation reduces risk by ~70%.

options.text: Moderate risk - either aspirin or anticoagulation acceptable
options.explanation: With score of 4, anticoagulation is clearly indicated.

options.text: Score is irrelevant since she's already in AF
options.explanation: CHA2DS2-VASc guides anticoagulation decisions in patients WITH AF.

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Dabigatran 150mg BID superior to warfarin for stroke prevention with similar major bleeding
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Dabigatran 150mg BID for stroke reduction; 110mg BID for lower bleeding risk (less stroke reduction)
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: Stroke/embolism: 1.11% vs 1.69% (RR 0.66). Major bleeding similar. 110mg BID: less bleeding but stroke similar to warfarin

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Apixaban superior to warfarin for stroke prevention AND major bleeding reduction
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Apixaban preferred DOAC - only one to reduce both stroke and bleeding vs warfarin
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: Stroke/embolism: 1.27% vs 1.60% (RR 0.79). Major bleeding: 2.13% vs 3.09% (HR 0.69). Mortality benefit too

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Rivaroxaban non-inferior to warfarin for stroke prevention in higher-risk AF
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Rivaroxaban 20mg daily with dinner (15mg if CrCl 15-50). Take with food for absorption
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: Stroke/embolism: 1.7% vs 2.2% (per-protocol: HR 0.79). Similar bleeding. Once-daily dosing advantage

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Apixaban dramatically superior to aspirin for stroke prevention in patients unsuitable for warfarin
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: If patient 'can't take warfarin,' DOAC is still option. Aspirin is NOT adequate stroke prevention
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: Stroke/embolism: 1.6% vs 3.7% (HR 0.45). Similar bleeding. Trial stopped early for superiority

evidenceBasedPearls.topic: RACE II - Lenient vs Strict Rate Control
evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Lenient rate control (HR <110) is non-inferior to strict control (HR <80)
evidenceBasedPearls.source: Van Gelder IC et al., NEJM 2010
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Target HR <110 at rest is acceptable - don't over-treat asymptomatic patients
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: Composite endpoint: 12.9% (lenient) vs 14.9% (strict). Easier to achieve, less medications needed

evidenceBasedPearls.topic: AFFIRM Trial - Rate vs Rhythm Control
evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Rhythm control not superior to rate control for mortality; both require anticoagulation
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Rate control is acceptable initial strategy. Anticoagulation required regardless of rhythm strategy
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: 5-year mortality: 23.8% (rhythm) vs 21.3% (rate) - trend favoring rate control. No CV mortality difference

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Early rhythm control (within 1 year of diagnosis) reduced CV outcomes vs usual care
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Consider early rhythm control in newly diagnosed AF, especially if symptomatic
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: CV death/stroke/HF hosp: 3.9% vs 5.0% (HR 0.79). Benefits seen early, sustained over time

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: AF ablation reduced death/HF hospitalization in HFrEF patients
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Consider AF ablation for HFrEF patients - may improve EF and outcomes
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: Death/HF hosp: 28.5% (ablation) vs 44.6% (medical). All-cause mortality: 13.4% vs 25%

evidenceBasedPearls.topic: X-VeRT and EMANATE - DOACs for Cardioversion
evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: DOACs are safe and effective for cardioversion without TEE guidance
evidenceBasedPearls.source: Cappato R et al. (X-VeRT), Eur Heart J 2014; Ezekowitz MD et al. (EMANATE), Circulation 2018
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: DOAC x3 weeks pre-cardioversion is alternative to TEE approach for AF >48h
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: Stroke rates <1% with either approach. DOACs allow earlier cardioversion than warfarin

evidenceBasedPearls.topic: LAAOS III - Left Atrial Appendage Closure
evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Surgical LAA occlusion during cardiac surgery reduced stroke in AF patients
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: If patient with AF undergoing cardiac surgery, request LAA occlusion
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: Stroke/embolism: 4.8% (LAA closure) vs 7.0% (no closure). HR 0.67. Continue anticoagulation
