chiefComplaint.original: My heart has been racing for 2 days and I feel weak
chiefComplaint.modified: My heart is racing and I feel like I'm going to pass out [patient is diaphoretic and confused]

feedback.correct: Unstable tachycardia (hypotension, altered mental status, chest pain) = immediate synchronized cardioversion. Don't wait for anticoagulation.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of instability. What's the immediate treatment?
feedback.incorrect: Unstable AF = SYNCHRONIZED CARDIOVERSION immediately. Signs of instability: hypotension, altered mental status, chest pain, acute heart failure. No time for rate control - electricity first.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Stable AF = rate control first, assess anticoagulation, evaluate causes. Unstable AF = immediate synchronized cardioversion regardless of anticoagulation status - life over clot.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient had facial droop?
whatIfScenarios.description: AF with RVR and new right-sided weakness and slurred speech

chiefComplaint.original: My heart has been racing for 2 days and I feel weak
chiefComplaint.modified: My heart is racing and my face feels funny and I can't move my right arm

feedback.correct: This is the feared complication of AF - cardioembolic stroke. Activate stroke protocol immediately. tPA if eligible, thrombectomy evaluation. AF management is secondary to acute stroke treatment.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of stroke. What's the connection to the AF?
feedback.incorrect: AF causes left atrial thrombus which embolizes to brain = cardioembolic stroke. This is why we anticoagulate! Now manage as acute stroke - tPA/thrombectomy evaluation. This is the emergency within the emergency.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: AF alone = anticoagulate to prevent stroke. AF + stroke = acute stroke protocol first (tPA, thrombectomy), manage AF secondarily. Long-term anticoagulation mandatory after cardioembolic stroke.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the QRS was wide and irregular?
whatIfScenarios.description: Irregular wide-complex tachycardia at 200 bpm

chiefComplaint.original: My heart has been racing for 2 days and I feel weak
chiefComplaint.modified: My heart is racing extremely fast and I feel faint

feedback.correct: DANGER! Wide-complex irregular tachycardia = AF with WPW until proven otherwise. Do NOT give AV nodal blockers (diltiazem, metoprolol, digoxin) - can cause VFib. Procainamide or cardioversion.
feedback.partial: Good concern for WPW. What's the critical medication to avoid?
feedback.incorrect: Wide irregular tachycardia = AF with WPW (accessory pathway). AV nodal blockers (CCB, beta-blockers, digoxin, adenosine) are CONTRAINDICATED - they block AV node but enhance accessory pathway conduction, causing VFib. Use procainamide or cardiovert.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Regular AF = standard rate control (diltiazem, metoprolol). Pre-excited AF (WPW) = NEVER use AV nodal blockers. This is a life-threatening distinction.

questions.stem: Based on the clinical presentation, which is the MOST appropriate next step?

options.text: Systematic history and focused physical examination
options.explanation: A targeted history and physical exam is the essential first step in clinical reasoning, guiding hypothesis generation and differential diagnosis.
Empiric treatment before adequate evaluation risks missing the correct diagnosis and may cause harm.
Diagnostic testing should be hypothesis-driven, not shotgun; targeted tests based on clinical reasoning yield better outcomes.
Consultation may be appropriate later but should follow initial clinical assessment and diagnostic reasoning.

questions.stem: Which cognitive bias poses the greatest risk in this clinical scenario?

options.explanation: Availability bias (overweighting recent experiences) is common but not the most frequent source of error in this scenario.
Anchoring (fixating on initial impression) is a risk but can be mitigated by systematic differential generation.
Premature closure — accepting a diagnosis before it is fully verified — is the single most common cognitive error in clinical reasoning across all settings.
Confirmation bias (seeking only supporting evidence) contributes to error but often follows from premature closure.

guidelineReferences.name: AHA/ACC Chest Pain Guidelines 2021
guidelineReferences.citation: Gulati M, et al. Circulation. 2021;144:e364-e454
keyPoints: High-sensitivity troponin preferred for acute chest pain evaluation
Risk stratification using validated tools (HEART, TIMI, GRACE)
ECG within 10 minutes of arrival for suspected ACS
Serial troponins at 0 and 3 hours (or 0 and 1 hour for hs-cTn)
Type 1 MI: plaque rupture requiring antiplatelet + revascularization
Type 2 MI: supply/demand mismatch - treat underlying cause

diabetic-58.name: J. Quincy

missedConsequences.Acute Myocardial Infarction: Patient sent home with 'acid reflux,' returns 8 hours later in cardiogenic shock. Emergent cath shows 99% LAD occlusion. Despite PCI, significant myocardial damage leads to EF of 25% and heart failure.
missedConsequences.Acute Coronary Syndrome / Unstable Angin: Without antiplatelet therapy and risk stratification, patient progresses to STEMI at home. Delays to reperfusion result in larger infarct size.
missedConsequences.Pulmonary Embolism: Missed PE leads to hemodynamic compromise. Patient develops right heart strain and requires ICU admission for thrombolysis.

commonPitfalls: Assuming 'burning' chest pain is always GI - cardiac ischemia can present as burning, especially in diabetics
Being reassured by negative initial troponin - serial troponins are essential; early troponin can be negative
Attributing symptoms to GERD because patient had some relief with antacids - nitroglycerin relief is not specific for cardiac pain, and neither is antacid relief
Missing the 'crescendo pattern' - symptoms getting worse or occurring with less exertion defines unstable angina
Forgetting that diabetics often have ATYPICAL presentations - 'silent ischemia' is common

mcqs.question: The patient's symptoms have been occurring with progressively less exertion over 2 weeks. What does this pattern indicate?

options.explanation: Stable angina has a fixed, reproducible threshold. Worsening threshold indicates instability.
A crescendo pattern (symptoms with less exertion) indicates plaque instability and high risk for MI. This requires urgent evaluation.
While stress can worsen GERD, the exertional pattern strongly suggests cardiac origin.
Never attribute chest pain to anxiety in a high-risk patient without ruling out cardiac causes.

mcqs.question: The initial troponin returns at 0.08 ng/mL (normal <0.04). The patient feels better after nitroglycerin. What is your next step?

options.text: Discharge with GI follow-up since symptoms improved
options.explanation: Elevated troponin indicates myocardial injury. Symptomatic relief doesn't rule out ACS.

options.text: Admit, repeat troponin in 3-6 hours, continue ACS protocol
options.explanation: Troponin is elevated. Serial troponins will show if it's rising (acute MI) or stable. Continue antiplatelet/anticoagulation therapy.

options.text: Order CT chest to rule out PE
options.explanation: While PE should be considered, the elevated troponin and exertional pattern point to ACS as the primary concern.

options.text: Reassure that mild troponin elevation is normal in diabetics
options.explanation: Any troponin elevation above normal is abnormal and requires evaluation.

mcqs.question: This patient's rising troponin with plaque rupture represents which type of myocardial infarction?

options.text: Type 1 MI - atherosclerotic plaque rupture with thrombosis
options.explanation: Type 1 MI = plaque rupture/erosion causing coronary thrombosis. This is the classic 'heart attack' requiring antiplatelet therapy and revascularization. Type 2 MI is supply/demand mismatch (e.g., severe anemia, hypotension, tachyarrhythmia) - treatment targets the underlying cause.
Type 2 MI occurs when oxygen demand exceeds supply (tachycardia, hypotension, anemia, hypoxia) WITHOUT acute plaque rupture. This patient has no such trigger - this is plaque-mediated Type 1 MI.
Type 3 MI is sudden cardiac death with symptoms of ischemia but death before biomarkers obtained. This patient is alive and has biomarkers.
Type 4 MI occurs during or after PCI. This patient hasn't had a procedure yet.

mcqs.question: Using the HEART score for this diabetic patient with highly suspicious history, ischemic ECG changes, age 58, multiple risk factors, and elevated troponin - what is the score and disposition?

options.text: HEART score 3-4, consider discharge with follow-up
options.explanation: This patient scores much higher. History (2 - highly suspicious), ECG (2 - significant ST depression), Age (1 - 45-65), Risk factors (2 - ≥3 factors), Troponin (2 - >2x normal) = 9.

options.text: HEART score 7-10, HIGH risk - admit and treat for ACS
options.explanation: HEART Score = 9 (HIGH RISK). History: 2 (highly suspicious). ECG: 2 (significant ST changes). Age: 1 (45-65). Risk factors: 2 (≥3 factors: DM, HTN, smoking, FHx, dyslipidemia). Troponin: 2 (>2x normal). Score ≥7 = high risk, requires admission and ACS treatment.

options.text: HEART score 0-3, LOW risk - safe for early discharge
options.explanation: Low risk (0-3) requires low-risk history, normal ECG, age <45 or minimal risk factors, and normal troponin. This patient has none of those.

options.text: HEART score doesn't apply to diabetic patients
options.explanation: HEART score applies to all chest pain patients. Diabetics may have 'atypical' presentations but still score high on other components.

allDiagnoses.name: Acute Coronary Syndrome / Unstable Angina
allDiagnoses.rationale: Diabetic patient with exertional chest pressure, radiation to shoulder/jaw, increasing frequency. Classic for ACS despite atypical 'burning' quality.

allDiagnoses.name: Stable Angina
allDiagnoses.rationale: Exertional chest discomfort relieved by rest fits stable angina, but worsening pattern suggests progression to unstable.

allDiagnoses.name: GERD
allDiagnoses.rationale: Burning quality, relation to meals, partial antacid relief. However, must rule out cardiac cause first in this risk profile.

allDiagnoses.name: Acute Myocardial Infarction
allDiagnoses.rationale: CRITICAL: 58yo diabetic with HTN, worsening exertional symptoms. Diabetics often present atypically - must have low threshold for ACS workup.

allDiagnoses.name: Aortic Dissection
allDiagnoses.rationale: HTN is a risk factor. Look for tearing pain to back, BP asymmetry, pulse deficits. Less likely given gradual onset but must consider.

allDiagnoses.name: Pulmonary Embolism
allDiagnoses.rationale: Recent long flight is a risk factor. Look for pleuritic pain, dyspnea, tachycardia, hypoxia.

allDiagnoses.name: Pericarditis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Though less likely presentation (would expect sharp, pleuritic pain improved by leaning forward), pericarditis can cause MI-like troponin elevation and must be considered in chest pain workup.

allDiagnoses.name: Pneumonia
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can cause chest discomfort, but typically with cough, fever, and pleuritic quality. No respiratory symptoms here.

allDiagnoses.name: Musculoskeletal Chest Pain
allDiagnoses.rationale: Not reproducible with palpation, no trauma history. Less likely but could coexist.

allDiagnoses.name: Esophageal Spasm
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can mimic cardiac pain but usually associated with dysphagia.

allDiagnoses.name: Panic/Anxiety Disorder
allDiagnoses.rationale: Diagnosis of exclusion. Never assume anxiety without ruling out organic causes, especially in high-risk patients.

allDiagnoses.name: Pneumothorax
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would expect sudden onset, pleuritic pain, dyspnea. No trauma or risk factors.

diabetic-58.oldcartsNarrative: "It's not exactly pain, more like a pressure or tightness that comes and goes. Started about two weeks ago. It's right in the center of my chest, kind of burning or squeezing. At first I thought it was just indigestion because it sometimes happened after big meals. But lately it's happening when I walk up stairs or carry groceries - even without eating. It lasts about 5-10 minutes and goes away when I rest. Sometimes it spreads to my left shoulder and jaw. I get a little short of breath and tired when it happens. I've had some acid reflux before but this feels different - stronger. My dad had a heart attack in his 60s. I have diabetes - been on metformin for 10 years - and high blood pressure. I take lisinopril but sometimes forget. Work has been stressful lately."

historyQuestions.text: When did this start?
historyQuestions.answer: It started about two weeks ago. At first it was occasional, but this past week it's been happening almost every day and with less and less activity.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Timeline establishes crescendo pattern - worsening over 2 weeks indicates unstable angina

historyQuestions.text: Does the discomfort come on with physical activity or exertion?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, lately it happens even with light activity like walking across the parking lot. It used to only happen after big meals.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Exertional chest discomfort in a diabetic with risk factors is highly concerning for ischemia. Worsening exertional threshold suggests unstable angina.

historyQuestions.text: Does rest relieve the discomfort?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, it usually goes away within a few minutes if I stop and sit down.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Relief with rest is classic for angina. Suggests demand ischemia.

historyQuestions.text: Does the pain go anywhere else - to your arm, jaw, neck, or back?
historyQuestions.answer: Sometimes it spreads to my left shoulder and up toward my jaw. Not to my back.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Radiation to left arm/jaw is classic for cardiac ischemia. Absence of back radiation argues against dissection.

historyQuestions.text: Has the frequency or intensity changed over time?
historyQuestions.answer: Definitely getting worse. Started maybe once or twice a week, now it's almost every day, and with less and less activity.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CRITICAL: Crescendo pattern (increasing frequency with decreasing exertion threshold) defines unstable angina. This is a high-risk feature.

historyQuestions.text: Do you get sweaty or clammy when this happens?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I don't really get sweaty with it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Diaphoresis often accompanies MI. Absence doesn't rule it out, especially in diabetics who may have atypical presentations.

historyQuestions.text: How well controlled is your diabetes?
historyQuestions.answer: My last A1c was around 8. I try to watch what I eat but it's hard with my schedule.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Suboptimal diabetes control (A1c 8) increases cardiovascular risk. Diabetics may have silent ischemia or atypical presentations.

historyQuestions.text: Have you ever taken nitroglycerin for chest pain?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I've never been prescribed that. I don't even know what that is really.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No prior angina diagnosis or treatment. This appears to be new-onset symptoms.

historyQuestions.text: Any recent long trips or periods of immobility?
historyQuestions.answer: Actually, I flew home from visiting my daughter about 3 weeks ago - about a 5-hour flight.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Recent long flight is a VTE risk factor. Must consider PE in differential, though presentation is more consistent with ACS.

historyQuestions.text: Any leg pain, swelling, or warmth?
historyQuestions.answer: No, my legs feel fine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No DVT symptoms, but PE can occur without obvious DVT. Doesn't rule out PE but lowers suspicion slightly.

historyQuestions.text: Is the pain sharp or worse with deep breaths?
historyQuestions.answer: No, it's not sharp and breathing doesn't really affect it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Non-pleuritic pain argues against PE, pericarditis, and pneumothorax.

historyQuestions.text: Can you reproduce the pain by pressing on your chest?
historyQuestions.answer: No, pressing on it doesn't make it worse.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Non-reproducible pain argues against musculoskeletal cause.

historyQuestions.text: Any family history of heart disease?
historyQuestions.answer: My dad had a heart attack when he was in his sixties.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Positive family history of premature CAD significantly increases risk.

historyQuestions.text: Do you smoke or have you ever smoked?
historyQuestions.answer: I quit about 5 years ago, but I smoked for probably 20 years before that.
historyQuestions.reasoning: 20 pack-year history is a significant cardiovascular risk factor.

historyQuestions.text: What medications are you currently taking?
historyQuestions.answer: Metformin for diabetes, lisinopril for blood pressure - though I sometimes forget to take it. And ibuprofen sometimes for aches.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Not on aspirin or statin. Suboptimal BP control (sometimes forgets lisinopril) adds to risk.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any allergies to medications?
historyQuestions.answer: No allergies that I know of. I've taken antibiotics and different pain meds without any problems.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical before administering aspirin, heparin, contrast for catheterization

historyQuestions.text: Besides diabetes and high blood pressure, any other medical conditions?
historyQuestions.answer: High cholesterol - my doctor mentioned it but I never started the medicine he wanted me to take. Nothing else major.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Untreated hyperlipidemia adds another major cardiovascular risk factor

historyQuestions.text: Does the discomfort get worse after eating or after meals?
historyQuestions.answer: Early on, yes - I thought it was indigestion because it sometimes happened after big meals. But now it happens even without eating, especially when I'm active. The meal connection seems less important now that it's happening with exertion.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Initial postprandial pattern can be seen with GERD or 'abdominal angina'. However, progression to exertional symptoms suggests cardiac etiology. Postprandial angina can occur due to increased cardiac demand after eating.

historyQuestions.text: Have you been taking your medications regularly, or have you missed doses or run out?
historyQuestions.answer: I'm pretty good with the metformin but honestly I forget the lisinopril maybe once or twice a week. I haven't run out but I'm not as consistent as I should be.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Suboptimal blood pressure control from medication non-adherence increases cardiovascular risk and may contribute to symptom progression.

historyQuestions.text: Have you tried any medications to help with the chest discomfort?
historyQuestions.answer: I tried some Tums thinking it was heartburn - sometimes it helps a little, sometimes not. I've also taken ibuprofen for general aches but that doesn't really touch this.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Partial antacid relief doesn't rule out cardiac cause - nitroglycerin can also provide relief from esophageal spasm. Response to NSAIDs would suggest musculoskeletal cause.

historyQuestions.text: Have you ever had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: Had my gallbladder out about 10 years ago. Laparoscopic - went home the same day. No heart procedures ever.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prior cardiac interventions would change management; general surgical history for risk assessment

historyQuestions.text: Have you ever had any heart tests - stress tests, echos, anything like that?
historyQuestions.answer: No, never. My doctor mentioned doing a stress test a couple years ago but I never got around to it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No baseline cardiac evaluation despite multiple risk factors - missed opportunity for earlier detection

historyQuestions.text: When did you last see your regular doctor?
historyQuestions.answer: About 6 months ago. I was supposed to go back for labs but got busy with work.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Suboptimal follow-up care; may have missed worsening metabolic parameters

historyQuestions.text: How much alcohol do you drink?
historyQuestions.answer: A glass of wine with dinner most nights. Occasionally a cocktail on weekends. Nothing excessive.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Moderate alcohol intake - generally neutral cardiovascular effect

historyQuestions.text: Who do you live with?
historyQuestions.answer: I live with my spouse. Kids are grown and moved out. It's just the two of us.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Good social support at home important for post-discharge care and cardiac rehab adherence

historyQuestions.text: Before these symptoms started, what were you able to do physically?
historyQuestions.answer: I could walk around fine, climb stairs at work without any issues. No problems until these past couple weeks.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Establishes baseline functional capacity - clear decline indicates new cardiac pathology

historyQuestions.text: What are you most worried about?
historyQuestions.answer: Honestly, I'm scared this is my heart. My dad's heart attack really affected our family. I don't want to go through that.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Understanding fears helps with communication; family history of MI creates appropriate concern

occupation_current_job_duties.answer: Senior accountant. Desk job, lots of computer work. Tax season is brutal - 60-70 hour weeks.
occupation_current_job_duties.relevance: Sedentary occupation contributes to metabolic syndrome and cardiovascular risk; high work stress is a CV risk factor.

occupation_current_work_stress.answer: Very stressful. Deadlines, client demands. I don't sleep well during busy season.
occupation_current_work_stress.relevance: Chronic work stress and poor sleep are independent cardiovascular risk factors and may trigger acute coronary events.

occupation_current_work_schedule.answer: Usually 8-6 but during tax season I'm there until midnight some nights.
occupation_current_work_schedule.relevance: Long work hours associated with increased cardiovascular risk; limits time for exercise and healthy meal preparation.

diet_eating_patterns.answer: Lots of take-out. Eat at my desk. During busy season it's pizza and Chinese food delivery.
diet_eating_patterns.relevance: Poor diet high in sodium and saturated fat contributes to HTN, diabetes control, and cardiovascular risk.

exercise_activity_level.answer: I used to play tennis. Haven't done anything in years. Too busy, too tired.
exercise_activity_level.relevance: Physical inactivity is major modifiable cardiovascular risk factor; contributes to obesity and metabolic dysfunction.

social_alcohol_detailed.answer: Glass of wine with dinner most nights. Helps me unwind after work.
social_alcohol_detailed.relevance: Moderate alcohol intake; not a major contributing factor in this case.

housing_air_quality.answer: Office building. Climate controlled. Not sure about the air quality honestly.
housing_air_quality.relevance: Indoor air quality generally not a major factor in ACS though air pollution is a recognized CV risk factor.

social_support_system.answer: Wife and kids, but I don't see them much during busy season. It's hard on everyone.
social_support_system.relevance: Work-life imbalance and social isolation contribute to stress and poor health behaviors.

physicalExam.finding: BP 136/86, HR 94 regular, RR 18, Temp 98.4°F, SpO2 97% RA. BP in right arm: 136/86, left arm: 134/84 - no significant difference.
physicalExam.reasoning: Mildly elevated BP and HR. No BP asymmetry argues against dissection.

physicalExam.finding: Alert, mildly anxious, no acute distress. Not diaphoretic. Slightly overweight.
physicalExam.reasoning: No signs of cardiogenic shock or severe distress. Overweight adds to CV risk profile.

physicalExam.text: Auscultate heart sounds at all areas
physicalExam.finding: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1, S2. No S3 or S4 gallop. No murmurs. No pericardial friction rub.
physicalExam.reasoning: No new murmur (would raise concern for papillary muscle dysfunction or dissection). No friction rub (argues against pericarditis).

physicalExam.finding: PMI at 5th intercostal space, midclavicular line. No heaves or thrills. No displaced PMI.
physicalExam.reasoning: Non-displaced PMI suggests no chronic cardiomegaly.

physicalExam.finding: Carotid pulses 2+ bilaterally, symmetric. No bruits. Normal upstroke.
physicalExam.reasoning: Symmetric pulses argue against dissection. No bruits.

physicalExam.finding: Radial, brachial, femoral, popliteal, DP, PT pulses all 2+ and symmetric bilaterally. No radial-femoral delay.
physicalExam.reasoning: Symmetric pulses throughout strongly argue against aortic dissection.

physicalExam.finding: JVP not elevated (approximately 7 cm H2O). No hepatojugular reflux.
physicalExam.reasoning: Normal JVP argues against right heart strain from large PE.

physicalExam.finding: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, crackles, or rhonchi. Good air movement throughout.
physicalExam.reasoning: Clear lungs are common in PE (can't rule it out). Symmetric breath sounds argue against pneumothorax.

physicalExam.finding: No tenderness to palpation over sternum, costochondral junctions, or chest wall. No reproducible pain.
physicalExam.reasoning: Non-reproducible pain argues against musculoskeletal cause.

physicalExam.finding: No calf tenderness, no asymmetric swelling, no palpable cords. Negative Homan's sign bilaterally. Trace bilateral ankle edema.
physicalExam.reasoning: No signs of DVT, though PE can occur without DVT. Trace edema non-specific.

physicalExam.finding: No abdominal bruits. No pulsatile mass.
physicalExam.reasoning: No abdominal aortic abnormality.

physicalExam.finding: No pallor, no cyanosis. No diaphoresis.
physicalExam.reasoning: No signs of cardiogenic shock.

labs.name: Troponin (serial)
labs.result: Initial hs-cTn: 0.08 ng/mL (elevated, normal <0.04). Repeat at 3 hours: 0.15 ng/mL (RISING pattern confirms acute injury). This is TYPE 1 MI - plaque rupture.
labs.reasoning: Rising troponin with ischemic symptoms = TYPE 1 MI (plaque rupture with thrombosis). ESC 0/1h algorithm: significant delta at 1h or high baseline = rule IN for MI. Type 2 MI would require a supply/demand trigger (tachycardia, hypotension, anemia) which this patient lacks.

labs.name: ECG/EKG
labs.result: Sinus rhythm at 94 bpm. ST depression 1mm in leads V4-V6 and II, III, aVF. No ST elevation. T-wave inversions in lateral leads. No LBBB.
labs.reasoning: ST depression ≥0.5mm in 2+ contiguous leads = NSTE-ACS. Lateral/inferior distribution suggests circumflex or RCA territory. If LBBB were present, would use Modified Sgarbossa criteria. STEMI would show ST elevation ≥1mm (≥2mm in V2-V3 for men).

labs.name: CBC
labs.reasoning: No anemia that could exacerbate ischemia. Normal WBC.

labs.name: BMP
labs.result: Na 139, K 4.2, Cl 102, CO2 24, BUN 18, Cr 1.1, Glucose 165.
labs.reasoning: Mild hyperglycemia consistent with diabetes. Normal renal function.

labs.name: BNP or NT-proBNP
labs.reasoning: Mildly elevated BNP suggests some cardiac strain. Can be elevated in ACS.

labs.name: Lipid Panel
labs.reasoning: Dyslipidemia present - adds to CV risk but doesn't change acute management.

labs.name: D-dimer
labs.reasoning: Mildly elevated but can be elevated in many conditions including ACS. Low-moderate suspicion for PE given presentation.

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.result: Heart size upper limits of normal. Lungs clear. No pulmonary edema. No pneumothorax.
imaging.reasoning: Rules out pneumothorax, pulmonary edema. Baseline for comparison.

imaging.name: Echocardiogram
imaging.result: To be performed - would assess wall motion abnormalities and EF.
imaging.reasoning: Can identify regional wall motion abnormalities suggesting ischemia. Important for risk stratification.

imaging.name: CT Angiography of Chest
imaging.result: Not immediately indicated given clinical picture pointing to ACS.
imaging.reasoning: PE is lower on differential given classic ACS presentation. Would delay definitive ACS treatment.

procedures.name: Establish IV access
procedures.reasoning: Essential for medication administration and potential emergency interventions.

treatments.name: Aspirin 162-325mg (chewed)
treatments.result: Aspirin 325mg chewed given immediately.
treatments.reasoning: CRITICAL: Aspirin reduces mortality in ACS. Chewing speeds absorption. Give 162-325mg immediately unless true allergy (not intolerance).

treatments.name: P2Y12 inhibitor loading dose
treatments.result: Clopidogrel 600mg loading dose given. Alternatives: Ticagrelor 180mg (preferred if no contraindication) or Prasugrel 60mg (if going to PCI, not prior stroke).
treatments.reasoning: Dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT) is standard for confirmed ACS. Ticagrelor preferred over clopidogrel per guidelines (faster onset, more potent). Prasugrel contraindicated with prior stroke/TIA.

treatments.name: Nitroglycerin (sublingual or IV)
treatments.result: SL NTG x3 given. Chest discomfort improved.
treatments.reasoning: First-line for anginal symptoms. Response supports ischemic etiology.

treatments.name: Anticoagulation (heparin or enoxaparin)
treatments.result: Heparin drip initiated.
treatments.reasoning: Anticoagulation is standard for ACS to prevent clot propagation.

treatments.name: Beta-blocker (metoprolol)
treatments.result: Metoprolol 25mg PO given.
treatments.reasoning: Reduces myocardial oxygen demand. Standard ACS therapy unless contraindicated.

treatments.name: High-intensity statin (atorvastatin 80mg)
treatments.result: Atorvastatin 80mg given.
treatments.reasoning: High-intensity statin therapy is indicated for all ACS patients.

treatments.name: Supplemental oxygen
treatments.result: SpO2 97% on RA - oxygen not required.
treatments.reasoning: Only indicated if SpO2 <90%. Not needed here.

treatments.name: Morphine for pain
treatments.result: Consider if pain persists despite nitrates.
treatments.reasoning: Third-line for persistent ischemic pain. Use cautiously.

consults.name: Cardiology consult
consults.result: STAT cardiology consult placed. Plan for cardiac catheterization.
consults.reasoning: NSTEMI requires cardiology involvement for risk stratification and consideration of early invasive strategy.

consults.name: Cardiac catheterization timing
consults.result: Plan for cath within 24 hours (early invasive strategy). High-risk features: rising troponin, dynamic ECG changes, HEART score 9, diabetes. Would be URGENT (<2 hours) if: refractory angina, hemodynamic instability, or electrical instability.
consults.reasoning: NSTEMI with high-risk features benefits from early invasive approach (24-48h). STEMI would require immediate cath (door-to-balloon <90 min) or fibrinolysis if transfer >120 min.

disposition.name: Admit to CCU/Cardiac ICU
disposition.result: Admitted for continuous monitoring and cath planning.
disposition.reasoning: NSTEMI requires telemetry monitoring. CCU appropriate for high-risk features.

disposition.name: Admit to telemetry floor
disposition.result: Appropriate if CCU not available and patient stable.
disposition.reasoning: Minimum level of care for ACS - requires continuous monitoring.

disposition.name: Discharge home
disposition.result: INAPPROPRIATE - patient has active ACS.
disposition.reasoning: NEVER discharge an active ACS. Patient requires admission.

heartScore.name: HEART Score
heartScore.description: Stratifies risk of major adverse cardiac events (MACE) in ED chest pain patients

components.criterion: ≥3 risk factors (DM, HTN, smoking, dyslipidemia, family hx, obesity)

thisPatient.nextStep: Admit for serial troponins and early invasive management
thisPatient.clinicalAction: MACE risk ~50-65%. Do NOT discharge. Admit to CCU/telemetry for early invasive strategy.

low.action: Consider discharge with follow-up if troponins negative

intermediate.action: Admit for serial troponins and observation

high.action: Admit for aggressive ACS treatment and early invasive strategy

heartScore.source: Six AJ et al. Neth Heart J. 2008;16(6):191-196

actualDiagnosis.name: NSTEMI (Type 1 MI) - Atherosclerotic Plaque Rupture
keyFindings: Classic ACS symptoms: exertional chest pressure with radiation to arm/jaw
Crescendo pattern: increasing frequency with decreasing exertional threshold = UNSTABLE
Multiple cardiac risk factors: diabetes, hypertension, smoking history, family history, dyslipidemia
Rising troponin (0.08 → 0.15 ng/mL) confirms acute myocardial injury - TYPE 1 MI (plaque rupture)
ECG with ST depression and T-wave inversions in lateral/inferior leads = ischemia
HEART Score: History (2) + ECG (2) + Age (1) + Risk factors (2) + Troponin (2) = 9 = HIGH RISK
Relief with rest and nitroglycerin supports ischemic etiology
This is NOT Type 2 MI (no supply/demand mismatch trigger like tachycardia, hypotension, or anemia)
teachingPoints: HEART SCORE (for ED chest pain): History (0-2) + ECG (0-2) + Age (0-2) + Risk factors (0-2) + Troponin (0-2). Score 0-3 = low risk (safe for discharge), 4-6 = intermediate, 7-10 = high risk. Calculate for every chest pain patient!

ESC 0/1-HOUR ALGORITHM: Rapid rule-out using hs-cTn at 0h and 1h. Rule-OUT if baseline very low OR low + minimal delta at 1h. Rule-IN if high baseline OR significant rise. Observe zone → repeat at 3h.
TYPE 1 vs TYPE 2 MI: Type 1 = atherosclerotic plaque rupture/erosion with thrombosis (this case). Type 2 = supply/demand mismatch (tachycardia, hypotension, anemia, hypoxia). Treatment differs!

NON-ACS CAUSES OF ELEVATED TROPONIN: CKD, HF, myocarditis, PE, sepsis, Takotsubo, severe HTN, arrhythmias. 'When troponin was a lousy assay it was a great test; now it's a great assay, it's a lousy test.'
DIABETIC PATIENTS PRESENT ATYPICALLY: 'Burning' or 'indigestion' quality should not falsely reassure you. Have a low threshold for ACS workup in diabetics.
CRESCENDO ANGINA IS HIGH-RISK: Increasing frequency + decreasing exertional threshold = unstable angina. This is an acute coronary syndrome.
DON'T BE FOOLED BY GERD-LIKE SYMPTOMS: Meal relation and partial antacid relief can occur in ACS. Risk factors should drive your concern.
SERIAL TROPONINS ARE ESSENTIAL: Initial troponin may be normal or mildly elevated. Rising pattern over 3-6 hours confirms acute myocardial injury.
ECG CHANGES IN NSTEMI: ST depression and T-wave inversions indicate ischemia. Unlike STEMI, no urgent need for immediate cath, but early invasive strategy (24-48h) recommended for high-risk features.
STEMI CRITERIA: ≥1mm ST elevation in 2+ contiguous leads (≥2mm in V2-V3 for men). Door-to-balloon goal <90 min for primary PCI. If transfer >120 min, give fibrinolytic.
MODIFIED SGARBOSSA (for LBBB/paced): Concordant ST elevation ≥1mm (5 pts), concordant ST depression V1-V3 (3 pts), discordant ST/S ratio ≥0.25 (2 pts). ≥3 points = STEMI equivalent.
ASPIRIN SAVES LIVES: Give 162-325mg chewed immediately in suspected ACS. P2Y12 inhibitor (clopidogrel 600mg or ticagrelor 180mg) for confirmed ACS.
WOMEN AND DIABETICS: Both groups commonly present with 'atypical' symptoms - dyspnea, fatigue, nausea rather than classic crushing chest pain. Never say 'atypical' - it implies benign.

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: HEART score 0-3 has 30-day MACE rate of only 1.7%, making outpatient management safe
evidenceBasedPearls.source: Backus BE et al., Int J Cardiol 2013
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Low HEART score (0-3) combined with negative serial troponins allows safe ED discharge
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: 30-day MACE: 1.7% (low), 16.6% (moderate), 50.1% (high risk)

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: 0h/1h hs-cTn algorithm safely rules out MI with NPV >99%
evidenceBasedPearls.source: ESC Guidelines 2023; Mueller C et al., NEJM 2019
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Use 0/1-hour hs-cTn protocol when available - can rule out MI in 60% of patients within 1 hour
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: NPV 99.4% for rule-out pathway; 75% sensitivity for rule-in pathway

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Ticagrelor reduced CV death, MI, or stroke vs clopidogrel (9.8% vs 11.7%)
evidenceBasedPearls.source: PLATO Trial, Wallentin L et al., NEJM 2009
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Ticagrelor preferred P2Y12 inhibitor for ACS unless contraindicated (NNT 54 to prevent 1 event)
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: HR 0.84, 95% CI 0.77-0.92; NNT 54; Increased minor bleeding but not major bleeding

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Prasugrel reduced CV events but increased major bleeding, including fatal bleeding
evidenceBasedPearls.source: TRITON-TIMI 38, Wiviott SD et al., NEJM 2007
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Prasugrel contraindicated if prior stroke/TIA, age ≥75, or weight <60kg
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: CV death/MI/stroke: 9.9% vs 12.1% (NNT 46); Major bleeding: 2.4% vs 1.8% (NNH 167)

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Routine early invasive strategy reduces recurrent ischemia and rehospitalization in high-risk NSTEMI
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: High-risk features (rising troponin, ST changes, GRACE >140) benefit from cath within 24 hours
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: Death/MI at 6 months: 7.3% invasive vs 9.5% conservative (TACTICS-TIMI 18)

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Low-dose (75-100mg) maintenance aspirin as effective as higher doses with less bleeding
evidenceBasedPearls.source: CURRENT-OASIS 7, Mehta SR et al., NEJM 2010
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: After initial 162-325mg loading dose, maintain on 81mg daily
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: No difference in CV events; GI bleeding increased with high-dose (HR 1.22)

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Every 30-minute delay in PCI increases mortality by 7.5%
evidenceBasedPearls.source: De Luca G et al., Circulation 2004
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Door-to-balloon goal <90 minutes. If transfer time >120 min, give fibrinolytics
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: 1-year mortality: 4.3% (<90min) vs 8.4% (>90min)

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Rising troponin pattern (delta >20% at 3-6h) distinguishes acute MI from chronic elevation
evidenceBasedPearls.source: Fourth Universal Definition of MI, Circulation 2018
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Serial troponins essential - single value insufficient. Look for rise-and-fall pattern
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: 99th percentile URL defines MI; rise/fall pattern required for Type 1 MI diagnosis

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Diabetics are 2x more likely to present with 'atypical' symptoms or 'silent' ischemia
evidenceBasedPearls.source: Canto JG et al., JAMA 2000; Zellweger MJ et al., Eur Heart J 2004
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Maintain high suspicion for ACS in diabetics even without classic chest pain - dyspnea, fatigue, nausea may be only symptoms
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: 32% of MI in diabetics present without chest pain vs 15% in non-diabetics

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Colchicine 0.5mg daily reduced CV events by 23% when started within 30 days of MI
evidenceBasedPearls.source: COLCOT Trial, Tardif JC et al., NEJM 2019
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Consider low-dose colchicine for secondary prevention in post-MI patients
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: CV death/MI/stroke/revascularization: 5.5% vs 7.1% (HR 0.77, NNT 63)

closureScript.diagnosis: I need to share some important news with you. Your blood tests and heart tracings show that you're having what we call a heart attack - specifically, your heart muscle isn't getting enough blood flow. The good news is that we caught this and can treat it.
closureScript.plan: We're going to admit you to our cardiac care unit right away. You'll have a procedure called a cardiac catheterization - probably tomorrow - where we'll look at the arteries supplying your heart and open any blockages we find. In the meantime, we're giving you medications to thin your blood and protect your heart muscle.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: While you're here, tell us immediately if the chest discomfort comes back or gets worse, if you feel short of breath, dizzy, or like your heart is racing. These could mean we need to act faster.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: I know this is scary news to hear. But you did exactly the right thing by coming in, and catching this when we did gives us the best chance to protect your heart. Your diabetes made your symptoms harder to recognize - that's why I want you to know that any chest discomfort in the future needs to be taken very seriously.

male-32-msk.name: J. Park
male-32-msk.chiefComplaint: Sharp chest pain when I move or breathe

missedConsequences.Costochondritis: If this is the actual diagnosis (it is), appropriate treatment is NSAIDs and reassurance. No adverse outcome.
missedConsequences.Pulmonary Embolism: If PE were present and missed, could be fatal. Important to have low threshold for D-dimer in right clinical context.
missedConsequences.Acute Coronary Syndrome: Missing ACS would be catastrophic, but this patient has no risk factors and presentation is clearly musculoskeletal.

commonPitfalls: Ordering extensive cardiac workup for clearly musculoskeletal pain - drives up costs and patient anxiety
Conversely, missing PE or ACS because pain is 'reproducible' - reproducibility doesn't rule out serious disease
Not asking about recent physical exertion, trauma, or new exercises
Forgetting that chest wall pain should be REPRODUCIBLE on exam - if not reproducible, reconsider diagnosis
Not reassuring the patient adequately - chest pain causes significant anxiety

mcqs.question: This 32-year-old with no cardiac risk factors has sharp, positional chest pain that worsens with deep breathing and started after lifting weights. What feature would MOST increase concern for cardiac disease?

options.text: Pain with palpation of the chest wall
options.explanation: Chest wall tenderness supports musculoskeletal cause.

options.text: Pain that came on during exertion and was associated with diaphoresis or dyspnea
options.explanation: Exertional onset with associated symptoms (diaphoresis, dyspnea, radiation) are red flags for cardiac etiology even in young patients. This patient's pain started AFTER exercise (during recovery), which is more consistent with MSK.

options.text: Pain that is worse with inspiration
options.explanation: Pleuritic pain can be musculoskeletal, pericarditis, PE, or pneumonia - but not typically cardiac ischemia.
Duration doesn't distinguish MSK from cardiac.

mcqs.question: You can reproduce the patient's exact pain by pressing on his left costochondral junction. What does this finding indicate?

options.text: This rules out cardiac disease completely
options.explanation: Reproducibility makes MSK more likely but doesn't completely rule out cardiac disease - up to 15% of ACS patients have some chest wall tenderness.

options.text: Costochondritis is very likely, but serious causes aren't completely excluded
options.explanation: Reproducible chest wall tenderness strongly supports costochondritis. However, chest wall tenderness can coexist with cardiac disease (15% of MI patients). Clinical judgment using the whole picture is key.

options.text: The patient needs a CT scan to confirm
options.explanation: Costochondritis is a clinical diagnosis. CT is not helpful.

options.text: This indicates referred pain from the heart
options.explanation: Cardiac pain is not reproducible with chest wall palpation.

allDiagnoses.name: Costochondritis / Musculoskeletal
allDiagnoses.rationale: ⭐ ACTUAL DIAGNOSIS. Young male, no risk factors, sharp pleuritic pain after weightlifting, reproducible on palpation.

allDiagnoses.name: Muscle Strain
allDiagnoses.rationale: Similar presentation, started after exercise. Treatment same as costochondritis.

allDiagnoses.name: Viral Pleuritis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can cause pleuritic chest pain. Usually with recent URI symptoms.

allDiagnoses.name: Pulmonary Embolism
allDiagnoses.rationale: PE can cause pleuritic chest pain. Low pretest probability here (no risk factors) but must consider.

allDiagnoses.name: Pneumothorax
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can occur spontaneously in young males. Usually sudden onset with dyspnea. Chest X-ray can rule out.

allDiagnoses.name: Acute Coronary Syndrome
allDiagnoses.rationale: Always consider, but very low probability in 32yo with no risk factors and pleuritic/reproducible pain.

allDiagnoses.name: GERD
allDiagnoses.rationale: Usually burning, postprandial, not pleuritic or positional.

allDiagnoses.name: Pericarditis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would expect positional relief (leaning forward), possible friction rub, often post-viral.

male-32-msk.oldcartsNarrative: "I've had this sharp pain on the left side of my chest for about 3 days. It started the day after I did a really hard chest workout at the gym - lots of bench press and pushups. The pain is sharp, not dull. It gets worse when I take a deep breath, when I move my left arm, or when I press on my chest. It doesn't radiate anywhere. I'm pretty healthy - I work out 4-5 times a week. I actually thought it would go away on its own but it's still there so I came in."

historyQuestions.text: When and how did the pain start?
historyQuestions.answer: It started 3 days ago, the morning after a hard chest workout. I did a lot of bench pressing and pushups. Woke up and felt this sharp pain.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Post-exercise onset strongly supports musculoskeletal etiology. Pain appearing AFTER (not during) exercise less concerning for cardiac.

historyQuestions.text: Describe the pain - sharp, dull, pressure?
historyQuestions.answer: Sharp, like a stabbing feeling. Not pressure or squeezing at all.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Sharp, stabbing quality suggests musculoskeletal or pleural. Cardiac ischemia typically causes pressure, squeezing, or tightness.

historyQuestions.text: Does position or movement affect the pain?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, definitely. It hurts more when I take a deep breath, when I twist my body, or when I move my left arm. Lying flat is uncomfortable.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Pleuritic (worse with breathing) and positional pain strongly supports musculoskeletal or pleural etiology. Cardiac ischemia is NOT positional.

historyQuestions.text: Does pressing on your chest affect the pain?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes! If I press right here on my chest, I can make the exact pain happen.
historyQuestions.reasoning: REPRODUCIBILITY with palpation is classic for costochondritis. Key physical exam finding to confirm.

historyQuestions.text: Any shortness of breath, sweating, nausea, or arm pain?
historyQuestions.answer: No, none of that. Just this sharp chest pain. I can breathe fine, I'm not sweating, no nausea.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Absence of associated symptoms (dyspnea, diaphoresis, nausea, radiation) argues against ACS.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any history of heart disease, high blood pressure, diabetes, or high cholesterol?
historyQuestions.answer: No, nothing. I'm really healthy. I work out all the time, eat well. No family history of heart attacks either.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No cardiac risk factors dramatically lowers pretest probability for ACS in young patient.

historyQuestions.text: Do you smoke or use any recreational drugs?
historyQuestions.answer: No smoking ever. No drugs. I occasionally have a few beers on weekends.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Smoking increases risk for both ACS and spontaneous pneumothorax. Cocaine can cause coronary vasospasm.

physicalExam.finding: BP 122/78, HR 76, RR 16, Temp 98.4°F, SpO2 99%. All normal.
physicalExam.reasoning: Normal vitals reassuring. Tachycardia/hypoxia would raise concern for PE or other serious cause.

physicalExam.finding: Healthy-appearing 32-year-old male, athletic build, in no acute distress. Comfortable at rest.
physicalExam.reasoning: Comfortable appearance reassuring. Patients with PE or ACS often appear ill or anxious.

physicalExam.finding: REPRODUCIBLE TENDERNESS at left 3rd-4th costochondral junctions. Pressing here exactly reproduces the patient's pain. No swelling or warmth.
physicalExam.reasoning: 🎯 KEY FINDING. Reproducible tenderness at costochondral junction confirms costochondritis. This is the diagnostic finding.

physicalExam.finding: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1, S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No JVD. Peripheral pulses intact.
physicalExam.reasoning: Normal cardiac exam reassuring. Pericardial friction rub would suggest pericarditis.

physicalExam.finding: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi. Equal breath sounds. Normal respiratory effort.
physicalExam.reasoning: Equal breath sounds argue against pneumothorax. Clear lungs argue against pneumonia.

labs.name: ECG
labs.result: Normal sinus rhythm at 74 bpm. No ST changes. No T wave abnormalities. Normal intervals.
labs.reasoning: Low threshold for ECG in any chest pain. Normal here provides reassurance and is reasonable to obtain.

labs.name: Troponin
labs.reasoning: Very low pretest probability but provides additional reassurance. Many would argue unnecessary with classic MSK findings.

labs.name: CBC
labs.reasoning: Not specifically indicated but part of routine labs if obtained.

labs.name: BMP
labs.reasoning: Not specifically indicated for costochondritis.

labs.name: BNP
labs.reasoning: Not indicated - no suspicion for heart failure. Ordering this is overutilization.

labs.name: D-dimer
labs.result: NOT INDICATED - pretest probability for PE is very low.
labs.reasoning: Wells PE score is 0. Testing low-probability patients leads to false positives and unnecessary CT scans.

labs.name: Lipid Panel
labs.reasoning: Can be checked as outpatient if indicated. Not relevant to acute presentation.

labs.name: CRP / ESR
labs.reasoning: Sometimes elevated in costochondritis but doesn't change management.

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.result: No pneumothorax. No infiltrates. No cardiomegaly. Normal mediastinum.
imaging.reasoning: Rules out pneumothorax and pneumonia. Not strictly necessary with classic costochondritis but reasonable.

imaging.name: CT Chest
imaging.result: NOT INDICATED for costochondritis.
imaging.reasoning: Unnecessary radiation and cost. Chest X-ray is sufficient if imaging desired.

imaging.name: CT Angiography (PE protocol)
imaging.result: NOT INDICATED - low pretest probability for PE.
imaging.reasoning: Wells score 0, no risk factors. CTA would be significant overutilization with radiation and contrast exposure.

imaging.name: Echocardiogram
imaging.result: NOT INDICATED for classic MSK chest pain.
imaging.reasoning: No cardiac findings to warrant echo. Would be overutilization.

imaging.name: Stress Test
imaging.result: NOT INDICATED for clearly musculoskeletal pain.
imaging.reasoning: Young patient, no risk factors, classic MSK presentation. Stress testing is inappropriate and increases anxiety.

procedures.name: IV access
procedures.reasoning: Patient is stable, low-risk. IV not required but can be placed if labs being drawn.

treatments.name: NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400-600mg TID)
treatments.result: Ibuprofen 400mg TID with food for 7-10 days.
treatments.reasoning: First-line treatment for costochondritis. Anti-inflammatory effect targets the pathology.

treatments.name: Acetaminophen
treatments.result: Tylenol 650mg q6h PRN as alternative if NSAIDs contraindicated.
treatments.reasoning: Less effective than NSAIDs but option if GI issues or NSAID intolerance.

treatments.name: Ice and rest
treatments.result: Apply ice to affected area 15-20 min several times daily, avoid aggravating activities.
treatments.reasoning: Supportive measures to reduce inflammation and pain.

treatments.name: Heat application
treatments.result: Warm compresses may help after initial inflammation subsides.
treatments.reasoning: Some patients find heat more helpful than ice.

treatments.name: Reassurance and education
treatments.result: Patient reassured this is not a heart problem. Educated about costochondritis - usually resolves in 1-2 weeks.
treatments.reasoning: Addressing patient anxiety is crucial. Many patients with chest pain fear heart attack.

treatments.name: Topical NSAID (diclofenac gel)
treatments.result: Can apply topical diclofenac to chest wall if preferred.
treatments.reasoning: Alternative to oral NSAIDs with fewer systemic side effects.

treatments.name: Opioid pain medication
treatments.result: NOT INDICATED for costochondritis.
treatments.reasoning: NSAIDs are more effective. Opioids inappropriate for this condition.

treatments.name: Aspirin 325mg
treatments.result: NOT INDICATED - this is not ACS.
treatments.reasoning: Would only give if concerned for ACS. This is clearly musculoskeletal.

treatments.name: Nitroglycerin
treatments.result: NOT INDICATED - no concern for cardiac ischemia.
treatments.reasoning: Would only use if suspecting angina. Inappropriate for MSK pain.

consults.name: Cardiology consult
consults.result: NOT INDICATED for classic costochondritis.
consults.reasoning: No cardiac findings. Consulting cardiology would be inappropriate overutilization.

consults.name: Physical therapy referral
consults.result: Consider if symptoms persist beyond 2-3 weeks or recurrent episodes.
consults.reasoning: Not needed initially but option for refractory or recurrent cases.

disposition.name: Discharge home with NSAIDs
disposition.result: Discharged with ibuprofen prescription, ice/rest instructions, and return precautions.
disposition.reasoning: Appropriate for uncomplicated costochondritis. Safe to discharge.

disposition.name: Observation unit
disposition.result: NOT INDICATED for classic costochondritis.
disposition.reasoning: Low-risk presentation does not require observation. Would be overutilization.

disposition.name: Hospital admission
disposition.result: NOT INDICATED - this is a benign outpatient diagnosis.
disposition.reasoning: Admitting costochondritis would be grossly inappropriate.

actualDiagnosis.name: Costochondritis (Musculoskeletal Chest Pain)
keyFindings: ⚠️ DIFFERENT DIAGNOSIS than NSTEMI variant - same chief complaint!
Sharp, pleuritic, positional pain (NOT pressure/squeezing)
Started after intense chest workout (clear precipitant)
REPRODUCIBLE on palpation of costochondral junction (diagnostic)
No cardiac risk factors in 32-year-old
No associated symptoms (no dyspnea, diaphoresis, nausea)
Normal ECG and vital signs
teachingPoints: ⚠️ SAME CHIEF COMPLAINT, DIFFERENT DIAGNOSIS: 'Chest pain' can be cardiac (NSTEMI) or benign (costochondritis). History and exam distinguish them.
PAIN CHARACTER MATTERS: Sharp/stabbing/pleuritic = think MSK/pleural. Pressure/squeezing/heaviness = think cardiac.
REPRODUCIBILITY SUPPORTS MSK: If you can reproduce the exact pain with palpation, costochondritis is very likely (but not 100% - 15% of MI patients have some chest wall tenderness).
RISK STRATIFICATION: Young patient + no risk factors + no concerning features + reproducible pain = very low probability of ACS.
AVOID OVER-TESTING: Don't order troponins, stress tests, and CT angiograms for clearly musculoskeletal pain. It's costly and increases patient anxiety.
REASSURANCE IS THERAPEUTIC: Patients with chest pain are often terrified of heart attack. Clear explanation that this is muscular is part of the treatment.
WHEN TO WORRY: Even in MSK chest pain, reconsider if: exertional symptoms, dyspnea, diaphoresis, radiation, risk factors, or abnormal ECG.

guidelineReferences.name: AHA/ACC Guideline for Evaluation of Chest Pain 2021
guidelineReferences.citation: Gulati M et al. Circulation. 2021;144(22):e368-e454
keyPoints: Pre-test probability (PTP) guides testing decisions
Very low PTP: No cardiac testing needed
Reproducible chest wall tenderness suggests MSK etiology
Avoid routine stress testing in low-risk patients
Clinical assessment alone sufficient for many presentations

guidelineReferences.name: ESC Guidelines for Chronic Coronary Syndromes 2019
guidelineReferences.citation: Knuuti J et al. Eur Heart J. 2020;41(3):407-477
keyPoints: Non-anginal chest pain: No further cardiac testing if low clinical likelihood
Typical angina: Substernal, exertional, relieved by rest/nitrates
Atypical angina: Meets 2 of 3 criteria
MSK causes are most common cause of chest pain in primary care
Clinical assessment can rule out CAD in very low probability patients

closureScript.diagnosis: Good news - this is not your heart. What you have is costochondritis, which means inflammation of the cartilage that connects your ribs to your breastbone. When I pressed on your chest and reproduced your exact pain, that confirmed it. This almost certainly happened from your intense chest workout.
closureScript.plan: I'm going to give you ibuprofen to take for the next week or so - that will reduce the inflammation and help with the pain. Take it with food to protect your stomach. You should also ice the area and take it easy on the chest exercises for a couple weeks.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: This should get better over the next 1-2 weeks. Come back if the pain gets worse instead of better, if you develop shortness of breath or feel like your heart is racing, if you feel lightheaded, or if you develop fever.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: I can tell you were worried this might be a heart attack - that's completely understandable. But your heart is fine. You're young, healthy, have no risk factors, and this pain has all the features of a muscular problem - it's sharp, positional, and I could reproduce it by pressing on your chest. Heart attacks don't work like that.
