whatIfScenarios.title: What if the pain occurred with exertion?
whatIfScenarios.description: The chest pain starts when walking up stairs and stops with rest

chiefComplaint.original: I've been having chest pain since yesterday
chiefComplaint.modified: I get chest pain every time I walk up stairs - it goes away when I rest

feedback.correct: Excellent! Exertional chest pain that resolves with rest is classic stable angina. Needs outpatient stress testing and cardiology referral.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of concerning features. What makes this stable vs unstable angina?
feedback.incorrect: Exertional chest pain = angina until proven otherwise. Classic pattern: predictable with exertion, substernal, relieved by rest. Requires cardiac evaluation.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Rest pain or unprovoked pain = higher risk (ACS workup). Exertional pain only = stable angina. Still need stress test, but can be outpatient if no high-risk features. Consider aortic stenosis if murmur present.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the pain was worse with deep breathing?
whatIfScenarios.description: Sharp chest pain that worsens with deep inspiration and coughing

chiefComplaint.original: I've been having chest pain since yesterday
chiefComplaint.modified: I have sharp chest pain that hurts more when I breathe in deeply or cough

feedback.correct: Good recognition of pleuritic pain! This pattern requires consideration of PE (especially with risk factors), pericarditis, pneumonia, or pneumothorax.
feedback.partial: Pleuritic pain shifts the differential. What pulmonary diagnosis must not be missed?
feedback.incorrect: Pleuritic (worse with breathing) suggests pleural/pericardial involvement. PE is a must-not-miss - use Wells score. Also consider pericarditis, pneumothorax, and pneumonia.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Cardiac ischemia = typically pressure/squeezing, NOT pleuritic. Pleuritic pain = think PE, pericarditis, pneumonia, pneumothorax. However, 20% of PE patients have NO pleuritic pain - so absence doesn't rule it out.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the pain radiated to the jaw?
whatIfScenarios.description: Chest pressure with radiation to the left jaw and arm

chiefComplaint.original: I've been having chest pain since yesterday
chiefComplaint.modified: I have chest pressure that goes up into my jaw and down my left arm

feedback.correct: This is classic ACS presentation. Immediate ECG, troponin, aspirin, and cardiology activation if STEMI.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of high-risk features. What's the immediate next step?
feedback.incorrect: Substernal pressure radiating to jaw/arm = ACS until proven otherwise. Immediate ECG (within 10 minutes), serial troponins, ASA, and activate cardiology if ST elevation.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Isolated chest pain may be low-risk. Radiation to jaw, left arm, or neck significantly increases probability of ACS. Immediate ECG and troponin mandatory. This is not 'wait and see.'

questions.stem: Based on the clinical presentation, which is the MOST appropriate next step?

options.text: Systematic history and focused physical examination
options.explanation: A targeted history and physical exam is the essential first step in clinical reasoning, guiding hypothesis generation and differential diagnosis.
Empiric treatment before adequate evaluation risks missing the correct diagnosis and may cause harm.
Diagnostic testing should be hypothesis-driven, not shotgun; targeted tests based on clinical reasoning yield better outcomes.
Consultation may be appropriate later but should follow initial clinical assessment and diagnostic reasoning.

questions.stem: Which cognitive bias poses the greatest risk in this clinical scenario?

options.explanation: Availability bias (overweighting recent experiences) is common but not the most frequent source of error in this scenario.
Anchoring (fixating on initial impression) is a risk but can be mitigated by systematic differential generation.
Premature closure — accepting a diagnosis before it is fully verified — is the single most common cognitive error in clinical reasoning across all settings.
Confirmation bias (seeking only supporting evidence) contributes to error but often follows from premature closure.

guidelineReferences.name: ACC/AHA/HRS Syncope Guidelines 2017
guidelineReferences.citation: Shen WK, et al. Circulation. 2017;136:e60-e122
keyPoints: History, physical exam, and ECG for initial evaluation (Class I)
Risk stratify: high-risk features include structural heart disease, abnormal ECG, syncope during exertion
Echocardiography when cardiac cause suspected
Tilt table testing for recurrent unexplained syncope
Hospital admission for high-risk features or serious conditions
Vasovagal syncope: education, hydration, counter-pressure maneuvers

adult-40-as.name: M. Torres
adult-40-as.chiefComplaint: I passed out while climbing stairs
adult-40-as.variantDescription: Exertional syncope from severe aortic stenosis

missedConsequences.Aortic Stenosis: Patient discharged with 'vasovagal syncope' and later has sudden cardiac death during exercise. Symptomatic severe AS has 50% mortality at 2 years without valve replacement.
missedConsequences.Cardiac Arrhythmia: Undiagnosed VT leads to cardiac arrest at home. Without defibrillation, patient does not survive.
missedConsequences.Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy (HOCM): HOCM patient continues to exercise, suffers sudden cardiac death on basketball court.

commonPitfalls: Assuming all syncope in young adults is vasovagal - EXERTIONAL syncope is always concerning for cardiac cause
Dismissing a 'childhood murmur' as benign - bicuspid aortic valve is the #1 cause of AS in patients under 65
Not performing dynamic auscultation - Valsalva and standing maneuvers distinguish AS from HOCM
Forgetting the AS triad: angina, syncope, dyspnea - once symptomatic, prognosis is poor without intervention
Missing pulsus parvus et tardus on exam - delayed, weak carotid upstroke is specific for severe AS

mcqs.question: A 40-year-old man passes out while climbing stairs. He has a known murmur since childhood. What is the MOST concerning feature of this presentation?

options.text: He passed out - syncope is always concerning
options.explanation: While syncope needs evaluation, the specific circumstances matter more than syncope alone.

options.text: EXERTIONAL syncope with a known murmur - suggests serious cardiac cause
options.explanation: Exertional syncope is ALWAYS pathologic until proven otherwise. Combined with a childhood murmur (possibly bicuspid aortic valve), this is highly concerning for severe aortic stenosis causing inadequate cardiac output during exertion.

options.text: His age - 40 is young for cardiac disease
options.explanation: While 40 is young, bicuspid aortic valve can cause severe AS by age 40-50.

options.text: No chest pain - this makes cardiac cause unlikely
options.explanation: Absence of chest pain doesn't rule out AS. The classic AS triad is angina, syncope, dyspnea - not all need be present.

mcqs.question: You hear a crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur at the right upper sternal border that radiates to the carotids. What maneuver would help distinguish aortic stenosis from HOCM?

options.text: Deep inspiration - increases AS murmur, decreases HOCM murmur
options.explanation: Inspiration affects right-sided murmurs more than left-sided.

options.text: Valsalva - AS murmur decreases, HOCM murmur increases
options.explanation: Valsalva decreases preload. In AS, less blood = softer murmur. In HOCM, less preload = more obstruction = LOUDER murmur. This is a key distinguishing maneuver.
Position changes can affect murmurs but don't make them disappear.
Handgrip increases afterload, which DECREASES HOCM murmur and doesn't significantly change AS.

allDiagnoses.name: Aortic Stenosis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Exertional syncope with known murmur since childhood. Classic presentation of symptomatic AS. Bicuspid aortic valve can cause critical AS even in young adults.

allDiagnoses.name: Vasovagal Syncope
allDiagnoses.rationale: Common cause of syncope, but typically has prodrome (lightheadedness, warmth, nausea) and occurs in specific situations (prolonged standing, emotional stress). Less likely given exertional pattern.

allDiagnoses.name: Cardiac Arrhythmia
allDiagnoses.rationale: VT, AV block, or other arrhythmia can cause sudden syncope without prodrome. Must rule out, especially with structural heart disease.

allDiagnoses.name: Acute Myocardial Infarction
allDiagnoses.rationale: MI can present with syncope. Less likely at age 40 without risk factors, but must consider.

allDiagnoses.name: Pulmonary Embolism
allDiagnoses.rationale: Large PE can cause syncope from acute right heart failure. No DVT symptoms here but must consider.

allDiagnoses.name: Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy (HOCM)
allDiagnoses.rationale: Another cause of exertional syncope with murmur. HOCM murmur behaves differently than AS (increases with Valsalva/standing).

allDiagnoses.name: Orthostatic Hypotension
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can cause syncope but typically with positional changes, not exertion. Should check orthostatics.

allDiagnoses.name: Seizure
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can mimic syncope. Look for post-ictal confusion, tongue biting, incontinence. Patient had rapid recovery.

allDiagnoses.name: Carotid Sinus Hypersensitivity
allDiagnoses.rationale: Usually in older patients. Triggered by neck pressure/turning.

adult-40-as.oldcartsNarrative: "I was climbing the stairs at work - just a couple of flights like I do every day - and suddenly I felt really lightheaded and my vision started going gray. Next thing I knew I was on the landing and my coworker was helping me up. I was only out for a few seconds, maybe 10-15 seconds, and then I felt pretty normal right away. No confusion after. This is the first time I've actually passed out, but I've been getting more winded than usual going up stairs the last few months, and sometimes I feel like my heart is pounding. I've always known I had a heart murmur since I was a kid - they said it was nothing to worry about. I did feel a little chest tightness when I was climbing before I passed out."

historyQuestions.text: When did this happen? Tell me exactly what happened.
historyQuestions.answer: It happened about 2 hours ago. I was climbing stairs at work - just a couple flights - and suddenly felt lightheaded, my vision went gray, and next thing I knew I was on the landing with my coworker helping me up.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Timing and circumstances are critical - exertional syncope requires urgent cardiac evaluation

historyQuestions.text: Does the lightheadedness or near-syncope happen with exertion specifically?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, mostly when I'm doing something physical - stairs, walking fast, sometimes when I'm exercising. Never just sitting around.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CRITICAL: Exertional syncope is always pathologic until proven otherwise. In setting of known murmur, strongly suggests valvular or structural heart disease.

historyQuestions.text: Did you have any warning before you passed out - lightheadedness, nausea, warmth, tunnel vision?
historyQuestions.answer: Just a brief moment of feeling lightheaded and my vision going gray, then nothing. Maybe 5-10 seconds of warning.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Minimal prodrome suggests cardiac cause. Vasovagal typically has longer prodrome with nausea/warmth. Arrhythmia can have no prodrome at all.

historyQuestions.text: How did you feel immediately after waking up?
historyQuestions.answer: Pretty normal honestly. A little shaken up but not confused or anything. I could stand up right away.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Rapid recovery without post-ictal confusion argues against seizure. Consistent with cardiac cause where once perfusion restored, patient recovers quickly.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me more about this heart murmur you've had since childhood.
historyQuestions.answer: They found it when I was maybe 10. The doctors listened a few times over the years and said it was probably a bicuspid valve or something, but nothing to worry about. I've never had any problems with it until recently.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CRITICAL: Bicuspid aortic valve is the most common cause of aortic stenosis in patients under 65. Previously 'innocent' murmur now causing symptoms = progressive stenosis.

historyQuestions.text: You mentioned being more winded lately - can you describe that?
historyQuestions.answer: I used to run up stairs no problem. Now even two flights and I'm breathing hard. It's been getting worse over the past 6 months or so.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Progressive dyspnea on exertion is part of the classic AS triad (angina, syncope, dyspnea). Indicates symptomatic AS - this changes prognosis and management.

historyQuestions.text: Any chest pain or chest tightness?
historyQuestions.answer: Sometimes when I'm exerting myself I get this tight feeling in my chest. It goes away when I stop. No pain at rest.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Exertional chest tightness completing the AS triad: Angina + Syncope + Dyspnea. This is symptomatic severe AS until proven otherwise.

historyQuestions.text: Any palpitations or feeling like your heart is racing or skipping?
historyQuestions.answer: Sometimes I feel like my heart is pounding, especially after activity. No skipping or fluttering.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Pounding sensation may be hyperdynamic LV trying to overcome obstruction. No clear arrhythmia symptoms, but would still want ECG.

historyQuestions.text: Any family history of sudden death or heart problems at a young age?
historyQuestions.answer: No, not that I know of. My parents are healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Family history of sudden death would raise concern for inherited cardiomyopathy or channelopathy.

historyQuestions.text: Any recent illness, fever, or infections?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I've been healthy otherwise.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No recent illness that could suggest endocarditis or myocarditis.

historyQuestions.text: Are you taking any medications?
historyQuestions.answer: Just a multivitamin. Nothing prescription.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No medications that could cause orthostatic hypotension or arrhythmia.

historyQuestions.text: Any allergies to medications?
historyQuestions.answer: No allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important for any potential treatments or procedures

historyQuestions.text: Any other medical problems besides the heart murmur?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I've always been healthy. Never been hospitalized. Just that murmur they found when I was a kid.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Confirms generally healthy patient with single finding of known murmur

historyQuestions.text: Any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries ever.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No prior cardiac interventions

historyQuestions.text: Do you smoke, drink, or use any drugs?
historyQuestions.answer: I don't smoke. I'll have a beer or two on weekends. No drugs.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No substance use that would contribute to arrhythmia or syncope

historyQuestions.text: What do you do for work?
historyQuestions.answer: I'm a software engineer. Office job, pretty sedentary other than taking the stairs.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Sedentary occupation; stairs are main physical exertion where symptoms occur

historyQuestions.text: When was the last time someone evaluated your heart?
historyQuestions.answer: I haven't seen a doctor in a few years honestly. Probably 5-6 years since anyone listened to my heart.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Long gap in cardiac monitoring - AS can progress significantly over 5 years

historyQuestions.text: What are you most worried about?
historyQuestions.answer: I'm worried something is wrong with my heart. This was scary. I've never passed out before and now I'm nervous about exercising.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Patient has appropriate concern; will need activity restriction until workup complete

sports_training_intensity.answer: High school varsity basketball. Practice every day, games twice a week. I'm one of the starters.
sports_training_intensity.relevance: High-intensity competitive athletics with exertional syncope is a red flag for HOCM or other structural heart disease - leading cause of sudden cardiac death in young athletes.

sports_training_environment.answer: Usually in the gym. Sometimes outdoor conditioning in the heat.
sports_training_environment.relevance: Heat exposure can worsen dehydration and trigger syncope; however, symptoms occurring indoors suggest intrinsic cardiac cause.

diet_hydration_sports.answer: I try to drink a lot during practice. Gatorade and water. Coach is pretty good about breaks.
diet_hydration_sports.relevance: Adequate hydration makes dehydration less likely as primary cause of exertional syncope.

diet_supplements_stimulants.answer: Sometimes energy drinks before games. Red Bull or Monster. Pre-workout sometimes.
diet_supplements_stimulants.relevance: Energy drinks and pre-workout supplements containing caffeine/stimulants can trigger arrhythmias, especially with underlying structural heart disease.

occupation_school_performance_pressure.answer: Lots of pressure. Scouts might be watching. Coach expects a lot from me.
occupation_school_performance_pressure.relevance: Performance pressure may mask symptoms or discourage reporting; athletes may 'push through' warning signs.

recreation_other_physical_activity.answer: Weight lifting, running. I work out on my own too, not just practice.
recreation_other_physical_activity.relevance: Additional intense exercise increases total cardiovascular strain and risk for those with underlying cardiac conditions.

social_sleep_patterns.answer: Not great during the season. Up late with homework, early for practice. Maybe 6 hours.
social_sleep_patterns.relevance: Sleep deprivation can contribute to syncope and may lower threshold for arrhythmias.

housing_heat_exposure.answer: House is air conditioned. School gym gets hot but not terrible.
housing_heat_exposure.relevance: No extreme heat exposure that would explain exertional syncope.

physicalExam.finding: Lying: BP 118/78, HR 70. Standing: BP 114/76, HR 76. No significant orthostatic changes.
physicalExam.reasoning: Negative orthostatics make orthostatic hypotension less likely as cause.

physicalExam.finding: Well-appearing male, no acute distress, no pallor or diaphoresis.
physicalExam.reasoning: Currently stable. No signs of ongoing hemodynamic compromise.

physicalExam.finding: Grade 3/6 harsh crescendo-decrescendo systolic ejection murmur, loudest at right upper sternal border (aortic area), radiating to both carotids. Normal S1, soft/single S2. No S3. Possible S4 present.
physicalExam.reasoning: CLASSIC AS MURMUR: Crescendo-decrescendo at RUSB radiating to carotids. Soft S2 indicates immobile valve. S4 suggests LV stiffness from hypertrophy.

physicalExam.finding: Carotid pulses are diminished bilaterally with delayed upstroke (pulsus parvus et tardus). Carotid thrill palpable.
physicalExam.reasoning: PULSUS PARVUS ET TARDUS is pathognomonic for severe AS. The pulse is weak (parvus) and slow-rising (tardus) due to obstruction.

physicalExam.finding: Sustained, forceful apical impulse. Not displaced. Systolic thrill at base.
physicalExam.reasoning: Sustained apical impulse indicates LV hypertrophy from chronic pressure overload. Thrill confirms significant turbulence.

physicalExam.finding: Murmur DECREASES with Valsalva and standing (decreased preload). Murmur INCREASES with squatting (increased preload). Opposite of HOCM pattern.
physicalExam.reasoning: CRITICAL FOR DIFFERENTIATION: AS murmur decreases with decreased preload (less blood crossing valve). HOCM murmur would increase. This confirms AS.

physicalExam.finding: Peripheral pulses present but diminished throughout. No radial-femoral delay.
physicalExam.reasoning: Diminished peripheral pulses consistent with reduced stroke volume from AS.

physicalExam.finding: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No crackles or wheezes.
physicalExam.reasoning: No pulmonary edema currently, though may develop if AS progresses.

physicalExam.finding: JVP normal, not elevated.
physicalExam.reasoning: No right heart failure signs.

physicalExam.finding: No peripheral edema. No calf tenderness.
physicalExam.reasoning: No signs of DVT or right heart failure.

physicalExam.finding: Alert, oriented x4. No focal deficits. Cranial nerves intact. Normal gait.
physicalExam.reasoning: No post-ictal findings. No focal deficits that would suggest stroke/TIA.

labs.name: ECG/EKG
labs.result: Sinus rhythm at 72 bpm. Left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria (deep S in V1, tall R in V5-V6). Left atrial enlargement. No acute ischemic changes.
labs.reasoning: LVH on ECG is expected with chronic AS from pressure overload. Important to rule out arrhythmia as cause of syncope.

labs.name: Troponin
labs.reasoning: Rules out MI as cause of syncope. Important given exertional chest tightness.

labs.name: BNP/NT-proBNP
labs.reasoning: Elevated BNP indicates cardiac strain and helps risk-stratify symptomatic AS. Higher BNP associated with worse outcomes.

labs.name: CBC
labs.reasoning: Rules out severe anemia as contributor to symptoms (anemia can worsen AS symptoms).

labs.name: BMP
labs.reasoning: Baseline for contrast administration if cardiac cath needed. Rules out electrolyte abnormalities.

labs.name: PT/INR, PTT
labs.reasoning: Baseline coagulation. Important if anticoagulation or procedures anticipated.

labs.name: Lipid Panel
labs.reasoning: Cardiovascular risk assessment. Does not change acute management.

labs.name: Hemoglobin A1c
labs.reasoning: Baseline metabolic assessment. Diabetes is CV risk factor.

labs.name: TSH
labs.reasoning: Thyroid dysfunction can affect cardiac function. Not primary concern here.

imaging.name: Transthoracic Echocardiogram
imaging.result: CRITICAL: Bicuspid aortic valve with severe aortic stenosis. Aortic valve area 0.8 cm² (normal >2.0). Mean gradient 48 mmHg. Peak velocity 4.5 m/s. Concentric LVH. EF 55%.
imaging.reasoning: DIAGNOSTIC TEST OF CHOICE for AS. Confirms severe stenosis (AVA <1.0 cm², mean gradient >40). This patient needs intervention.

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.result: Mild cardiomegaly. No pulmonary edema. Post-stenotic dilation of ascending aorta. Calcified aortic valve visible.
imaging.reasoning: Post-stenotic dilation is common with AS. Calcified valve may be visible. Rules out pulmonary edema.

imaging.name: CT Chest with calcium scoring
imaging.result: Would show heavily calcified aortic valve. Ascending aortic dilation 4.2 cm.
imaging.reasoning: Calcium scoring can be part of pre-procedural planning. Assesses ascending aorta for aneurysm (common with bicuspid valve).

imaging.name: Cardiac Catheterization
imaging.result: May be performed pre-operatively to assess coronary arteries.
imaging.reasoning: Often done before valve surgery in patients >40 or with CAD risk factors to assess need for concurrent CABG.

imaging.name: Transesophageal Echocardiogram (TEE)
imaging.result: Better visualization of valve anatomy if TTE insufficient.
imaging.reasoning: May be needed for TAVR planning or if TTE images inadequate.

imaging.name: CT Angiography of Aorta
imaging.result: Pre-procedural imaging for TAVR planning.
imaging.reasoning: Required for TAVR to assess vascular access and aortic root anatomy.

imaging.name: CT Head (non-contrast)
imaging.result: No acute intracranial abnormality.
imaging.reasoning: Consider if concern for stroke/TIA with syncope, but cardiac etiology clear here.

procedures.name: Holter monitor (24-48 hour)
procedures.result: To be arranged - would evaluate for arrhythmia as contributor.
procedures.reasoning: Would evaluate for arrhythmia as contributor, though echo findings explain symptoms.

procedures.name: IV access
procedures.reasoning: For medication administration and emergency access.

procedures.name: Tilt table testing
procedures.result: NOT INDICATED - clear cardiac etiology for syncope.
procedures.reasoning: Syncope clearly explained by severe AS. Tilt table would be inappropriate.

treatments.name: Activity restriction - avoid strenuous exertion
treatments.result: Patient counseled to avoid strenuous activity until definitive treatment. No competitive sports or heavy lifting.
treatments.reasoning: Exertional syncope with severe AS carries sudden death risk. Must restrict activity until valve replacement.

treatments.name: Avoid vasodilators and volume depletion
treatments.result: Counseled to maintain hydration, avoid hot tubs, no nitrates, avoid new BP medications without cardiology input.
treatments.reasoning: Patients with severe AS are preload-dependent. Vasodilation can cause dangerous hypotension and syncope.

treatments.name: Beta-blocker (cautious)
treatments.result: Continue current metoprolol. Use cautiously - can worsen symptoms if excessive rate control.
treatments.reasoning: Beta-blockers may help rate control but must be used carefully in AS. Avoid over-treatment.

treatments.name: Diuretics
treatments.result: Use very cautiously if at all - patient is preload-dependent.
treatments.reasoning: AS patients need adequate preload. Diuretics can precipitate hypotension.

treatments.name: Nitroglycerin
treatments.result: AVOID - can cause severe hypotension.
treatments.reasoning: Nitrates cause vasodilation which is dangerous in severe AS. Contraindicated.

treatments.name: ACE inhibitor
treatments.result: Use cautiously if at all in severe AS.
treatments.reasoning: Traditionally avoided in severe AS due to afterload reduction, though some data suggests safety.

treatments.name: Endocarditis prophylaxis
treatments.result: No longer routinely recommended for native valve AS.
treatments.reasoning: Per AHA guidelines, prophylaxis not required for AS unless prior endocarditis or prosthetic valve.

consults.name: Cardiology consult - URGENT
consults.result: URGENT cardiology consult placed. Plan for surgical or transcatheter aortic valve replacement evaluation.
consults.reasoning: Symptomatic severe AS requires intervention. Without treatment, survival is 50% at 2 years with syncope.

consults.name: Cardiac surgery consultation
consults.result: To be arranged for valve replacement planning (SAVR vs TAVR).
consults.reasoning: Definitive treatment is aortic valve replacement. Surgical AVR for younger/lower risk, TAVR for older/higher risk.

consults.name: Cardiac anesthesia consultation
consults.result: Pre-operative evaluation for valve replacement.
consults.reasoning: Required before valve surgery for risk assessment and planning.

disposition.name: Admit to telemetry for monitoring and expedited workup
disposition.result: Admitted for echo confirmation and urgent cardiology evaluation.
disposition.reasoning: Exertional syncope with suspected severe AS warrants admission for expedited evaluation and monitoring.

disposition.name: Admit to CCU/ICU
disposition.result: Consider if hemodynamically unstable.
disposition.reasoning: Floor/telemetry usually sufficient if stable. ICU if recurrent syncope or hemodynamic concerns.

disposition.name: Discharge home with outpatient follow-up
disposition.result: INAPPROPRIATE - high-risk patient
disposition.reasoning: Symptomatic severe AS with syncope has poor short-term prognosis. Should not discharge without definitive intervention plan.

actualDiagnosis.name: Severe Symptomatic Aortic Stenosis (Bicuspid Aortic Valve)
keyFindings: Exertional syncope - the most ominous symptom of AS
Classic AS triad present: Angina (exertional chest tightness) + Syncope + Dyspnea on exertion
Crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur at RUSB radiating to carotids
Pulsus parvus et tardus (weak, delayed carotid upstroke)
Murmur decreases with Valsalva (confirms AS, not HOCM)
Known bicuspid valve since childhood - most common cause of AS in patients <65
Echo confirms severe AS: AVA 0.8 cm², mean gradient 48 mmHg
teachingPoints: EXERTIONAL SYNCOPE IS ALWAYS PATHOLOGIC: Never assume vasovagal in someone who passes out during exertion. This is cardiac until proven otherwise.
AORTIC STENOSIS TRIAD: Angina, Syncope, Dyspnea (CHF). Once symptoms appear, prognosis without treatment is 5 years (angina), 3 years (syncope), 2 years (CHF).
BICUSPID AORTIC VALVE: Present in 1-2% of population. Most common cause of AS in young adults. Can remain asymptomatic for decades then progress.
PULSUS PARVUS ET TARDUS: Pathognomonic for AS. The carotid pulse is weak (parvus) and slow-rising (tardus) because blood must slowly squeeze through the stenotic valve.
DYNAMIC MANEUVERS DIFFERENTIATE AS FROM HOCM: AS murmur decreases with decreased preload (Valsalva, standing). HOCM murmur increases. This is a crucial bedside test.
AVOID VASODILATORS IN SEVERE AS: These patients are preload-dependent. Nitrates, diuretics, and antihypertensives can cause dangerous hypotension.
SYMPTOMATIC AS REQUIRES INTERVENTION: Aortic valve replacement (surgical or TAVR) is the only definitive treatment. Medical therapy alone doesn't change outcomes.

guidelineReferences.name: ACC/AHA Guideline for Management of Valvular Heart Disease 2020
guidelineReferences.citation: Otto CM et al. J Am Coll Cardiol. 2021;77(4):e25-e197
keyPoints: Severe AS: AVA <1.0 cm², mean gradient ≥40 mmHg, or Vmax ≥4 m/s
Symptomatic severe AS (Class I): Intervention recommended
SAVR vs TAVR: Shared decision-making based on surgical risk
TAVR preferred in patients ≥65 with no anatomic contraindications
Avoid nitrates and vasodilators in severe AS

guidelineReferences.name: ESC/EACTS Guidelines for Valvular Heart Disease 2021
guidelineReferences.citation: Vahanian A et al. Eur Heart J. 2022;43(7):561-632
keyPoints: Exertional syncope is most ominous symptom of AS
Symptom onset predicts poor prognosis without intervention
Heart Team approach for intervention planning
Asymptomatic severe AS: Close monitoring or intervention if very severe
Bicuspid aortic valve: Most common cause of AS in <65 years

closureScript.diagnosis: Mr. Torres, we've found the reason you passed out. That heart murmur you've had since you were a child has become more serious. The valve that lets blood flow out of your heart has become very tight - we call this aortic stenosis. When you exercise, your heart can't pump enough blood through that tight valve, and that's why you fainted.
closureScript.plan: We're going to admit you to the hospital. You'll need to see a heart specialist and get a detailed ultrasound of your heart called an echocardiogram. Based on what we're seeing, you're likely going to need surgery or a procedure to fix or replace that valve. The good news is that once that's done, you should feel much better and be able to be active again.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: Until we get this fixed, you absolutely cannot do any strenuous exercise or heavy lifting. No running, no gym, no sports. Another fainting episode could be much more serious. If you feel any chest pain, get more short of breath, or feel like you might pass out again, you need to call 911 immediately.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: I know this is a lot to take in. You came in thinking this was no big deal, and now we're talking about heart surgery. But here's the key thing: we caught this. People with your condition who don't get treatment have serious risks. With treatment, you can get back to a normal, active life. The cardiologist will explain all your options.

patientBackground.recentEvents: Patient was in usual state of health until symptoms began. No major life changes recently.
patientBackground.recentIllnesses: Baseline chronic conditions as per medical history. No new acute illnesses.
patientBackground.contextualFactors: Dealing with typical life stressors. Concerned about current symptoms.
patientBackground.supportSystem: Has family and friends available for support. Not isolated.
patientBackground.healthBeliefs: Generally follows medical advice. Open to treatment recommendations.
patientBackground.previousERVisits: Occasional ER visits for minor issues. Hospitalizations as documented in history.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if it was triggered by blood draw?
whatIfScenarios.description: Patient fainted during routine blood draw with prodrome of nausea, warmth, and tunnel vision

chiefComplaint.original: I passed out during exercise
chiefComplaint.modified: I passed out when they were drawing my blood. I felt nauseous and sweaty beforehand.

feedback.correct: Classic vasovagal syncope: clear trigger + prodrome + quick recovery. Low risk if no cardiac red flags. Can often discharge with reassurance.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of vasovagal pattern. What features make this low-risk?
feedback.incorrect: Vasovagal syncope: emotional/painful trigger, prodrome (nausea, warmth, tunnel vision), quick recovery. This is BENIGN. Contrast with exertional syncope which is always pathologic.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Exertional syncope = always pathologic, cardiac until proven otherwise. Situational syncope with prodrome = usually vasovagal, benign. Key distinction for risk stratification.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if there was no warning at all?
whatIfScenarios.description: Patient was talking, then suddenly unconscious with no prodrome

chiefComplaint.original: I passed out during exercise
chiefComplaint.modified: I was just standing there talking and the next thing I knew I was on the floor. No warning at all.

feedback.correct: Syncope without warning = cardiac until proven otherwise. High risk for arrhythmia. Needs ECG, telemetry, likely admission. Consider event monitor.
feedback.partial: Good concern for cardiac cause. What's the implication of NO prodrome?
feedback.incorrect: No prodrome = cardiac syncope until proven otherwise. Arrhythmias (VT, heart block) cause sudden loss of cardiac output with no warning. High risk - needs ECG, monitor, possibly EP study.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Vasovagal = prodrome (warning symptoms). Cardiac = often no warning, just sudden unconsciousness. Absence of prodrome = high-risk feature requiring aggressive workup.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient felt racing heart before fainting?
whatIfScenarios.description: Felt heart racing extremely fast, then passed out

chiefComplaint.original: I passed out during exercise
chiefComplaint.modified: My heart started racing really fast out of nowhere, then I passed out

feedback.correct: Palpitations + syncope = tachyarrhythmia. VT is the most dangerous. Needs ECG (look for WPW, long QT), telemetry, likely EP evaluation.
feedback.partial: Good link between palpitations and cardiac cause. What arrhythmia is most concerning?
feedback.incorrect: Palpitations before syncope = arrhythmia-induced. Could be VT (most dangerous), SVT with rapid rate, or AF with WPW. ECG critical - look for pre-excitation, QT prolongation, Brugada pattern.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Syncope alone = broad differential. Palpitations + syncope = narrows to tachyarrhythmia. This guides workup toward rhythm monitoring and EP evaluation.

questions.stem: Based on the clinical presentation, which is the MOST appropriate next step?

options.text: Systematic history and focused physical examination
options.explanation: A targeted history and physical exam is the essential first step in clinical reasoning, guiding hypothesis generation and differential diagnosis.
Empiric treatment before adequate evaluation risks missing the correct diagnosis and may cause harm.
Diagnostic testing should be hypothesis-driven, not shotgun; targeted tests based on clinical reasoning yield better outcomes.
Consultation may be appropriate later but should follow initial clinical assessment and diagnostic reasoning.

questions.stem: Which cognitive bias poses the greatest risk in this clinical scenario?

options.explanation: Availability bias (overweighting recent experiences) is common but not the most frequent source of error in this scenario.
Anchoring (fixating on initial impression) is a risk but can be mitigated by systematic differential generation.
Premature closure — accepting a diagnosis before it is fully verified — is the single most common cognitive error in clinical reasoning across all settings.
Confirmation bias (seeking only supporting evidence) contributes to error but often follows from premature closure.

questions.stem: An ECG shows ST elevation in leads II, III, and aVF with reciprocal ST depression in I and aVL. Which coronary artery is most likely occluded?

options.explanation: ST elevation in inferior leads (II, III, aVF) with reciprocal changes in lateral leads (I, aVL) localizes to an inferior STEMI, most commonly caused by RCA occlusion (~85% of cases). Check right-sided leads (V4R) to assess for RV involvement.
LAD occlusion causes anterior STEMI with ST elevation in V1-V4 (± V5-V6), not inferior leads.
LCx causes lateral STEMI (I, aVL, V5-V6). While LCx can cause inferior changes in ~15% of cases (dominant LCx), RCA is far more common.
Left main occlusion causes widespread ST depression with ST elevation in aVR — a pattern of diffuse subendocardial ischemia, not isolated inferior STEMI.

guidelineReferences.name: 2022 AHA/ACC/HFSA Guideline for Heart Failure
guidelineReferences.citation: Heidenreich PA, et al. Circulation. 2022;145:e895-e1032
keyPoints: Assess volume status and congestion
IV diuretics for acute decompensation
Identify and treat precipitating factors
Optimize GDMT before discharge

female-58-chf.name: S. Martin
female-58-chf.chiefComplaint: I get short of breath really easily lately

missedConsequences.CHF Exacerbation (Systolic Heart Failure: Without diuresis and CHF management, patient develops flash pulmonary edema overnight. Requires emergent intubation and ICU admission. Prolonged hospitalization.
missedConsequences.Acute Myocardial Infarction: Missed NSTEMI as trigger for CHF exacerbation. Patient progresses to cardiogenic shock. Requires emergent cath but significant myocardial damage already done.
missedConsequences.Pulmonary Embolism: PE missed because symptoms attributed to CHF. Anticoagulation delayed. Patient develops hemodynamic collapse from propagating clot.

commonPitfalls: Missing medication non-adherence as the trigger - ALWAYS ask about adherence in CHF exacerbations
Forgetting that NSAIDs (ibuprofen) worsen heart failure by causing fluid retention
Not considering PE just because patient has known CHF - recent flight + HRT = VTE risk factors
Forgetting that ACS can trigger CHF decompensation - always check for ischemia
Not examining JVP - elevated JVP is the most specific physical finding for CHF
Missing the S3 gallop - pathognomonic for systolic dysfunction but easy to miss with tachycardia

mcqs.question: The patient ran out of her furosemide one week ago. She also takes ibuprofen for knee pain. Both factors contribute to CHF exacerbation. How do NSAIDs worsen heart failure?

options.explanation: NSAIDs are not directly toxic to cardiac myocytes.

options.text: NSAIDs cause sodium and water retention by inhibiting renal prostaglandins
options.explanation: NSAIDs block prostaglandins that normally promote natriuresis. This causes sodium/water retention, increased preload, and worsened congestion. Also reduces effectiveness of diuretics.
NSAIDs don't directly affect heart rate.
NSAIDs actually tend to increase blood pressure, not lower it.

mcqs.question: You hear an S3 gallop on cardiac exam. What does an S3 indicate in this 58-year-old with known CHF?

options.text: Normal finding in adults - can be ignored
options.explanation: S3 is normal in young people but pathologic in adults over 40.
That describes S4, not S3.

options.text: Indicates volume-overloaded, dilated ventricle with systolic dysfunction
options.explanation: S3 occurs during rapid ventricular filling when blood hits a volume-overloaded, dilated ventricle. Pathognomonic for systolic heart failure in adults.
S3 can occur with mitral regurgitation but is more broadly a sign of LV dysfunction/volume overload.

allDiagnoses.name: CHF Exacerbation (Systolic Heart Failure)
allDiagnoses.rationale: Prior MI, orthopnea, PND, ankle edema, elevated BP, and ran out of diuretic. Classic presentation of decompensated heart failure.

allDiagnoses.name: CHF (Diastolic/HFpEF)
allDiagnoses.rationale: HTN, diabetes, and obesity are risk factors for HFpEF. Would need echo to differentiate from systolic failure.

allDiagnoses.name: Acute Myocardial Infarction
allDiagnoses.rationale: History of prior MI. Exertional chest pressure present. Could be ACS precipitating CHF exacerbation.

allDiagnoses.name: Pulmonary Embolism
allDiagnoses.rationale: Recent 5-hour flight + HRT (estrogen) = PE risk factors. Must consider even with CHF history.

allDiagnoses.name: Severe Valvular Disease
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can cause or worsen heart failure. Would need echo to evaluate.

allDiagnoses.name: COPD Exacerbation
allDiagnoses.rationale: 25 pack-year smoking history is a risk factor. But orthopnea/PND point more to cardiac cause.

allDiagnoses.name: Anemia
allDiagnoses.rationale: Ibuprofen use could cause GI bleeding. Pallor noted. Would worsen CHF symptoms.

allDiagnoses.name: Pneumonia
allDiagnoses.rationale: No fever, cough, or sputum production.

female-58-chf.oldcartsNarrative: "About three weeks ago I started getting more winded than usual. I used to walk my dog around the block no problem, but now I have to stop once or twice to catch my breath. Even walking from the parking lot makes me winded now. At night I need two pillows or I can't breathe, and twice this week I woke up gasping and had to sit up. My ankles have been swelling, especially by evening. I also get this tight feeling in my chest when I walk uphill - goes away when I stop. I'm feeling really tired and a little lightheaded. I ran out of my water pill last week and haven't refilled it. I had a heart attack about 6 years ago and they told me I had mild heart failure after. I take ibuprofen for my knee pain a few times a week. Oh, and I flew home from visiting my daughter 3 weeks ago - about a 5-hour flight. I also recently started hormone therapy for menopause."

historyQuestions.text: When did this shortness of breath start?
historyQuestions.answer: About three weeks ago. It's been gradually getting worse. At first I just noticed I was more winded walking, but now even small activities make me short of breath.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Subacute onset over 3 weeks is typical for CHF decompensation; correlates with missed diuretic and travel history

historyQuestions.text: Do you need extra pillows to sleep? How many?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, I need two pillows now or I feel like I'm suffocating lying flat. I never needed that before a few weeks ago.
historyQuestions.reasoning: New orthopnea is a classic symptom of CHF exacerbation. Indicates pulmonary congestion when supine.

historyQuestions.text: Do you ever wake up suddenly at night short of breath?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes! Twice this week I woke up gasping, like I couldn't get air. I had to sit up on the edge of the bed for 15-20 minutes before it got better.
historyQuestions.reasoning: PND (paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea) is highly specific for CHF. Caused by fluid redistribution when supine.

historyQuestions.text: Have you noticed any swelling in your legs or feet?
historyQuestions.answer: My ankles are puffy, especially by evening. My shoes feel tighter. It's been getting worse over the past week.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Peripheral edema indicates right heart failure/fluid overload. Worsening pattern suggests decompensation.

historyQuestions.text: Are you taking all your medications regularly?
historyQuestions.answer: I ran out of my water pill - furosemide - about a week ago and haven't had a chance to get it refilled. The pharmacy said they needed a new prescription.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CRITICAL: Diuretic non-adherence is the #1 cause of CHF exacerbation. Missing furosemide leads to fluid accumulation.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me more about the chest tightness.
historyQuestions.answer: It's more like pressure than pain. Happens when I'm walking, especially uphill. Goes away after a few minutes of rest. No pain at rest.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Exertional chest pressure in patient with prior MI could indicate ongoing ischemia. May be anginal equivalent or demand ischemia from CHF.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your heart attack.
historyQuestions.answer: It was 6 years ago. I had stents put in. A few months later they told me my heart was a little weak - mild heart failure they said. I've been on medications since then.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prior MI with reduced EF is classic setup for ischemic cardiomyopathy and CHF.

historyQuestions.text: You mentioned a flight - tell me about that.
historyQuestions.answer: I flew to visit my daughter about 3 weeks ago. It was about 5 hours each way. I did get up and walk around a little.
historyQuestions.reasoning: 5-hour flight is VTE risk factor. Timing correlates with symptom onset. Must consider PE.

historyQuestions.text: You mentioned hormone therapy - what are you taking?
historyQuestions.answer: I just started estrogen patches about 2 months ago for hot flashes and night sweats. My gynecologist prescribed them.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Estrogen therapy increases VTE risk. Combined with recent travel, raises PE concern.

historyQuestions.text: Any pain or swelling in just one leg? Calf pain?
historyQuestions.answer: No, the swelling is in both ankles equally. No calf pain.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Bilateral symmetric edema is more consistent with CHF. Unilateral would raise DVT concern.

historyQuestions.text: Any cough?
historyQuestions.answer: No cough.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No cough makes pneumonia and COPD exacerbation less likely.

historyQuestions.text: How often do you take ibuprofen?
historyQuestions.answer: Probably 3-4 times a week for my knee arthritis.
historyQuestions.reasoning: NSAIDs cause fluid retention and can worsen CHF. Also risk for GI bleeding.

historyQuestions.text: Do you or did you smoke?
historyQuestions.answer: I quit 5 years ago but I smoked for about 25 years before that - about half a pack a day.
historyQuestions.reasoning: 25 pack-year history is significant. COPD should be considered but presentation more classic for CHF.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No known drug allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical for safe prescribing.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

housing_home_setup.answer: Two-story house. Bedroom's upstairs but I've been sleeping on the couch because I can't make it up.
housing_home_setup.relevance: Inability to climb stairs indicates significant functional limitation from CHF; home setup affects ability to manage chronic disease.

housing_living_situation.answer: Live with my husband. He helps, but he's got his own health problems.
housing_living_situation.relevance: Social support affects medication adherence and ability to follow up; caregiver burden can affect outcomes.

diet_sodium_intake.answer: I try to watch it but it's hard. I like my soups and processed foods are cheaper.
diet_sodium_intake.relevance: High sodium intake is major driver of CHF exacerbations. Dietary counseling is essential for management.

diet_fluid_intake.answer: I drink a lot of water. My doctor said stay hydrated, so I drink maybe 8-10 glasses a day.
diet_fluid_intake.relevance: Excessive fluid intake can worsen CHF. Many patients receive conflicting advice about hydration.

social_healthcare_access.answer: I have Medicare but the copays add up. Sometimes I skip refills to save money.
social_healthcare_access.relevance: Cost-related medication non-adherence is common cause of CHF exacerbations. Social work referral needed.

social_medication_management.answer: I take a lot of pills. Sometimes I forget which ones I've taken. It gets confusing.
social_medication_management.relevance: Polypharmacy and confusion about medications leads to non-adherence - major factor in CHF exacerbations.

occupation_activity_level.answer: I'm retired. Used to be more active but now I barely leave the house. Too tired.
occupation_activity_level.relevance: Deconditioning worsens functional status and CHF symptoms; cardiac rehab may help.

housing_temperature_control.answer: House gets hot in summer. We have AC but try not to run it too much - expensive.
housing_temperature_control.relevance: Heat exposure can stress cardiovascular system and worsen CHF symptoms.

physicalExam.finding: BP 148/90, HR 94, RR 22, Temp 98.4°F, SpO2 94% on RA. Elevated BP, mild tachycardia, tachypnea, borderline hypoxia.
physicalExam.reasoning: Tachypnea and borderline hypoxia indicate respiratory compromise. Elevated BP may be contributing to decompensation.

physicalExam.finding: Obese female, mild respiratory distress, speaks in full sentences but appears fatigued. Slightly pale.
physicalExam.reasoning: Can still speak in sentences - not severe respiratory failure. Pallor may indicate anemia.

physicalExam.finding: JVP elevated to 12 cm H2O (normal <8). Positive hepatojugular reflux.
physicalExam.reasoning: CRITICAL FINDING: Elevated JVP indicates right heart pressure/volume overload. Very specific for CHF.

physicalExam.finding: Regular rhythm, tachycardic. S1, S2 present. S3 gallop heard at apex. Soft systolic murmur at apex (MR). No rubs.
physicalExam.reasoning: S3 GALLOP is pathognomonic for systolic heart failure (ventricular dysfunction). MR murmur often accompanies dilated LV.

physicalExam.finding: PMI displaced laterally to anterior axillary line (normally midclavicular). Diffuse and sustained.
physicalExam.reasoning: Laterally displaced PMI indicates cardiomegaly from chronic LV dilation.

physicalExam.finding: Bibasilar crackles (rales) extending to mid-lung fields bilaterally. No wheezes.
physicalExam.reasoning: BIBASILAR CRACKLES indicate pulmonary edema. Classic finding in decompensated CHF.

physicalExam.finding: 2+ pitting edema bilateral ankles extending to mid-shin. No calf tenderness or asymmetry. No Homan's sign.
physicalExam.reasoning: Bilateral symmetric edema consistent with CHF. No DVT signs, though doesn't rule out PE.

physicalExam.finding: Soft, non-tender. Liver palpable 2cm below costal margin. Mild hepatomegaly. No ascites.
physicalExam.reasoning: Hepatomegaly from hepatic congestion supports right heart failure.

physicalExam.finding: Conjunctival pallor present. Pale nail beds.
physicalExam.reasoning: Pallor suggests anemia, which could be exacerbating CHF symptoms.

labs.name: BNP or NT-proBNP
labs.reasoning: DIAGNOSTIC for CHF. BNP >400 strongly suggests acute heart failure. Very useful for distinguishing cardiac vs pulmonary dyspnea.

labs.name: Troponin
labs.reasoning: Mild elevation common in CHF ('demand ischemia'). Not high enough for acute MI but indicates myocardial strain.

labs.name: CBC
labs.reasoning: Microcytic anemia present. Likely iron deficiency from chronic NSAID use. Anemia worsens CHF symptoms.

labs.name: BMP
labs.result: Na 138, K 3.8, Cl 98, CO2 22, BUN 32, Cr 1.4 (slightly elevated), Glucose 145
labs.reasoning: Mild prerenal azotemia (elevated BUN/Cr) from poor cardiac output. Electrolytes important for diuretic dosing.

labs.name: ECG
labs.result: Sinus tachycardia at 94. Old Q waves in V1-V3 (prior anterior MI). LVH by voltage. No acute ST changes.
labs.reasoning: Confirms prior MI. No acute ischemia currently. Rules out arrhythmia as precipitant.

labs.name: TSH
labs.reasoning: Thyroid dysfunction can cause or worsen CHF. Hyperthyroidism causes high-output failure.

labs.name: Iron Studies (Fe, TIBC, Ferritin)
labs.reasoning: Confirms iron deficiency anemia. IV iron may help if ferritin <100 in CHF patients.

labs.name: Lactate
labs.reasoning: Normal lactate indicates adequate tissue perfusion. Elevated would suggest cardiogenic shock.

labs.name: Liver Function Tests
labs.result: AST 45, ALT 38, Alk Phos 92, T.Bili 1.2 - mildly elevated
labs.reasoning: Mild elevations from hepatic congestion. Markedly elevated would suggest cardiogenic shock.

labs.name: PT/INR, PTT
labs.reasoning: Baseline coagulation. Important if anticoagulation considered for AF or LV thrombus.

labs.name: D-dimer
labs.reasoning: Often elevated in CHF nonspecifically. Would pursue CTA only if high clinical suspicion for PE.

labs.name: Procalcitonin
labs.reasoning: Helps distinguish CHF from pneumonia. Low value argues against infection.

labs.name: Hemoglobin A1c
labs.reasoning: Documents diabetes control. Not urgent but part of CHF workup.

labs.name: Lipid Panel
labs.reasoning: Not urgent in acute setting. Can check as outpatient.

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.result: Cardiomegaly. Bilateral pulmonary vascular congestion. Small bilateral pleural effusions. Kerley B lines present. No consolidation.
imaging.reasoning: Classic CHF findings: cardiomegaly, pulmonary edema, pleural effusions, Kerley B lines (interstitial edema).

imaging.name: Echocardiogram
imaging.result: EF 30% (reduced; normal >55%). Dilated LV. Moderate MR. No pericardial effusion. Diastolic dysfunction grade II.
imaging.reasoning: Confirms systolic heart failure with reduced EF. Guides treatment (GDMT) and prognosis.

imaging.name: Bedside Lung Ultrasound (POCUS)
imaging.result: Bilateral B-lines consistent with pulmonary edema. Small bilateral pleural effusions.
imaging.reasoning: Rapid bedside confirmation of pulmonary edema. Can monitor response to diuresis.

imaging.name: CT Angiography Chest (PE protocol)
imaging.result: Not routinely indicated unless high suspicion for PE.
imaging.reasoning: Consider if Wells score elevated or clinical concern for PE. This patient has clear CHF precipitant.

imaging.name: CT Chest (non-contrast)
imaging.result: Not indicated - CXR sufficient.
imaging.reasoning: CXR adequate for CHF diagnosis. CT adds radiation without benefit.

procedures.name: Foley catheter
procedures.reasoning: Monitors urine output during diuresis. Goal 1-2 L/day negative.

procedures.name: Thoracentesis
procedures.result: Not indicated - effusions small and will respond to diuresis.
procedures.reasoning: Reserve for large effusions causing respiratory distress or if concern for empyema.

treatments.name: IV Furosemide (Lasix)
treatments.result: Furosemide 40mg IV given. Urine output 800mL in first 2 hours.
treatments.reasoning: FIRST-LINE treatment for acute decompensated CHF. IV diuresis to relieve pulmonary congestion. Use 1-2x home oral dose as IV.

treatments.name: Supplemental oxygen
treatments.result: 2L NC applied, SpO2 improved to 97%.
treatments.reasoning: Indicated for hypoxia. Improves oxygenation while treating underlying cause.

treatments.name: Discontinue NSAIDs
treatments.result: Ibuprofen stopped. Counseled on alternative pain management.
treatments.reasoning: NSAIDs cause fluid retention and worsen CHF. Must be stopped permanently.

treatments.name: Fluid restriction (1.5-2L/day)
treatments.result: Patient counseled on fluid restriction.
treatments.reasoning: Reduces fluid intake to help achieve net negative balance.

treatments.name: Low sodium diet (<2g/day)
treatments.result: Dietary counseling and nutrition consult placed.
treatments.reasoning: Reduces fluid retention. Essential for long-term CHF management.

treatments.name: Daily weights and strict I/Os
treatments.result: Daily weight monitoring initiated. Goal 1-2 kg/day fluid loss.
treatments.reasoning: Best way to monitor diuresis. Weight more reliable than I/O charting.

treatments.name: BiPAP (if severe respiratory distress)
treatments.result: Not needed currently - patient responding to oxygen.
treatments.reasoning: For severe pulmonary edema. Reduces preload and work of breathing.

treatments.name: Nitroglycerin drip (if hypertensive)
treatments.result: Not started - BP acceptable after diuresis.
treatments.reasoning: Reduces preload/afterload in hypertensive CHF. Use if BP >140 systolic.

treatments.name: Morphine
treatments.result: Not routinely used - may worsen outcomes.
treatments.reasoning: Previously used but NOW DISCOURAGED. Associated with increased mortality in acute CHF.

treatments.name: Hold beta-blocker during acute phase
treatments.result: Metoprolol held during acute decompensation. Restart before discharge.
treatments.reasoning: Hold during acute decompensation, but do not stop permanently. Restart when euvolemic.

treatments.name: Continue ACE-I/ARB
treatments.result: Lisinopril continued.
treatments.reasoning: Generally continue unless hypotensive or significant renal dysfunction.

treatments.name: IV fluid bolus
treatments.result: CONTRAINDICATED - patient is volume overloaded.
treatments.reasoning: HARMFUL: Patient has volume OVERLOAD. IV fluids would worsen pulmonary edema. This is the opposite of what's needed.

treatments.name: Digoxin loading dose
treatments.result: Not indicated for acute decompensated CHF.
treatments.reasoning: Digoxin has limited role in modern CHF management. Does not improve mortality. Risk of toxicity.

treatments.name: Dobutamine drip
treatments.result: Not indicated - patient is 'warm and wet' not 'cold and wet'.
treatments.reasoning: Inotropes reserved for cardiogenic shock ('cold and wet'). This patient has adequate perfusion.

treatments.name: Milrinone drip
treatments.result: Not indicated - patient is adequately perfusing.
treatments.reasoning: Inotropes for low-output states only. Associated with increased arrhythmias.

treatments.name: Calcium channel blocker (diltiazem/verapamil)
treatments.result: CONTRAINDICATED in systolic heart failure.
treatments.reasoning: HARMFUL: Non-dihydropyridine CCBs (diltiazem, verapamil) have negative inotropic effects and worsen systolic CHF.

treatments.name: NSAID for cardiac inflammation
treatments.result: CONTRAINDICATED in CHF.
treatments.reasoning: HARMFUL: NSAIDs cause sodium/fluid retention and worsen CHF. Never give NSAIDs to CHF patients.

treatments.name: Empiric antibiotics for pneumonia
treatments.result: Not indicated - no evidence of pneumonia.
treatments.reasoning: CXR shows pulmonary edema, not pneumonia. Crackles are from CHF, not infection. No fever.

treatments.name: Albuterol nebulizer
treatments.result: Not indicated - no wheezing or bronchospasm.
treatments.reasoning: Patient has CHF, not asthma/COPD. Albuterol can worsen tachycardia. Treat the underlying CHF.

treatments.name: IV steroids
treatments.result: Not indicated for CHF.
treatments.reasoning: Steroids cause fluid retention. No role in acute CHF management.

treatments.name: Therapeutic anticoagulation
treatments.result: Not indicated unless specific indication (AF, LV thrombus).
treatments.reasoning: Patient is in sinus rhythm. Prophylactic anticoagulation not proven beneficial in CHF without AF.

consults.name: Cardiology consult
consults.result: Cardiology consulted for CHF optimization and possible cath.
consults.reasoning: Should optimize GDMT. May need coronary evaluation given ischemic cardiomyopathy.

consults.name: Social work consult
consults.result: SW consulted for medication assistance programs.
consults.reasoning: Patient had medication non-adherence due to cost. Address barriers to adherence.

consults.name: Nutrition/Dietitian consult
consults.result: Dietitian consulted for low-sodium diet education.
consults.reasoning: Dietary counseling essential for CHF self-management.

consults.name: Clinical pharmacist review
consults.result: Medication reconciliation and education.
consults.reasoning: Optimize medications, provide education, simplify regimen if possible.

disposition.name: Admit to telemetry for diuresis
disposition.result: Admitted for IV diuresis, monitoring, and GDMT optimization.
disposition.reasoning: Acute decompensated CHF requires admission for IV diuresis and monitoring.

disposition.name: Admit to ICU/CCU
disposition.result: Consider if requiring BiPAP, inotropes, or hemodynamically unstable.
disposition.reasoning: This patient is 'warm and wet' - floor appropriate. ICU for 'cold and wet' or respiratory failure.

disposition.name: Discharge with oral diuretics
disposition.result: Patient too symptomatic for outpatient management.
disposition.reasoning: Patient has hypoxia, significant pulmonary edema. Needs IV diuresis and monitoring.

actualDiagnosis.name: Acute Decompensated Heart Failure (CHF Exacerbation) - Systolic HF from Ischemic Cardiomyopathy
keyFindings: Classic CHF symptoms: dyspnea on exertion, orthopnea (2 pillows), PND, bilateral edema
Precipitant identified: ran out of furosemide (diuretic non-adherence)
Additional contributor: chronic NSAID use causing fluid retention
Physical exam: JVP elevated, S3 gallop, bibasilar crackles, bilateral pitting edema
BNP markedly elevated (1,450) confirming cardiac cause of dyspnea
CXR: cardiomegaly, pulmonary edema, Kerley B lines, pleural effusions
Echo: EF 30%, dilated LV, moderate MR (ischemic cardiomyopathy from prior MI)
teachingPoints: MEDICATION NON-ADHERENCE is the #1 cause of CHF exacerbation. Always ask about medications, especially diuretics.
S3 GALLOP is pathognomonic for systolic heart failure. It's the sound of blood sloshing into a dilated, non-compliant ventricle.
JVP ELEVATION is the most specific physical exam finding for CHF. Learn to measure it accurately.
BNP is extremely helpful: <100 essentially rules out CHF, >400 strongly suggests it. Useful when diagnosis is unclear.
NSAIDs WORSEN CHF: They cause sodium retention and reduce effectiveness of diuretics and ACE inhibitors. Avoid in all CHF patients.
DON'T FORGET PE: Recent travel + estrogen therapy are risk factors. CHF and PE can coexist. Keep it on your differential.
CHF IS A CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS: Orthopnea + PND + JVD + S3 + crackles + edema = CHF until proven otherwise.

guidelineReferences.name: ACC/AHA/HFSA Guideline for Management of Heart Failure 2022
guidelineReferences.citation: Heidenreich PA et al. Circulation. 2022;145(18):e895-e1032
keyPoints: IV loop diuretics are first-line for acute decompensated HF
GDMT for HFrEF: ACEi/ARB/ARNI + beta-blocker + MRA + SGLT2i (4 pillars)
Avoid NSAIDs in all heart failure patients
Daily weights and sodium restriction (<2g/day) are essential
S3 gallop and elevated JVP are specific for volume overload

guidelineReferences.name: ESC Guidelines for Heart Failure 2021
guidelineReferences.citation: McDonagh TA et al. Eur Heart J. 2021;42(36):3599-3726
keyPoints: BNP <100 or NT-proBNP <300 essentially rules out HF
Diuretic goal: 1-2 kg/day weight loss until euvolemic
Hold beta-blocker during acute decompensation; restart before discharge
Identify and treat precipitants: medication non-adherence, ischemia, arrhythmia, infection
SGLT2 inhibitors (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin) reduce hospitalizations in HFrEF

closureScript.diagnosis: Mrs. Martin, your heart is struggling to pump effectively, and fluid has been building up in your lungs and legs. This is a flare-up of your heart condition. The main reason it happened is that you ran out of your water pill - that medication is really important for keeping fluid from building up.
closureScript.plan: We're going to admit you to the hospital for a few days. We'll give you IV diuretics to help your body get rid of the extra fluid, and you'll feel a lot better once that fluid is gone. We'll restart your medications and make sure you have enough to go home with. We'll also check your heart with an ultrasound.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: While you're here, tell us if your breathing gets worse, if you have any chest pain, or if you feel like your heart is racing. When you go home, you need to weigh yourself every day - if you gain more than 3 pounds in a day or 5 pounds in a week, that means fluid is building up and you should call your doctor right away.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: I know it's frustrating when the pharmacy needs a new prescription. Let's make sure before you leave that you have enough medication and that there's a system to prevent this from happening again. Also, I noticed you take ibuprofen for your knee - we need to find you a different pain medication, because ibuprofen actually makes heart failure worse.
