whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient was hypotensive with cool extremities?
whatIfScenarios.description: Same CHF but BP 82/54, cool mottled extremities, altered mental status

chiefComplaint.original: I can't breathe when I lie down
chiefComplaint.modified: I can't breathe, I feel terrible, and my hands and feet are cold

feedback.correct: 'Cold and wet' = cardiogenic shock. Can't just diurese - need inotropes (dobutamine, milrinone) and/or pressors. Consider mechanical support (IABP, Impella). Emergent cardiology, ICU. Rule out acute MI as cause.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of shock. How does treatment differ from 'warm and wet'?
feedback.incorrect: CHF profiles: 'Warm and wet' (perfused but congested) = diuretics. 'Cold and wet' (hypoperfused AND congested) = CARDIOGENIC SHOCK. Needs ICU, inotropes, consider mechanical support. Diuretics alone will worsen hypoperfusion.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: 'Warm and wet' = good perfusion, just congested, diurese on floor. 'Cold and wet' = shock, needs inotropes, ICU. 'Cold and dry' = needs fluids carefully. The perfusion status changes everything.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if there was a new harsh systolic murmur?
whatIfScenarios.description: Sudden decompensation with new harsh holosystolic murmur after recent MI

chiefComplaint.original: I can't breathe when I lie down
chiefComplaint.modified: I had a heart attack last week, now suddenly I can't breathe at all

expectedReasoning.topDiagnosis: Acute mechanical complication of MI (VSD or papillary muscle rupture)

feedback.correct: New murmur after MI = mechanical complication until proven otherwise. VSD or papillary muscle rupture (acute MR). SURGICAL EMERGENCY. Emergent echo, cardiothoracic surgery consult, IABP, may need emergent OR.
feedback.partial: Good concern for mechanical complication. What's the urgency level?
feedback.incorrect: Mechanical complications of MI (days 3-7 typically): VSD, papillary muscle rupture (acute MR), free wall rupture. New harsh murmur + shock = surgical emergency. Echo confirms. IABP as bridge to surgery. High mortality without intervention.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Chronic MR murmur = gradual, known, stable. NEW murmur after MI = mechanical complication, surgical emergency. The timing and acuity of the murmur are critical.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the EF was normal (HFpEF)?
whatIfScenarios.description: Same CHF presentation but echo shows EF 60% with diastolic dysfunction

chiefComplaint.original: I can't breathe when I lie down
chiefComplaint.modified: Same presentation but echo shows EF 60%, diastolic dysfunction, LVH

expectedReasoning.topDiagnosis: Heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF)

feedback.correct: HFpEF = heart failure with normal EF. Stiff ventricle can't relax/fill properly. Treatment: diuretics for congestion, BP control, treat afib if present. Limited evidence for neurohormonal agents (unlike HFrEF). Often elderly, female, HTN, DM, obesity.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of HFpEF. How does treatment differ from HFrEF?
feedback.incorrect: HFpEF (EF ≥50%) = diastolic failure. Ventricle is stiff, can't fill. Treatment: diuretics (for symptoms), BP control, rate control if afib. SGLT2 inhibitors now have evidence. Unlike HFrEF, limited evidence for beta-blockers/ACEi specifically for HFpEF.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: HFrEF (EF <40%) = systolic failure, strong evidence for beta-blockers, ACEi/ARB/ARNI, MRAs. HFpEF (EF ≥50%) = diastolic failure, treat symptoms + comorbidities, SGLT2i have recent evidence. Same symptoms, different pathophysiology.

questions.stem: Based on the clinical presentation, which is the MOST appropriate next step?

options.text: Systematic history and focused physical examination
options.explanation: A targeted history and physical exam is the essential first step in clinical reasoning, guiding hypothesis generation and differential diagnosis.
Empiric treatment before adequate evaluation risks missing the correct diagnosis and may cause harm.
Diagnostic testing should be hypothesis-driven, not shotgun; targeted tests based on clinical reasoning yield better outcomes.
Consultation may be appropriate later but should follow initial clinical assessment and diagnostic reasoning.

questions.stem: Which cognitive bias poses the greatest risk in this clinical scenario?

options.explanation: Availability bias (overweighting recent experiences) is common but not the most frequent source of error in this scenario.
Anchoring (fixating on initial impression) is a risk but can be mitigated by systematic differential generation.
Premature closure — accepting a diagnosis before it is fully verified — is the single most common cognitive error in clinical reasoning across all settings.
Confirmation bias (seeking only supporting evidence) contributes to error but often follows from premature closure.

guidelineReferences.name: 2017 ACC/AHA Hypertension Guidelines
guidelineReferences.citation: Whelton PK, et al. J Am Coll Cardiol. 2018;71:e127-e248
keyPoints: Stage 2 HTN: BP ≥140/90 mmHg
Initial workup: CBC, CMP, lipid panel, TSH, urinalysis, ECG
Target organ damage assessment: LVH, retinopathy, nephropathy
Lifestyle modifications: DASH diet, sodium restriction, exercise, weight loss

guidelineReferences.name: ADA Standards of Care 2026 - Section 10: Hypertension Management
guidelineReferences.citation: American Diabetes Association. Diabetes Care. 2026;49(Suppl 1):S216-S245
keyPoints: BP target <130/80 mmHg for patients with diabetes and hypertension
ACE inhibitor or ARB preferred in diabetes, especially with albuminuria
Combination therapy often required to achieve BP goals

male-42-essential.name: R. Chen
male-42-essential.chiefComplaint: I was told my blood pressure was high at a health fair
male-42-essential.variantDescription: Essential HTN with target organ damage (LVH)

missedConsequences.Essential (Primary) Hypertension: Patient told 'just lose some weight' without starting treatment. Continues with untreated HTN. Presents 2 years later with hemorrhagic stroke and permanent disability.
missedConsequences.Pheochromocytoma: Undiagnosed pheo leads to hypertensive crisis during surgery for unrelated condition. Intraoperative cardiovascular collapse.
missedConsequences.Renal Artery Stenosis: Progressive renal artery stenosis leads to ischemic nephropathy and renal failure.

commonPitfalls: Dismissing 'white coat hypertension' without confirming with home or ambulatory BP monitoring
Not screening for secondary causes in anyone <30 or >55 at diagnosis, or with resistant/severe HTN
Forgetting to check BP in BOTH arms at initial diagnosis - asymmetry suggests coarctation or vascular disease
Missing hypokalemia on labs which suggests primary aldosteronism
Not looking for target organ damage (LVH, retinopathy, nephropathy) which changes management
Not counseling on lifestyle modifications which are first-line treatment

mcqs.question: This 42-year-old has newly discovered HTN with Stage 2 readings. Which of the following should make you suspect SECONDARY hypertension?

options.explanation: Family history supports PRIMARY (essential) hypertension, not secondary.
These are risk factors for essential hypertension.

options.text: Age <30 or >55 at diagnosis, resistant HTN, or hypokalemia
options.explanation: Secondary HTN red flags include: young age (<30) or older age (>55) at diagnosis, severe/resistant HTN, hypokalemia (suggests aldosteronism), abdominal bruit (RAS), episodic spells (pheo).
High sodium is a risk factor for essential hypertension.

mcqs.question: You hear an S4 gallop on cardiac exam. What does this suggest, and why is it important?

options.text: S4 is normal in adults - no clinical significance
options.explanation: S4 is NOT normal. It indicates stiff, hypertrophied ventricle.

options.text: S4 suggests left ventricular hypertrophy - evidence of target organ damage
options.explanation: S4 occurs when atria contract against a stiff, non-compliant ventricle (LVH). Finding target organ damage (LVH, retinopathy, nephropathy) indicates longstanding/severe HTN and influences treatment intensity.

options.text: S4 indicates heart failure - patient needs urgent admission
options.explanation: S4 indicates diastolic dysfunction from LVH, not necessarily heart failure. S3 is more concerning for systolic failure.

options.text: S4 suggests valvular disease - needs echo first
options.explanation: S4 is related to ventricular compliance, not valvular disease.

allDiagnoses.name: Essential (Primary) Hypertension
allDiagnoses.rationale: Most common cause of HTN (>90%). Age 42, family history, obesity, high-sodium diet, sedentary lifestyle all risk factors. Gradual onset without secondary symptoms.

allDiagnoses.name: Pheochromocytoma
allDiagnoses.rationale: Rare but dangerous catecholamine-secreting tumor. Look for episodic headaches, sweating, palpitations - the 'spells.'

allDiagnoses.name: Renal Artery Stenosis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Important secondary cause. Look for abdominal bruit, renal dysfunction, resistant HTN.

allDiagnoses.name: Primary Aldosteronism
allDiagnoses.rationale: More common than previously thought. Look for hypokalemia, resistant HTN.

allDiagnoses.name: Coarctation of the Aorta
allDiagnoses.rationale: Congenital narrowing of aorta. Look for BP difference between arms, weak femoral pulses, radial-femoral delay.

allDiagnoses.name: Hyperthyroidism
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can cause systolic HTN. Would expect weight loss, tremor, heat intolerance.

allDiagnoses.name: Obstructive Sleep Apnea
allDiagnoses.rationale: Common contributor to HTN. Would expect snoring, daytime somnolence.

allDiagnoses.name: Cushing's Syndrome
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would expect weight gain, striae, moon facies.

allDiagnoses.name: Medication-Induced HTN
allDiagnoses.rationale: NSAIDs, decongestants, stimulants can raise BP. No such medications reported.

male-42-essential.oldcartsNarrative: "I went to a health fair at work last week and they said my blood pressure was 158/96. They checked it twice and said it was high both times. I've never been told I had high blood pressure before, but I admit I haven't seen a doctor in years. I've gained about 30 pounds since college. I probably eat too much salt - lots of processed foods and eating out. My dad has high blood pressure and my mom had a stroke in her 60s. I sit at a desk all day and don't really exercise. I don't smoke anymore - quit 5 years ago. I have maybe 2-3 beers on weekends."

historyQuestions.text: When did you first learn your blood pressure was high?
historyQuestions.answer: Just last week at a health fair at work. They checked it twice and it was high both times - something like 158/96. I've never been told it was high before, but I haven't seen a doctor in years.
historyQuestions.reasoning: New diagnosis, though likely undetected for years given risk factors and lack of medical care

historyQuestions.text: Have you noticed any symptoms - headaches, vision changes, chest pain, shortness of breath?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I really feel fine. That's why I was surprised about the blood pressure.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Asymptomatic HTN is typical of essential HTN. Pheochromocytoma would cause episodic symptoms.

historyQuestions.text: Do you ever have episodes of sudden headache, sweating, or racing heart that come in 'spells'?
historyQuestions.answer: No, nothing like that. Pretty steady day to day.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No paroxysmal symptoms argues against pheochromocytoma.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your family history of heart disease or high blood pressure.
historyQuestions.answer: My dad takes blood pressure pills. My mom had a stroke when she was about 65. My older brother has high blood pressure too.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Strong family history of HTN and stroke supports essential hypertension.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your diet and salt intake.
historyQuestions.answer: Honestly not great. I eat out a lot - fast food, Chinese takeout. Lots of processed snacks. I probably eat way more salt than I should.
historyQuestions.reasoning: High sodium diet is major modifiable risk factor for essential HTN.

historyQuestions.text: How much physical activity do you get?
historyQuestions.answer: Almost none. I sit at a computer all day and then sit on the couch at night. I know I should exercise but haven't made time.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Sedentary lifestyle is major modifiable risk factor.

historyQuestions.text: How has your weight changed over the years?
historyQuestions.answer: I've put on about 30 pounds since college. I'm probably about 215 now.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Obesity is major HTN risk factor. Also increases sleep apnea risk.

historyQuestions.text: Do you snore? Any trouble sleeping or daytime tiredness?
historyQuestions.answer: My wife says I snore. I do feel tired during the day but I figured that's normal.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Snoring and daytime somnolence suggest possible OSA, which can cause or worsen HTN.

historyQuestions.text: What medications or supplements do you take, including over-the-counter?
historyQuestions.answer: Just ibuprofen sometimes for back pain. Maybe a couple times a week.
historyQuestions.reasoning: NSAIDs can elevate BP modestly. Not likely primary cause here but worth noting.

historyQuestions.text: Any tobacco or alcohol use?
historyQuestions.answer: I quit smoking 5 years ago. I was about half a pack a day. I have a few beers on weekends, maybe 2-3.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Former smoker increases cardiovascular risk. Alcohol use is moderate.

historyQuestions.text: Any leg weakness, cramping with walking, or cold feet?
historyQuestions.answer: No, legs feel fine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Claudication or cold lower extremities can occur with coarctation. Absence is reassuring.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No known drug allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical for safe prescribing.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

diet_sodium_intake.answer: I don't add much salt but I eat a lot of packaged foods and eat out maybe 4-5 times a week.
diet_sodium_intake.relevance: Processed foods and restaurant meals are major sources of hidden sodium - likely contributing significantly to elevated BP.

diet_eating_patterns.answer: I skip breakfast, grab something quick for lunch at my desk, big dinner. Lots of snacking.
diet_eating_patterns.relevance: Irregular eating patterns and large evening meals contribute to metabolic dysfunction and weight gain.

occupation_current_job_stress.answer: IT manager. High stress, tight deadlines. I'm always putting out fires. Hard to take breaks.
occupation_current_job_stress.relevance: Chronic occupational stress contributes to sustained sympathetic activation and hypertension.

occupation_current_work_schedule.answer: Long hours. Usually 50-60 hours a week. On-call for emergencies. Sleep is unpredictable.
occupation_current_work_schedule.relevance: Long work hours, unpredictable schedule, and poor sleep all independently associated with hypertension.

exercise_activity_level.answer: Used to go to the gym but haven't been in months. Too busy. I sit most of the day.
exercise_activity_level.relevance: Physical inactivity is major modifiable risk factor for hypertension; sedentary occupation compounds this.

social_sleep_patterns.answer: Maybe 5-6 hours on work nights. I snore - my wife complains. I don't feel rested.
social_sleep_patterns.relevance: Short sleep duration and snoring suggest possible OSA - important secondary cause of hypertension to evaluate.

social_caffeine_intake.answer: Lots of coffee to get through the day. Maybe 5-6 cups. Energy drinks sometimes too.
social_caffeine_intake.relevance: High caffeine intake can acutely elevate BP and contribute to poor sleep quality.

social_stress_management.answer: I don't really have time for hobbies. Watch TV to unwind. Sometimes a beer helps me relax.
social_stress_management.relevance: Limited stress management strategies and sedentary leisure activities contribute to cardiovascular risk.

physicalExam.finding: Right arm: 156/98. Left arm: 154/96. No significant inter-arm difference.
physicalExam.reasoning: No BP asymmetry argues against coarctation. >20 mmHg difference would be concerning.

physicalExam.finding: Overweight male (BMI 31), appears comfortable, no acute distress. No truncal obesity, no moon facies, no striae.
physicalExam.reasoning: Obesity present. No cushingoid features.

physicalExam.finding: Mild arteriolar narrowing (grade I hypertensive retinopathy). No AV nicking, hemorrhages, exudates, or papilledema.
physicalExam.reasoning: Mild chronic changes suggest HTN has been present for some time. No acute/severe changes.

physicalExam.finding: No thyromegaly, no nodules, no tenderness. No tremor.
physicalExam.reasoning: Normal thyroid exam argues against hyperthyroidism.

physicalExam.finding: Regular rhythm, normal S1/S2. S4 gallop present (suggests LVH). No murmurs.
physicalExam.reasoning: S4 gallop suggests left ventricular stiffness from chronic HTN. Indicates some end-organ effect.

physicalExam.finding: No abdominal bruits. No renal artery bruits.
physicalExam.reasoning: Absence of renal bruits makes RAS less likely, though doesn't completely rule it out.

physicalExam.finding: All pulses 2+ and symmetric. Femoral pulses strong bilaterally. No radial-femoral delay.
physicalExam.reasoning: Normal femoral pulses and no radial-femoral delay effectively rules out coarctation.

physicalExam.finding: No peripheral edema.
physicalExam.reasoning: No signs of heart failure currently.

physicalExam.finding: Alert, oriented. No focal deficits. Normal strength and sensation.
physicalExam.reasoning: No evidence of prior stroke or hypertensive encephalopathy.

labs.name: Basic Metabolic Panel
labs.result: Na 140, K 4.1 (normal), Cl 102, CO2 25, BUN 16, Cr 1.0 (normal), Glucose 105.
labs.reasoning: CRITICAL: Normal potassium argues against primary aldosteronism. Normal creatinine rules out renal dysfunction. Baseline before starting ACE-I.

labs.name: ECG
labs.result: Sinus rhythm. LVH by voltage criteria (Sokolow-Lyon positive). No ischemic changes.
labs.reasoning: LVH indicates chronic HTN with target organ damage. Important for risk stratification and treatment decisions.

labs.name: Lipid Panel
labs.reasoning: Dyslipidemia present - adds to cardiovascular risk. Needs treatment along with HTN.

labs.name: Urinalysis (with urine microalbumin)
labs.result: No proteinuria, no hematuria. Microalbumin:creatinine ratio normal.
labs.reasoning: Screens for hypertensive nephropathy. Proteinuria would indicate target organ damage.

labs.name: Hemoglobin A1c
labs.reasoning: Prediabetes - adds to cardiovascular risk. Lifestyle modification even more important. Consider metformin.

labs.name: TSH
labs.reasoning: Normal TSH rules out thyroid dysfunction as cause of HTN. Hyperthyroidism can cause systolic HTN.

labs.name: CBC
labs.reasoning: Baseline. Polycythemia can cause HTN.

labs.name: Uric Acid
labs.reasoning: Elevated uric acid is associated with HTN and cardiovascular risk. May influence diuretic choice.

labs.name: Aldosterone:Renin Ratio
labs.result: Not indicated currently - no suspicion for primary aldosteronism (K+ normal, BP not resistant).
labs.reasoning: Screen if: hypokalemia, resistant HTN, adrenal incidentaloma. Not needed in routine new HTN with normal K+.

labs.name: Plasma/Urine Catecholamines/Metanephrines
labs.reasoning: Only if suspicion for pheochromocytoma (paroxysmal HTN, spells of headache/sweating/palpitations).

labs.name: Cortisol / Dexamethasone Suppression Test
labs.reasoning: Only if clinical suspicion for Cushing syndrome (central obesity, striae, moon facies, buffalo hump).

imaging.name: Echocardiogram
imaging.result: Mild concentric LVH. Normal EF 60%. No valvular disease. Normal diastolic function.
imaging.reasoning: Confirms and quantifies LVH. Useful for risk stratification. Not urgently needed but recommended.

imaging.name: Renal artery Doppler ultrasound
imaging.result: Not indicated given low clinical suspicion for RAS.
imaging.reasoning: Consider if: young female (fibromuscular dysplasia), abdominal bruit, resistant HTN, flash pulmonary edema, rising creatinine on ACE-I.

imaging.name: CT Angiography Renal Arteries
imaging.result: Not indicated - no suspicion for renovascular HTN.
imaging.reasoning: Gold standard for RAS but invasive. Reserve for high suspicion.

imaging.name: CT Adrenals
imaging.result: Not indicated - no suspicion for adrenal tumor.
imaging.reasoning: Would order if biochemical evidence of pheochromocytoma or aldosteronism.

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.result: Mildly enlarged cardiac silhouette. No pulmonary congestion.
imaging.reasoning: Not routinely needed for HTN. Useful if heart failure suspected.

procedures.name: Fundoscopic examination
procedures.result: Grade 1 hypertensive retinopathy (arteriolar narrowing). No hemorrhages, exudates, or papilledema.
procedures.reasoning: Direct visualization of end-organ effects. Papilledema = hypertensive emergency. Should be done on all new HTN diagnoses.

procedures.name: Ambulatory Blood Pressure Monitoring (ABPM)
procedures.result: Consider if suspicion for white coat HTN or masked HTN.
procedures.reasoning: Gold standard for confirming HTN diagnosis. Helps distinguish white coat HTN. May be useful before starting lifelong medication.

treatments.name: Lifestyle counseling (DASH diet, exercise, weight loss, sodium restriction)
treatments.result: Extensive counseling provided. DASH diet handout given. Exercise prescription: 150 min/week moderate activity. Sodium <2g/day.
treatments.reasoning: FIRST-LINE for all HTN. Can lower BP 10-15 mmHg. Essential even if medications started. Weight loss of 1 kg = 1 mmHg reduction.

treatments.name: ACE inhibitor (lisinopril 10mg daily)
treatments.result: Lisinopril 10mg daily prescribed. Follow-up in 2-4 weeks to assess response and titrate.
treatments.reasoning: First-line antihypertensive. Protects against diabetic nephropathy (important given prediabetes). Check K+ and Cr in 1-2 weeks.

treatments.name: ARB (losartan) - alternative to ACE-I
treatments.result: Use if ACE-I cough develops. Same efficacy, different side effect profile.
treatments.reasoning: Alternative first-line if ACE-I not tolerated. Do not combine ACE-I and ARB.

treatments.name: Thiazide diuretic (chlorthalidone 12.5mg)
treatments.result: Alternative first-line agent, or can be added if BP not controlled on ACE inhibitor.
treatments.reasoning: Excellent first-line, especially for Black patients. Chlorthalidone preferred over HCTZ (longer acting, better outcomes data).

treatments.name: Calcium channel blocker (amlodipine 5mg)
treatments.result: Can be used as first-line or added to ACE-I if needed.
treatments.reasoning: Good option especially for Black patients or as add-on. Avoid short-acting CCBs.

treatments.name: Beta-blocker (metoprolol)
treatments.result: Not first-line for uncomplicated HTN.
treatments.reasoning: Reserve for patients with compelling indications (CAD, HF, arrhythmia). Not preferred as initial monotherapy for HTN alone.

treatments.name: Statin therapy (atorvastatin)
treatments.result: Given dyslipidemia, would calculate 10-year ASCVD risk and treat accordingly.
treatments.reasoning: LDL 155 with HTN increases cardiovascular risk. Likely qualifies for statin therapy.

treatments.name: Aspirin
treatments.result: Not routinely recommended for primary prevention.
treatments.reasoning: USPSTF 2022: Aspirin for primary prevention is individualized. Not routine for HTN without established CVD.

consults.name: Cardiology consult
consults.result: Not needed for routine HTN management.
consults.reasoning: Primary care can manage most HTN. Refer if resistant HTN, secondary HTN suspected, or complex cardiac disease.

consults.name: Nephrology consult
consults.result: Not indicated - renal function normal.
consults.reasoning: Would consult if suspected renovascular HTN, resistant HTN, or declining renal function.

consults.name: Nutrition/Dietitian referral
consults.result: Referred for DASH diet counseling and weight management.
consults.reasoning: Professional dietary counseling improves adherence to lifestyle changes.

disposition.name: Outpatient follow-up in 2-4 weeks
disposition.result: Follow-up scheduled in 3 weeks to recheck BP, assess medication effect, check K+/Cr.
disposition.reasoning: Need to confirm BP response and monitor for medication side effects. Titrate as needed.

disposition.name: Home BP monitoring
disposition.result: Instructed on proper technique. Asked to check and log BP twice daily (morning and evening).
disposition.reasoning: Helps rule out white coat HTN, monitors response to treatment, improves patient engagement.

disposition.name: Emergency referral
disposition.result: NOT indicated - this is NOT hypertensive emergency.
disposition.reasoning: Hypertensive emergency requires end-organ damage (encephalopathy, AKI, aortic dissection, MI, etc.). This patient is asymptomatic.

actualDiagnosis.name: Essential (Primary) Hypertension, Stage 2, with Target Organ Damage (LVH)
keyFindings: New diagnosis of HTN: BP 156/98 (Stage 2: systolic ≥140 or diastolic ≥90)
Multiple modifiable risk factors: obesity (BMI 31), high-sodium diet, sedentary lifestyle
Strong family history of HTN and stroke
No symptoms of secondary causes (no spells, no claudication, no muscle weakness)
Physical exam: S4 gallop, mild hypertensive retinopathy
Normal K+ (argues against aldosteronism), no renal bruits (argues against RAS), equal arm BPs and strong femoral pulses (rules out coarctation)
Target organ damage already present: LVH on ECG and echo
teachingPoints: ESSENTIAL HTN is the diagnosis in >90% of cases: But always screen for secondary causes, especially in young patients, severe/resistant HTN, or with suspicious symptoms.
SECONDARY HTN RED FLAGS: Age <30 or >55 at onset, severe/resistant HTN, hypokalemia, abdominal bruit, episodic symptoms (spells), cushingoid appearance.
BP IN BOTH ARMS: Should be done at initial diagnosis. >20 mmHg difference suggests coarctation or subclavian stenosis.
FUNDOSCOPIC EXAM: Can detect chronic hypertensive damage. Papilledema indicates hypertensive emergency.
S4 GALLOP = LVH: Indicates chronic pressure overload. The heart is already affected - treatment is urgent.
LIFESTYLE IS FIRST-LINE: DASH diet + exercise + weight loss + sodium restriction can lower BP 10-15 mmHg. Essential even if medications needed.
LVH IS TARGET ORGAN DAMAGE: Patient has Stage 2 HTN with evidence of end-organ effects. This elevates cardiovascular risk significantly.

guidelineReferences.name: ACC/AHA Guidelines for Prevention and Treatment of Hypertension 2017
guidelineReferences.citation: Whelton PK et al. J Am Coll Cardiol. 2018;71(19):e127-e248
keyPoints: Stage 2 HTN: SBP ≥140 or DBP ≥90 mmHg
First-line: Thiazide, CCB, ACE-I, or ARB
Target <130/80 for most adults with CVD risk
DASH diet + exercise can reduce BP 10-15 mmHg
Screen for secondary causes if suspicious features

guidelineReferences.name: ESC/ESH Guidelines for Arterial Hypertension 2018
guidelineReferences.citation: Williams B et al. Eur Heart J. 2018;39(33):3021-3104
keyPoints: Out-of-office BP measurement recommended to confirm diagnosis
Combination therapy often needed for Stage 2 HTN
Assess HMOD (hypertension-mediated organ damage) at diagnosis
LVH increases cardiovascular risk significantly
Lifestyle modifications: salt <5g/day, BMI 20-25, exercise 30 min/day

closureScript.diagnosis: Mr. Chen, your blood pressure is elevated - it's been running around 156/98, which we call Stage 2 hypertension. The exam of your heart suggests your blood pressure has actually been high for a while - your heart has thickened up from pumping against that high pressure. This is called left ventricular hypertrophy.
closureScript.plan: We need to start treatment. First, I want you to make some lifestyle changes: cut back on salt and processed foods, try to exercise most days, and work on losing some weight. These changes alone can lower your blood pressure by 10-15 points. But given that your heart is already showing effects, I'm also going to start you on a blood pressure medication today. We'll check some blood and urine tests, and I want you to follow up in 2-4 weeks so we can see how you're responding.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: Call us or go to the ER if you develop a severe headache, vision changes, chest pain, or trouble breathing. These could indicate very high blood pressure that needs immediate treatment.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: I know you feel fine, and that's actually common with high blood pressure - it's called 'the silent killer' for a reason. You feel okay until something bad happens, like a stroke or heart attack. Think of your mom's stroke - that's what we're trying to prevent. Taking a pill every day and eating healthier is a small price to pay to avoid that.

patientBackground.recentEvents: Patient was in usual state of health until symptoms began. No major life changes recently.
patientBackground.recentIllnesses: Baseline chronic conditions as per medical history. No new acute illnesses.
patientBackground.contextualFactors: Dealing with typical life stressors. Concerned about current symptoms.
patientBackground.supportSystem: Has family and friends available for support. Not isolated.
patientBackground.healthBeliefs: Generally follows medical advice. Open to treatment recommendations.
patientBackground.previousERVisits: Occasional ER visits for minor issues. Hospitalizations as documented in history.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if BP was 220/130 with headache and vision changes?
whatIfScenarios.description: Severe hypertension with encephalopathy symptoms and papilledema

chiefComplaint.original: My blood pressure was high at the health fair
chiefComplaint.modified: I have the worst headache of my life, my vision is blurry, and I'm confused

feedback.correct: HYPERTENSIVE EMERGENCY = severe HTN + END-ORGAN DAMAGE. This is NOT the same as severe HTN alone. IV meds (nicardipine, labetalol), ICU, lower BP by 25% in first hour (NOT to normal - risk of stroke).
feedback.partial: Good recognition of emergency. What's the BP target?
feedback.incorrect: Hypertensive emergency = severe HTN + acute end-organ damage (brain, heart, aorta, kidneys). Requires IV therapy in ICU. Target: reduce BP by 25% in first hour, then to 160/100 over 2-6 hours. Rapid normalization can cause stroke!

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Hypertensive urgency = severe HTN without end-organ damage (can treat as outpatient). Hypertensive emergency = end-organ damage present (ICU, IV therapy). The end-organ damage makes all the difference.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if this was a 28-year-old with resistant hypertension?
whatIfScenarios.description: Young patient with HTN resistant to 3 drugs, hypokalemia

chiefComplaint.original: My blood pressure was high at the health fair
chiefComplaint.modified: 28-year-old: My BP is still 160/105 even though I'm taking 3 medications. My potassium keeps running low.

feedback.correct: Young + resistant HTN + hypokalemia = primary aldosteronism until proven otherwise. Check aldosterone:renin ratio. Other secondary causes: RAS (renal artery stenosis), pheochromocytoma, Cushing, coarctation.
feedback.partial: Good concern for secondary HTN. What's the most likely cause?
feedback.incorrect: Secondary HTN red flags: young age (<30), resistant HTN, hypokalemia (aldosteronism), episodic symptoms (pheo), cushingoid features. Workup: aldosterone/renin ratio, renal artery imaging, 24h urinary catecholamines/metanephrines. Don't assume essential HTN in young resistant patients.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Essential HTN = >90% of cases, older patients, responds to treatment. Secondary HTN = young, resistant, with clues (hypokalemia, spells, Cushing features). Identifying secondary cause can be curative.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if there was an abdominal bruit?
whatIfScenarios.description: Hypertension with abdominal bruit and acute rise in creatinine on ACE inhibitor

chiefComplaint.original: My blood pressure was high at the health fair
chiefComplaint.modified: Started ACE inhibitor last week. Now my creatinine jumped from 1.0 to 2.4.

feedback.correct: Creatinine rise >30% on ACE-I = bilateral RAS or RAS with solitary kidney. ACE-I blocks angiotensin II that was maintaining GFR. Stop ACE-I, image renal arteries (CTA/MRA), consider revascularization.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of RAS. Why does ACE-I cause this?
feedback.incorrect: Renal artery stenosis clues: abdominal bruit, resistant HTN, flash pulmonary edema, creatinine rise on ACE-I/ARB. ACE-I unmasks RAS - kidney depends on angiotensin II to maintain GFR past the stenosis. Workup: renal artery duplex, CTA, or MRA. May need revascularization.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Normal response to ACE-I = Cr may rise slightly (<20%). RAS response = Cr rises significantly (>30%). This is diagnostic clue, not just side effect. Image the renal arteries.

guidelineReferences.name: 2017 ACC/AHA/HRS Guideline for Syncope
guidelineReferences.citation: Shen WK, et al. Circulation. 2017;136:e60-e122
keyPoints: Initial evaluation requires thorough history, physical examination, and ECG (Class I)
Syncope in aortic stenosis is associated with structural heart disease - high-risk feature
Exertional syncope suggests severe, symptomatic AS requiring urgent valve intervention
Echocardiography recommended when cardiac cause suspected (Class I)
Hospital admission indicated for serious conditions including severe aortic stenosis
Risk stratification: structural heart disease and syncope during exertion = higher risk

guidelineReferences.citation: Otto CM, et al. Circulation. 2021;143:e72-e227
keyPoints: Symptomatic severe AS (including syncope) is Class I indication for AVR
Severe AS defined as: aortic valve area ≤1.0 cm², mean gradient ≥40 mmHg, or Vmax ≥4 m/s

male-78-as.name: Harold Phillips
male-78-as.chiefComplaint: I passed out while climbing the stairs
male-78-as.variantDescription: Severe aortic stenosis with exertional syncope

missedConsequences.Severe Aortic Stenosis: Exertional syncope indicates severe AS. Median survival <2 years without valve replacement.

commonPitfalls: Attributing syncope to vasovagal without cardiac workup in elderly
Missing crescendo-decrescendo murmur
Not recognizing exertional syncope = cardiac until proven otherwise

allDiagnoses.name: Severe Aortic Stenosis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Elderly with exertional syncope, systolic murmur to carotids, diminished pulses = classic.

allDiagnoses.name: Cardiac Arrhythmia
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can cause syncope. AS patients prone to arrhythmias.

allDiagnoses.name: Orthostatic Hypotension
allDiagnoses.rationale: Wouldn't explain exertional component or murmur.

male-78-as.oldcartsNarrative: "I was climbing stairs and suddenly everything went gray. No warning. My wife said I was out 30 seconds. I've been getting more winded with stairs and sometimes get chest tightness. Doctor mentioned a murmur years ago."

historyQuestions.text: What were you doing when you passed out?
historyQuestions.answer: Climbing stairs. Almost at the top when everything went black.
historyQuestions.reasoning: EXERTIONAL syncope in elderly = cardiac until proven otherwise.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about getting winded.
historyQuestions.answer: Over 6 months I can't do what I used to. Stairs, groceries - have to stop and rest.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Progressive exertional dyspnea is part of AS triad.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about chest tightness.
historyQuestions.answer: When I exert myself, pressure in my chest. Goes away with rest.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Exertional angina - another AS symptom triad component.

historyQuestions.text: You mentioned a murmur?
historyQuestions.answer: A doctor heard it maybe 10 years ago. Said to keep an eye on it but I never followed up.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Known murmur not followed - AS can progress over years

historyQuestions.text: What medications do you take?
historyQuestions.answer: Lisinopril for blood pressure, aspirin, and atorvastatin. Nothing else.
historyQuestions.reasoning: ACE inhibitor - use cautiously in severe AS due to preload dependence

historyQuestions.text: Any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important before any procedures

historyQuestions.text: Any other medical problems?
historyQuestions.answer: High blood pressure and high cholesterol. Had a knee replacement. Prostate surgery years ago.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history relevant for valve replacement planning

historyQuestions.text: Any family history of heart problems?
historyQuestions.answer: My father died of a heart attack at 70. My brother had bypass surgery.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Family history of CAD - may need coronary evaluation before valve surgery

historyQuestions.text: Did you have any warning before you passed out?
historyQuestions.answer: Nothing. One second I was climbing, next thing I knew I was on the floor. My wife said I was out about 30 seconds.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No prodrome - concerning for cardiac syncope vs vasovagal which has prodrome

social_functional_decline.answer: Used to walk 2 miles daily. Now barely make it around the block. Stopped golfing.
social_functional_decline.relevance: Progressive functional decline indicates advancing cardiac disease.

social_healthcare_followup.answer: Haven't seen heart doctor in years. Figured no news is good news.
social_healthcare_followup.relevance: Lost to follow-up allowed AS to progress without monitoring.

physicalExam.finding: Carotid upstroke DELAYED and DIMINISHED (parvus et tardus). Bruit present.
physicalExam.reasoning: PARVUS ET TARDUS is classic finding of severe AS.

physicalExam.finding: Harsh, late-peaking crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur at RUSB, radiating to carotids. Soft S2.
physicalExam.reasoning: LATE PEAKING and SOFT S2 indicate SEVERE stenosis.

labs.name: BNP or NT-proBNP
labs.result: BNP 1,250 pg/mL (markedly elevated). Indicates significant cardiac strain from pressure overload.
labs.reasoning: BNP correlates with AS severity and prognosis. Higher levels predict worse outcomes. Useful for risk stratification.

labs.name: Troponin
labs.result: Troponin I 0.02 ng/mL (normal). No evidence of acute myocardial injury.
labs.reasoning: Rules out MI as cause of syncope. Exertional angina in AS is from supply-demand mismatch, not always coronary disease.

labs.name: CBC
labs.reasoning: Rules out anemia which can worsen AS symptoms by increasing cardiac output demands.

labs.name: BMP
labs.result: Na 140, K 4.2, Cr 1.1, Glucose 105 - normal renal function
labs.reasoning: Baseline renal function before contrast studies (cardiac cath, CT). Important for procedural planning.

labs.name: PT/INR, PTT
labs.reasoning: Baseline coagulation. Will need anticoagulation post-valve replacement (if mechanical valve) or monitoring with surgical procedures.

labs.name: Liver function tests
labs.reasoning: Right heart failure from severe AS can cause hepatic congestion. Normal here.

labs.name: TSH
labs.reasoning: Thyroid dysfunction can affect cardiac function. Part of pre-operative evaluation.

labs.name: Hemoglobin A1c
labs.reasoning: Diabetes assessment. Impacts surgical risk and wound healing.

imaging.name: Transthoracic Echocardiogram
imaging.result: SEVERE AORTIC STENOSIS: AVA 0.7 cm² (normal >2.0), mean gradient 52 mmHg, peak velocity 4.8 m/s. Concentric LVH. EF 45% (mild LV dysfunction). No significant mitral regurgitation. Elevated PASP 45 mmHg.
imaging.reasoning: DIAGNOSTIC TEST OF CHOICE. Severe AS defined by: AVA <1.0 cm², mean gradient >40 mmHg, peak velocity >4 m/s. This patient meets all criteria. Reduced EF indicates decompensation.

imaging.name: 12-lead ECG
imaging.result: Sinus rhythm at 68 bpm. LVH by voltage criteria (S in V1 + R in V5 >35mm). LAE. No acute ST changes. No arrhythmia.
imaging.reasoning: LVH from chronic pressure overload is expected. Rules out arrhythmia as cause of syncope. Baseline before procedures.

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.result: Cardiomegaly. Calcified aortic valve visible. Post-stenotic dilation of ascending aorta. No pulmonary edema. No pleural effusion.
imaging.reasoning: Calcified valve and post-stenotic aortic dilation are classic radiographic findings. No pulmonary edema suggests compensation.

imaging.name: CT for aortic valve calcium scoring
imaging.result: Heavily calcified aortic valve. Calcium score >2000 AU consistent with severe AS. Ascending aorta 4.3 cm (mildly dilated).
imaging.reasoning: Calcium scoring can confirm severity when echo is discordant. Assesses ascending aorta for aneurysm (associated with bicuspid valve).

imaging.name: Left heart catheterization
imaging.result: Pre-operative evaluation: Moderate CAD with 60% LAD lesion, 50% RCA. LVEDP 22 mmHg (elevated). Aortic valve gradient confirmed.
imaging.reasoning: Guidelines recommend coronary angiography before valve surgery in men >40, women >50, or with CAD risk factors. Determines need for concurrent CABG.

imaging.name: CT angiography (TAVR planning)
imaging.result: Vascular access suitable for transfemoral TAVR. Aortic annulus measurements obtained for valve sizing.
imaging.reasoning: Required for TAVR planning. Assesses: annulus size for valve selection, vascular access (iliofemoral), coronary heights, aortic root anatomy.

imaging.name: Transesophageal Echocardiogram
imaging.result: May be needed for better valve visualization or TAVR planning
imaging.reasoning: Better visualization of valve anatomy. May be needed intra-operatively for TAVR or if TTE images suboptimal.

procedures.name: Holter monitor (24-48 hour)
procedures.result: Arranged to evaluate for arrhythmia, though echo findings clearly explain syncope
procedures.reasoning: AS patients are prone to arrhythmias. Holter may detect occult arrhythmias. However, the severe AS with exertional syncope is the primary explanation.

procedures.name: Exercise stress testing
procedures.reasoning: CONTRAINDICATED in symptomatic severe AS due to risk of syncope, arrhythmia, and sudden death. Only consider supervised low-level test in asymptomatic patients if truly needed.

treatments.name: Activity restriction - avoid all exertion
treatments.result: Patient counseled to avoid ALL strenuous activity until valve replacement. No stairs unless necessary. No lifting. Use wheelchair for transport.
treatments.reasoning: Exertional syncope with severe AS carries sudden death risk. Complete activity restriction until definitive treatment.

treatments.name: Avoid vasodilators
treatments.result: Hold ACE inhibitor (lisinopril). Avoid nitrates, CCBs, and other vasodilators. Avoid hot tubs/saunas.
treatments.reasoning: Severe AS patients are PRELOAD DEPENDENT. Vasodilation causes dangerous hypotension. Fixed valve obstruction cannot increase cardiac output to compensate.

treatments.name: Avoid aggressive diuresis
treatments.result: If diuretics needed, use very cautiously with close monitoring
treatments.reasoning: Volume depletion is dangerous in AS. Reduced preload across fixed obstruction causes hypotension and syncope.

treatments.name: Maintain sinus rhythm
treatments.result: Rate control and rhythm control important - atrial kick provides 20-25% of cardiac output in AS
treatments.reasoning: Loss of atrial kick (AF) is poorly tolerated in AS. Vigorous rate control and consideration of cardioversion if AF develops.

treatments.name: Continuous telemetry monitoring
treatments.result: On telemetry for arrhythmia monitoring
treatments.reasoning: Monitor for arrhythmias. Severe AS patients at risk for sudden death from arrhythmia.

treatments.name: Adequate IV hydration
treatments.result: Maintenance fluids to ensure adequate preload
treatments.reasoning: Maintain preload. Avoid both volume overload (pulmonary edema) and volume depletion (syncope).

consults.name: Cardiology - URGENT
consults.result: URGENT cardiology consult for valve replacement evaluation. This patient has Class I indication for AVR.
consults.reasoning: Symptomatic severe AS (syncope, angina, HF) is Class I indication for valve replacement. Without intervention, median survival is <2 years with syncope.

consults.name: Cardiothoracic Surgery consultation
consults.result: Consulted for SAVR evaluation. Patient to be discussed at Heart Team conference for SAVR vs TAVR decision.
consults.reasoning: All patients need Heart Team (cardiology + CT surgery) evaluation. Factors: age, surgical risk (STS score), anatomy, patient preference.

consults.name: Interventional Cardiology (TAVR)
consults.result: Consulted as part of Heart Team for TAVR evaluation
consults.reasoning: TAVR now approved for all-risk patients. Decision based on STS score, anatomy, life expectancy, patient preference.

consults.name: Cardiac Anesthesia
consults.result: Pre-operative evaluation for valve replacement
consults.reasoning: Anesthesia planning for either SAVR or TAVR. Risk assessment and optimization.

disposition.name: Admit to telemetry for monitoring and expedited evaluation
disposition.result: Admitted for cardiac monitoring, echocardiogram confirmation, cardiology consultation, and valve replacement planning.
disposition.reasoning: Exertional syncope with severe AS is HIGH RISK. Needs inpatient monitoring and expedited evaluation for valve replacement. Do not discharge without definitive plan.

disposition.name: Admit to CCU
disposition.result: Consider if hemodynamically unstable or recurrent syncope
disposition.reasoning: ICU for: hemodynamic instability, recurrent syncope, arrhythmias, decompensated heart failure.

disposition.name: Discharge home
disposition.result: INAPPROPRIATE - high-risk patient with symptomatic severe AS
disposition.reasoning: NEVER discharge symptomatic severe AS without definitive intervention plan. Median survival with syncope is 1-2 years without valve replacement.

actualDiagnosis.name: Severe Aortic Stenosis with Exertional Syncope
keyFindings: Exertional syncope without prodrome
Late-peaking systolic murmur radiating to carotids
Parvus et tardus (weak, delayed) carotid pulse
Soft S2 indicating calcified immobile valve
Echo: valve area 0.7 cm², mean gradient >40 mmHg
Progressive exertional dyspnea and angina
teachingPoints: AS symptom triad: Angina, Syncope, Heart failure - poor prognosis without intervention
Exertional syncope in elderly = cardiac until proven otherwise
Symptomatic severe AS needs URGENT valve replacement

guidelineReferences.name: 2020 ACC/AHA Guideline for Management of Valvular Heart Disease
guidelineReferences.citation: Otto CM et al. J Am Coll Cardiol. 2021;77(4):e25-e197
keyPoints: Symptomatic severe AS (syncope, angina, HF): Class I indication for AVR
Heart Team discussion for all patients considering intervention
TAVR or SAVR reasonable for most patients - decision based on individual factors
TAVR recommended for patients with high/prohibitive surgical risk
Avoid vasodilators and maintain preload in severe AS

guidelineReferences.name: 2021 ESC/EACTS Guidelines for Management of Valvular Heart Disease
guidelineReferences.citation: Vahanian A et al. Eur Heart J. 2022;43(7):561-632
keyPoints: Severe AS: AVA <1.0 cm², mean gradient >40 mmHg, Vmax >4.0 m/s
Intervention indicated for all symptomatic severe AS
STS-PROM and EuroSCORE II guide TAVR vs SAVR decision
TAVR recommended for patients ≥75 years suitable for transfemoral approach
Low-flow low-gradient AS may need dobutamine stress echo for diagnosis

closureScript.diagnosis: You have severe aortic stenosis - the valve letting blood leave your heart is very narrowed. This caused your symptoms and fainting.
closureScript.plan: I'm admitting you. You'll likely need valve replacement - either surgery or TAVR procedure.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: Avoid exertion until fixed. Sit or lie down if faint.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: Valve replacement is very effective. People often feel dramatically better.

patientBackground.recentEvents: Patient was in usual state of health until symptoms began. No major life changes recently.
patientBackground.recentIllnesses: Baseline chronic conditions as per medical history. No new acute illnesses.
patientBackground.contextualFactors: Dealing with typical life stressors. Concerned about current symptoms.
patientBackground.supportSystem: Has family and friends available for support. Not isolated.
patientBackground.healthBeliefs: Generally follows medical advice. Open to treatment recommendations.
patientBackground.previousERVisits: Occasional ER visits for minor issues. Hospitalizations as documented in history.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient was 85 years old with multiple comorbidities?
whatIfScenarios.description: Same presentation but patient is elderly with high surgical risk

chiefComplaint.original: I passed out while climbing stairs
chiefComplaint.modified: Same presentation - but patient is 85 with COPD, CKD, and prior stroke. STS score 8%.

mustNotMiss: TAVR preferred over SAVR for high-risk
keyDiscriminators: TAVR approved for all risk groups

feedback.correct: Age and comorbidities don't preclude treatment! TAVR is approved for all risk groups. This patient likely better candidate for TAVR than SAVR. Heart Team decision considers STS score, frailty, anatomy, life expectancy. Symptomatic severe AS still has very poor prognosis without intervention.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of TAVR indication. What factors guide TAVR vs SAVR?
feedback.incorrect: TAVR revolutionized treatment of high-risk patients. Decision factors: STS score (>8% favors TAVR), anatomy (bicuspid may favor SAVR), life expectancy (>1 year needed), patient preference. Even octogenarians benefit from intervention. Never 'too old' if functional.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Low surgical risk younger patient = SAVR often preferred (durability). High-risk elderly = TAVR. Both are effective - Heart Team decides based on individual factors.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient presented with acute pulmonary edema?
whatIfScenarios.description: Severe AS with acute decompensated heart failure

chiefComplaint.original: I passed out while climbing stairs
chiefComplaint.modified: Can't breathe, worse lying down. Severe orthopnea, pink frothy sputum. Known AS.

expectedReasoning.topDiagnosis: Severe AS with acute decompensated heart failure

feedback.correct: AS + acute HF is a medical emergency. Careful diuresis (they're preload dependent but drowning). Avoid afterload reduction (dangerous in AS). May need inotropes. URGENT valve intervention - don't wait for optimization. Mortality without intervention is very high.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of urgency. What hemodynamic considerations in AS?
feedback.incorrect: AS with acute HF: preload dependent (need filling) but in pulmonary edema. Gentle diuresis with close monitoring. NO vasodilators (afterload reduction dangerous - can't increase CO). May need dobutamine. Urgent TAVR or SAVR - don't delay. Consider balloon valvuloplasty as bridge.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Stable symptomatic AS = expedited but not emergent intervention. AS with acute HF = urgent intervention, medical bridge therapy, high mortality without treatment.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if severe AS was discovered before elective surgery?
whatIfScenarios.description: Patient needs hip replacement, found to have severe AS on preop echo

chiefComplaint.original: I passed out while climbing stairs
chiefComplaint.modified: Asymptomatic patient needing elective hip replacement. Preop echo shows severe AS (AVA 0.8, gradient 45).

expectedReasoning.topDiagnosis: Severe AS - surgical risk assessment needed
mustNotMiss: Need for AVR before elective surgery

feedback.correct: Severe AS + noncardiac surgery is complex. If truly asymptomatic, elective surgery can often proceed with careful hemodynamic management. If symptomatic (even subtle), AVR should be considered first. Cardiology consult essential. Avoid hypotension, maintain sinus rhythm, invasive monitoring.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of complexity. When should AVR precede noncardiac surgery?
feedback.incorrect: Severe AS + noncardiac surgery: 1) Confirm truly asymptomatic (stress test may unmask symptoms), 2) If symptomatic - AVR first for elective cases, 3) If asymptomatic - can proceed with precautions: invasive monitoring, avoid hypotension, maintain preload, avoid tachycardia/bradycardia. Cardiology involved.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Symptomatic severe AS needs AVR regardless. Asymptomatic severe AS + elective surgery = careful evaluation, may proceed with precautions or may need AVR first depending on symptoms and surgery risk.

questions.stem: Based on the clinical presentation, which is the MOST appropriate next step?

options.text: Systematic history and focused physical examination
options.explanation: A targeted history and physical exam is the essential first step in clinical reasoning, guiding hypothesis generation and differential diagnosis.
Empiric treatment before adequate evaluation risks missing the correct diagnosis and may cause harm.
Diagnostic testing should be hypothesis-driven, not shotgun; targeted tests based on clinical reasoning yield better outcomes.
Consultation may be appropriate later but should follow initial clinical assessment and diagnostic reasoning.

questions.stem: Which cognitive bias poses the greatest risk in this clinical scenario?

options.explanation: Availability bias (overweighting recent experiences) is common but not the most frequent source of error in this scenario.
Anchoring (fixating on initial impression) is a risk but can be mitigated by systematic differential generation.
Premature closure — accepting a diagnosis before it is fully verified — is the single most common cognitive error in clinical reasoning across all settings.
Confirmation bias (seeking only supporting evidence) contributes to error but often follows from premature closure.

guidelineReferences.name: 2017 ACC/AHA/HRS Guideline for Syncope
guidelineReferences.citation: Shen WK, et al. Circulation. 2017;136:e60-e122
keyPoints: Sustained VT causing syncope is a high-risk feature requiring hospital admission
Abnormal ECG is a key short-term and long-term risk factor (Table 5)
ICD evaluation indicated for syncope with structural heart disease and reduced EF
EPS may identify substrate for clinical tachyarrhythmia as potential cause
Inducible VT in ischemic heart disease is predictive of spontaneous VT and prognosis
History, physical examination, and baseline ECG are Class I recommendations for all patients

male-62-vt.name: Derek Kim
male-62-vt.chiefComplaint: I passed out at work without any warning
male-62-vt.variantDescription: VT causing syncope in patient with cardiomyopathy

missedConsequences.Ventricular Tachycardia: Undiagnosed VT leads to sudden cardiac death.

commonPitfalls: Attributing syncope to vasovagal in patient with heart disease
Not obtaining ECG looking for structural heart disease signs
Discharging without monitoring

allDiagnoses.name: Ventricular Tachycardia
allDiagnoses.rationale: Sudden syncope without warning in patient with prior MI and low EF.

allDiagnoses.name: Complete Heart Block
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can cause sudden syncope (Stokes-Adams attack).

allDiagnoses.name: Vasovagal Syncope
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would have prodrome. NOT in patient with structural heart disease.

male-62-vt.oldcartsNarrative: "I was at my desk and woke up on the floor. No warning - just lights out. Heart was racing when I came to. Had a heart attack 5 years ago, they said my heart function is weak - EF 35%."

historyQuestions.text: Was there ANY warning?
historyQuestions.answer: None. I was typing and then woke up on the floor.
historyQuestions.reasoning: NO PRODROME = cardiac until proven otherwise.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your heart history.
historyQuestions.answer: Heart attack 5 years ago. Stents. EF was 35%.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prior MI + reduced EF = high risk for ventricular arrhythmias.

historyQuestions.text: Family history of heart problems or sudden death?
historyQuestions.answer: Father died suddenly at 58 - his heart. Brother has pacemaker.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Family history of sudden death is major risk factor.

historyQuestions.text: What medications do you take?
historyQuestions.answer: Metoprolol, lisinopril, aspirin, atorvastatin, and clopidogrel (Plavix). I take a bunch of pills.
historyQuestions.reasoning: On appropriate post-MI medications including beta-blocker

historyQuestions.text: Any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: Amiodarone gave me thyroid problems. Had to stop it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important - amiodarone allergy limits antiarrhythmic options

historyQuestions.text: Did you feel your heart racing before or after?
historyQuestions.answer: When I woke up my heart was pounding - really fast. Took a few minutes to slow down.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Post-syncope tachycardia suggests arrhythmia as cause

historyQuestions.text: Has this ever happened before?
historyQuestions.answer: I've felt lightheaded a few times in the past year but never passed out until now.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prior near-syncope episodes may have been self-terminating VT

historyQuestions.text: Has anyone ever talked to you about a defibrillator?
historyQuestions.answer: My cardiologist mentioned it after my heart attack but I wasn't ready. Maybe I should have listened.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Patient was previously offered ICD but declined - now presenting with indication

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any other medical conditions?
historyQuestions.answer: High blood pressure. Otherwise healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Past medical history affects diagnosis and treatment.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

social_medication_adherence.answer: Take most pills. Sometimes miss beta-blocker if too tired.
social_medication_adherence.relevance: Non-adherence to beta-blocker reduces arrhythmia protection.

social_caffeine_alcohol.answer: Lots of coffee. Couple drinks most nights.
social_caffeine_alcohol.relevance: Caffeine and alcohol are arrhythmia triggers.

physicalExam.finding: Soft S3 gallop. Occasional ectopy noted during exam.
physicalExam.reasoning: S3 indicates reduced EF. Ectopy on exam is concerning.

labs.name: Basic Metabolic Panel
labs.result: Na 138, K 3.4 (low-normal), Cl 102, CO2 24, BUN 18, Cr 1.1, Glucose 98, Ca 9.2, Mg 1.6 (low-normal)
labs.reasoning: Electrolyte abnormalities (especially K+, Mg2+, Ca2+) can trigger arrhythmias. Low-normal values should be repleted in a cardiac patient.

labs.name: CBC
labs.reasoning: Anemia can exacerbate cardiac ischemia and unmask arrhythmias. Normal here.

labs.name: Troponin
labs.result: Troponin I 0.03 ng/mL (normal <0.04). No acute ischemia.
labs.reasoning: Syncope can be caused by acute MI. Rule out ischemia as trigger for arrhythmia.

labs.name: BNP
labs.result: BNP 890 pg/mL (elevated). Consistent with known heart failure.
labs.reasoning: Elevated BNP confirms heart failure. Higher BNP associated with worse arrhythmia risk.

labs.name: TSH
labs.reasoning: Thyroid dysfunction can cause arrhythmias. Normal here.

labs.name: Digoxin Level (if applicable)
labs.reasoning: Digoxin toxicity can cause arrhythmias. Not applicable here.

imaging.name: 12-lead ECG
imaging.result: Sinus rhythm, rate 72. Old anterior MI (Q waves V1-V4). Frequent PVCs with couplets. 6-beat run of NSVT captured during tracing.
imaging.reasoning: CRITICAL FINDING: NSVT in a patient with prior MI and syncope = presumed VT as cause until proven otherwise. High sudden death risk.

imaging.name: Transthoracic Echocardiogram
imaging.result: EF 30% (severely reduced). Anterior and septal akinesis consistent with prior MI. Moderate MR. No thrombus. LVIDD 6.2 cm (dilated).
imaging.reasoning: EF ≤35% + syncope + NSVT = Class I indication for ICD per ACC/AHA guidelines. Dilated LV confirms ischemic cardiomyopathy.

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.result: Cardiomegaly. Mild pulmonary vascular congestion. No acute infiltrate.
imaging.reasoning: Confirms cardiomegaly and mild volume overload consistent with HFrEF.

procedures.name: Continuous Cardiac Monitoring
procedures.result: Admitted to telemetry. Multiple NSVT runs (3-8 beats) captured overnight. PVC burden estimated at 15%.
procedures.reasoning: Continuous monitoring essential - documents arrhythmia burden and captures events for EP review.

procedures.name: Electrophysiology Study
procedures.result: Inducible monomorphic VT with cycle length 280ms. Confirmed VT as mechanism.
procedures.reasoning: EP study confirms VT inducibility. Combined with symptoms and reduced EF, confirms ICD indication.

procedures.name: Cardiac Catheterization (if not recent)
procedures.result: Known CAD with old LAD occlusion. No new lesions. Collaterals present.
procedures.reasoning: Define coronary anatomy. Revascularization may reduce arrhythmia burden if ischemia present.

treatments.name: Implantable Cardioverter-Defibrillator (ICD)
treatments.result: ICD implantation scheduled. Single-chamber ICD selected given preserved AV conduction.
treatments.reasoning: PRIMARY therapy for syncope + VT + EF ≤35%. Reduces sudden cardiac death by 50-60%. Not optional - this is life-saving.

treatments.name: Optimize Beta-Blocker
treatments.result: Metoprolol succinate increased to 100mg daily (previously on 50mg).
treatments.reasoning: Beta-blockers reduce arrhythmia burden and sudden death in HFrEF. Uptitrate to maximum tolerated dose.

treatments.name: Antiarrhythmic Therapy
treatments.result: Amiodarone 400mg loading, then 200mg daily for VT suppression.
treatments.reasoning: Consider if high VT burden or ICD shocks. Reduces appropriate shocks. Monitor thyroid and LFTs.

treatments.name: Electrolyte Repletion
treatments.result: K+ repleted to >4.0, Mg to >2.0. Potassium chloride 40 mEq PO x 1, Magnesium oxide 400mg daily.
treatments.reasoning: Optimize electrolytes to reduce arrhythmia risk. Keep K+ 4.0-5.0, Mg >2.0 in arrhythmia patients.

treatments.name: Optimize ACE-I/ARB
treatments.result: Continue lisinopril 20mg daily. Consider ARNI if tolerating.
treatments.reasoning: GDMT for HFrEF reduces mortality. ARNI (sacubitril/valsartan) preferred if BP tolerates.

treatments.name: Driving Restrictions
treatments.result: Counseled on no driving until cleared by EP. State reporting as required.
treatments.reasoning: Syncope + arrhythmia = no driving. Varies by state but typically 3-6 months post-ICD. Legal and safety requirement.

consults.name: Electrophysiology
consults.result: EP consulted. Agrees with ICD implantation. Will perform EP study and device implant.
consults.reasoning: EP manages ICD implantation and arrhythmia follow-up. Essential consult.

consults.name: Heart Failure Team
consults.result: HF team involved for GDMT optimization. Will see in HF clinic.
consults.reasoning: Optimize medical therapy to improve EF and reduce arrhythmia burden.

disposition.name: Admit to Telemetry/Cardiac Care
disposition.result: Admitted to telemetry for monitoring and ICD planning.
disposition.reasoning: Do NOT discharge. High sudden death risk until ICD placed. Must stay on continuous monitoring.

disposition.name: ICD Clinic Follow-up
disposition.result: ICD implanted during admission. Follow-up in device clinic in 2 weeks.
disposition.reasoning: Close device follow-up essential. Remote monitoring will be set up.

actualDiagnosis.name: Ventricular Tachycardia due to Ischemic Cardiomyopathy
keyFindings: Sudden syncope without prodrome
Prior MI with reduced ejection fraction (30%)
NSVT captured on ECG/monitoring
High PVC burden
Family history of sudden cardiac death
Signs of heart failure on exam
teachingPoints: Syncope WITHOUT prodrome = cardiac until proven otherwise
Prior MI + reduced EF = HIGH risk for VT and sudden death
EF ≤35% + syncope = ICD candidate

guidelineReferences.name: 2017 AHA/ACC/HRS Guideline for Management of Ventricular Arrhythmias and Prevention of Sudden Cardiac Death
guidelineReferences.citation: Al-Khatib SM et al. J Am Coll Cardiol. 2018;72(14):e91-e220
keyPoints: ICD recommended for EF ≤35% + NYHA Class II-III (Class I indication)
ICD for survivors of VT/VF not from reversible cause
Amiodarone may reduce ICD shocks but does not reduce mortality
Beta-blockers first-line medical therapy to reduce arrhythmia burden
Catheter ablation for recurrent VT or ICD shocks

guidelineReferences.name: 2022 ACC/AHA Guideline for Management of Heart Failure
guidelineReferences.citation: Heidenreich PA et al. J Am Coll Cardiol. 2022;79(17):e263-e421
keyPoints: ICD for primary prevention in HFrEF (EF ≤35%) on GDMT for 3+ months
Optimize GDMT before ICD consideration
CRT-D if LBBB with QRS ≥150ms
Shared decision-making for ICD in elderly/multiple comorbidities
Reassess need if EF improves to >35%

closureScript.diagnosis: You likely had a dangerous heart rhythm that made you pass out. Your weakened heart from the prior heart attack puts you at risk for this.
closureScript.plan: I'm admitting you for continuous heart monitoring. You'll see the electrophysiology team about a defibrillator to protect you.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: Don't drive until cleared. If you feel heart racing or faint, lie down immediately.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: A defibrillator can be life-saving - it can shock your heart back to normal if a dangerous rhythm occurs.
