whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient was a young athlete with family history of sudden death?
whatIfScenarios.description: 20-year-old collapsed during basketball game, ECG shows prolonged QTc

chiefComplaint.original: I passed out suddenly
chiefComplaint.modified: 20-year-old collapsed playing basketball. Family history: cousin died suddenly at 18. ECG shows QTc 520ms.

shouldRise: Need for beta-blocker and possible ICD

feedback.correct: Long QT syndrome: QTc >470ms (males) or >480ms (females) with symptoms = high risk. Treatment: Beta-blockers (reduce sudden death), avoid QT-prolonging drugs, ICD if prior arrest or syncope on therapy. Genetic testing. Screen family members!
feedback.partial: Good recognition of LQTS. What's the treatment?
feedback.incorrect: Long QT syndrome is life-threatening. QTc >500ms = very high risk. Treatment: beta-blockers (nadolol preferred), avoid QT-prolonging drugs (extensive list), ICD for syncope despite therapy or prior arrest. Genetic testing guides prognosis. All first-degree relatives need ECG screening.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Ischemic cardiomyopathy VT = structural heart, ICD for low EF. LQTS = electrical disease, beta-blockers first, ICD if high-risk. Different pathophysiology, different primary treatment.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient had bradycardia and complete heart block?
whatIfScenarios.description: Elderly patient, HR 35, ECG shows complete heart block

chiefComplaint.original: I passed out suddenly
chiefComplaint.modified: 80-year-old with syncope. HR 35, BP 90/60. ECG shows complete heart block with wide escape rhythm.

expectedReasoning.topDiagnosis: Complete heart block causing syncope (Stokes-Adams attack)

feedback.correct: Complete heart block with syncope = PACEMAKER indication (Class I). Temporary pacing if hemodynamically unstable. Atropine often ineffective in complete block. Permanent pacemaker before discharge. Check for reversible causes (Lyme, meds) but usually degenerative.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of pacemaker need. What about temporary measures?
feedback.incorrect: Complete heart block: no atrial impulses reach ventricles. Syncope = Stokes-Adams attack. Emergency: transcutaneous pacing if unstable, then transvenous wire. Atropine usually ineffective (block is below AV node). Permanent pacemaker is definitive treatment. Check for reversible causes but rare.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: VT = too fast, need ICD. Complete heart block = too slow, need pacemaker. Both cause syncope but opposite mechanisms and treatments.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if ECG showed coved ST elevation in V1-V2?
whatIfScenarios.description: Patient from Southeast Asia with syncope, characteristic Brugada pattern

chiefComplaint.original: I passed out suddenly
chiefComplaint.modified: 35-year-old Filipino man, syncope during sleep. ECG shows coved ST elevation in V1-V2 with T-wave inversion (type 1 Brugada pattern).

mustNotMiss: High risk for VF during sleep/rest

feedback.correct: Brugada syndrome: channelopathy causing VF, often during sleep/rest (vagal tone). Type 1 pattern + syncope = ICD indication. More common in Southeast Asian men. Avoid fever (can trigger), avoid certain drugs (Na channel blockers). Genetic testing and family screening.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of Brugada. When is ICD indicated?
feedback.incorrect: Brugada syndrome: sodium channelopathy causing VF. Type 1 (coved) pattern + symptoms = highest risk. Treatment: ICD for syncope or resuscitated arrest. Avoid: fever (treat aggressively), procainamide/flecainide, excessive alcohol. Quinidine may help some. Screen family members.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Ischemic VT = structural heart disease. Brugada = structurally normal, electrical disease. Both need ICD for syncope but different patient demographics and triggers.

questions.stem: Based on the clinical presentation, which is the MOST appropriate next step?

options.text: Systematic history and focused physical examination
options.explanation: A targeted history and physical exam is the essential first step in clinical reasoning, guiding hypothesis generation and differential diagnosis.
Empiric treatment before adequate evaluation risks missing the correct diagnosis and may cause harm.
Diagnostic testing should be hypothesis-driven, not shotgun; targeted tests based on clinical reasoning yield better outcomes.
Consultation may be appropriate later but should follow initial clinical assessment and diagnostic reasoning.

questions.stem: Which cognitive bias poses the greatest risk in this clinical scenario?

options.explanation: Availability bias (overweighting recent experiences) is common but not the most frequent source of error in this scenario.
Anchoring (fixating on initial impression) is a risk but can be mitigated by systematic differential generation.
Premature closure — accepting a diagnosis before it is fully verified — is the single most common cognitive error in clinical reasoning across all settings.
Confirmation bias (seeking only supporting evidence) contributes to error but often follows from premature closure.

guidelineReferences.name: ESC Guidelines for Pericardial Diseases 2015
guidelineReferences.citation: Adler Y, et al. Eur Heart J. 2015;36:2921-2964
keyPoints: Diagnosis: 2 of 4 criteria (chest pain, friction rub, ECG changes, effusion)
NSAIDs + Colchicine is first-line therapy
Colchicine reduces recurrence risk by 50%
Avoid corticosteroids initially (increase recurrence)
Echocardiogram to assess for effusion/tamponade
High-risk: fever >38°C, subacute onset, large effusion, tamponade

male-28-viral.name: Theodore Martinez

missedConsequences.Acute Pericarditis: Untreated pericarditis can lead to recurrent episodes. Rarely progresses to constrictive pericarditis.
missedConsequences.Myopericarditis: Missed myocardial involvement can lead to arrhythmias or heart failure if not recognized.
missedConsequences.Acute Coronary Syndrome: Young patients can have MI (especially with cocaine use). Must always consider.
missedConsequences.Pericardial Effusion/Tamponade: Effusion can develop and compromise hemodynamics.

commonPitfalls: Dismissing chest pain in young patient without full evaluation
Missing the positional nature of the pain (worse lying flat, better leaning forward)
Not hearing the pericardial friction rub (must listen with patient leaning forward)
Mistaking diffuse ST elevation for STEMI and activating cath lab
Not checking for myocardial involvement (troponin) - myopericarditis affects management
Forgetting to rule out other causes of pericarditis (autoimmune, uremic, malignant, TB)

mcqs.question: What characteristic of chest pain is most suggestive of pericarditis?

options.text: Pain radiating to the left arm
options.explanation: Arm radiation suggests cardiac ischemia (ACS).
Nitro relief suggests angina.

options.text: Sharp pain worse with inspiration and lying flat, improved leaning forward
options.explanation: Pleuritic, positional chest pain is classic for pericarditis. Pain improves leaning forward because it reduces pericardial stretch.

options.text: Pain with exertion, relieved by rest
options.explanation: Exertional pattern suggests stable angina.

mcqs.question: How do you distinguish the ECG findings of pericarditis from STEMI?

options.explanation: Pericarditis has ST elevation, not depression.

options.text: Pericarditis has diffuse ST elevation with PR depression, no reciprocal changes
options.explanation: Pericarditis: diffuse concave-up ST elevation in multiple territories, PR depression (especially in II), no reciprocal changes. STEMI: territorial ST elevation with reciprocal changes.
Q waves indicate myocardial infarction, not pericarditis.

options.text: Pericarditis has ST elevation only in V1-V4
options.explanation: Territorial elevation suggests STEMI (LAD territory). Pericarditis is diffuse.

allDiagnoses.name: Acute Pericarditis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Young male with sharp central chest pain and recent viral illness. Need to assess for positional features and friction rub.

allDiagnoses.name: Musculoskeletal Chest Pain
allDiagnoses.rationale: Sharp, localized pain in young person. Common cause of chest pain. Would be reproducible with palpation.

allDiagnoses.name: Acute Coronary Syndrome
allDiagnoses.rationale: Must always consider ACS even in young patients. Ask about cocaine use. Check ECG and troponin.

allDiagnoses.name: Myopericarditis
allDiagnoses.rationale: If pericarditis, must check troponin - elevated troponin indicates myocardial involvement.

allDiagnoses.name: Pulmonary Embolism
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can cause pleuritic chest pain. Assess for risk factors.

allDiagnoses.name: Pneumothorax
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can cause sudden sharp chest pain. Would have respiratory findings and abnormal CXR.

allDiagnoses.name: Aortic Dissection
allDiagnoses.rationale: Severe chest pain radiating to back. Rare in young but catastrophic if missed.

allDiagnoses.name: Pneumonia/Pleuritis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can cause pleuritic pain. Recent URI could have progressed. Check for cough, fever.

allDiagnoses.name: GERD/Esophageal Spasm
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can cause chest pain but typically burning, related to meals. No fever.

allDiagnoses.name: Costochondritis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Reproducible with palpation of costochondral junctions. No systemic symptoms.

allDiagnoses.name: Anxiety/Panic
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can cause chest pain but typically with other anxiety symptoms. Diagnosis of exclusion.

male-28-viral.oldcartsNarrative: "This sharp pain in my chest started 2 days ago. It's right in the center of my chest. It's sharp, like a stabbing feeling, probably 7 out of 10. It's been pretty constant since it started. I had a bad cold about a week ago - sore throat, runny nose, felt achy. I thought I was over it, but now this chest pain. I also feel a little feverish today."

historyQuestions.text: Tell me more about how position affects the pain.
historyQuestions.answer: Lying flat is the worst - feels like something is pressing on my chest. Leaning forward like this (demonstrates) definitely helps. I've been sleeping propped up.
historyQuestions.reasoning: POSITIONAL PAIN - worse supine, better leaning forward - is classic for pericarditis. Reduces stretch on inflamed pericardium.

historyQuestions.text: Does breathing affect the pain?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes! Deep breaths make it stab. I've been taking shallow breaths to avoid it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: PLEURITIC pain (worse with inspiration) is characteristic of pericarditis.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about the illness you had before this.
historyQuestions.answer: Started about 10 days ago - sore throat, stuffy nose, body aches, low fever. Felt like a typical cold. Was better for a couple days, then this chest pain started.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Recent viral URI is classic prodrome. Post-viral pericarditis is most common cause in young adults.

historyQuestions.text: Does the pain go anywhere else?
historyQuestions.answer: Sometimes to my left shoulder and neck. And I feel it in my back between my shoulder blades.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Pericardial pain can radiate to trapezius ridge (phrenic nerve innervation of pericardium).

historyQuestions.text: Does exertion or activity make it worse?
historyQuestions.answer: Not really exertion specifically. It's more the breathing and position that matters. Walking around doesn't make it worse unless I'm breathing hard.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Non-exertional pain argues against typical angina. Important distinction.

historyQuestions.text: Any drug use, including cocaine or other stimulants?
historyQuestions.answer: No, nothing like that. I don't do drugs. Maybe drink a few beers on weekends.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Cocaine is important cause of MI in young patients. Must ask.

historyQuestions.text: Any history of autoimmune diseases, kidney problems, or cancer?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I'm healthy. No medical problems. Don't take any medications.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Rules out autoimmune, uremic, or malignant pericarditis.

historyQuestions.text: What medications do you take?
historyQuestions.answer: Nothing. I'm healthy. Just take ibuprofen sometimes for headaches.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No medications - important baseline; ibuprofen will be treatment

historyQuestions.text: Any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important before prescribing NSAIDs and colchicine

historyQuestions.text: Any other medical problems?
historyQuestions.answer: None. I've always been healthy. Never been hospitalized.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No cardiac history or autoimmune disease

historyQuestions.text: Any family history of heart problems at young age?
historyQuestions.answer: No. My dad has high blood pressure but that's it. Nobody had heart attacks young.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No premature CAD in family - lowers cardiac risk

historyQuestions.text: Any shortness of breath?
historyQuestions.answer: A little when lying flat. But I think it's because lying down hurts. I'm not gasping for air or anything.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Dyspnea could indicate effusion or myocardial involvement

historyQuestions.text: Any fainting or near-fainting?
historyQuestions.answer: No, nothing like that.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Syncope would suggest hemodynamic compromise from effusion/tamponade

historyQuestions.text: Any swelling in your legs or calves?
historyQuestions.answer: No swelling.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Assessing for DVT as PE risk factor

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

social_viral_exposure.answer: My girlfriend was sick about 2 weeks ago with the same cold symptoms. A few people at work too.
social_viral_exposure.relevance: Viral exposure history supports viral etiology. Post-viral pericarditis is most common type in young adults.

occupation_physical_demands.answer: Software developer. Desk job. I go to the gym 3-4 times a week though - lifting weights, cardio.
occupation_physical_demands.relevance: Exercise will need to be restricted until pericarditis resolves to reduce risk of myocarditis complications.

social_stress_factors.answer: Work has been stressful with a big project deadline. Probably ran myself down, which is why I got sick.
social_stress_factors.relevance: Stress and overwork may have contributed to viral susceptibility and immune response.

social_substance_use.answer: No drugs ever. Social drinking on weekends. No smoking or vaping.
social_substance_use.relevance: Confirms no cocaine use (which would make ACS more likely).

recreation_recent_activity.answer: I tried to work out a couple days ago even though I still felt a little sick. Maybe pushed too hard.
recreation_recent_activity.relevance: Exercise during viral illness may have contributed to pericardial inflammation.

social_travel_history.relevance: Rules out TB exposure or other travel-related causes of pericarditis.

physicalExam.finding: BP 118/72, HR 92 (mild tachycardia), RR 18, Temp 100.2°F (low-grade fever), SpO2 99%.
physicalExam.reasoning: Low-grade fever and tachycardia consistent with inflammatory/infectious process.

physicalExam.finding: Young man in mild distress, sitting forward in bed. Appears uncomfortable when repositioning. Taking shallow breaths.
physicalExam.reasoning: Preference for sitting forward (tripod position) and shallow breathing to minimize pain.

physicalExam.text: Cardiac auscultation with patient leaning forward
physicalExam.finding: Pericardial friction rub heard at left lower sternal border with patient leaning forward at end-expiration. High-pitched, scratchy, three-component rub (systolic, early diastolic, late diastolic). Rub is evanescent - comes and goes.
physicalExam.reasoning: PERICARDIAL FRICTION RUB is pathognomonic. Must listen with patient leaning forward, at end-expiration. Three-component rub is classic.

physicalExam.finding: No chest wall tenderness. Pain is not reproducible with palpation.
physicalExam.reasoning: Non-reproducible pain rules out costochondritis/MSK cause.

physicalExam.finding: JVP normal, no elevation. No Kussmaul's sign.
physicalExam.reasoning: Normal JVP argues against significant effusion or tamponade.

physicalExam.finding: S1 and S2 normal intensity, not muffled. Regular rhythm. No S3 or S4.
physicalExam.reasoning: Clear heart sounds argue against large effusion (would cause muffled sounds).

physicalExam.finding: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No pleural rub.
physicalExam.reasoning: Clear lungs make pulmonary causes less likely.

physicalExam.finding: No peripheral edema. Pulses full and equal.
physicalExam.reasoning: No signs of heart failure or vascular compromise.

labs.name: Troponin
labs.reasoning: IMPORTANT: Elevated troponin indicates myopericarditis (myocardial involvement). Affects prognosis and requires stricter activity restriction.

labs.name: CBC
labs.result: WBC 11.2 (mild leukocytosis), normal differential. Hgb 14.8, Plt 245.
labs.reasoning: Mild leukocytosis consistent with viral/inflammatory process.

labs.name: BMP
labs.reasoning: Rules out uremic pericarditis. Baseline before NSAIDs.

labs.name: CRP
labs.reasoning: Elevated CRP confirms inflammation. Used to guide duration of treatment - continue meds until normalized.

labs.name: ESR
labs.reasoning: Additional marker of inflammation. Slower to normalize than CRP.

labs.name: ANA (if recurrent or atypical)
labs.result: Not indicated for first episode with clear viral prodrome.
labs.reasoning: Consider autoimmune workup if recurrent pericarditis or atypical features.

labs.name: TSH
labs.reasoning: Hypothyroidism can cause pericardial effusion.

imaging.name: ECG
imaging.result: Diffuse concave-upward ST elevation in leads I, II, III, aVF, V3-V6. PR depression in lead II. PR elevation in aVR. No reciprocal ST depression. No Q waves.
imaging.reasoning: CLASSIC pericarditis ECG: diffuse ST elevation (concave up), PR depression in limb leads, PR elevation in aVR. NO reciprocal changes (unlike STEMI).

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.result: Heart size normal. No cardiomegaly. Lungs clear. No effusion.
imaging.reasoning: Normal heart size suggests no large pericardial effusion. Enlarged cardiac silhouette would prompt urgent echo.

imaging.name: Echocardiogram
imaging.result: Normal LV size and function, EF 60%. Small circumferential pericardial effusion (no hemodynamic significance). No tamponade physiology. No wall motion abnormalities.
imaging.reasoning: Small effusion is common in pericarditis. Assesses for tamponade and myocardial function. Wall motion abnormalities suggest myocarditis.

treatments.name: Ibuprofen 600mg PO TID (FIRST-LINE NSAID)
treatments.result: Ibuprofen 600mg TID with food started. Continue until symptoms resolve AND CRP normalizes, then taper over 1-2 weeks.
treatments.reasoning: NSAIDs are first-line for pericarditis. Ibuprofen preferred due to better side effect profile than indomethacin. High-dose aspirin (750-1000mg TID) is alternative.

treatments.name: Colchicine 0.5mg BID (ESSENTIAL ADD-ON)
treatments.result: Colchicine 0.5mg BID started. Continue for 3 months for first episode. REDUCES RECURRENCE BY 50%.
treatments.reasoning: ALWAYS add colchicine - reduces recurrence from 30% to 15%. COPE and ICAP trials showed clear benefit. 0.5mg daily if <70kg. Continue 3 months (first episode) or 6 months (recurrence).

treatments.name: PPI for GI protection
treatments.result: Omeprazole 20mg daily for GI protection during high-dose NSAID therapy.
treatments.reasoning: GI protection needed with high-dose NSAID therapy. PPI preferred over H2 blockers.

treatments.name: ACTIVITY RESTRICTION - CRITICAL
treatments.result: NO strenuous exercise or athletics until: (1) symptoms resolved, (2) CRP normalized, (3) if troponin elevated (myopericarditis): minimum 3-6 months restriction.
treatments.reasoning: CRITICAL: Exercise during myopericarditis can cause arrhythmias and sudden death. This patient has elevated troponin - strict activity restriction for 3-6 months minimum.

treatments.name: Avoid corticosteroids (usually)
treatments.result: Steroids NOT recommended for viral pericarditis - increase recurrence risk.
treatments.reasoning: Steroids increase recurrence rate and should be avoided in viral/idiopathic pericarditis. Reserve for: autoimmune pericarditis, contraindication to NSAIDs/colchicine, or refractory cases.

treatments.name: Aspirin 750-1000mg TID (alternative NSAID)
treatments.result: Alternative to ibuprofen. Preferred if patient on anticoagulation.
treatments.reasoning: High-dose aspirin is alternative first-line NSAID. Preferred if patient already on anticoagulation (can use with aspirin, avoid ibuprofen with warfarin).

consults.name: Cardiology (for myopericarditis)
consults.result: Cardiology consulted given elevated troponin. Will follow for activity clearance.
consults.reasoning: Cardiology involvement recommended when troponin elevated (myopericarditis). They will guide return-to-activity clearance.

disposition.name: Discharge home with close follow-up
disposition.result: Stable for discharge. Follow-up in 1-2 weeks with repeat CRP. Cardiology follow-up for activity clearance.
disposition.reasoning: Uncomplicated pericarditis (small effusion, stable vitals, no tamponade) can be managed outpatient. Need close follow-up for CRP-guided treatment duration.

disposition.name: Admission criteria (when to admit)
disposition.result: Would admit for: high fever (>38°C), large effusion, tamponade physiology, failure to respond to NSAIDs, immunosuppression, trauma, anticoagulation.
disposition.reasoning: High-risk features requiring admission: Fever >38°C, large effusion, tamponade, anticoagulation use, immunosuppression, traumatic etiology, failure to respond to NSAIDs in 1 week.

disposition.name: Return precautions
disposition.result: Return immediately for: worsening shortness of breath (especially when lying flat), lightheadedness/fainting, chest pain that worsens despite medications, new leg swelling.
disposition.reasoning: Tamponade can develop. Symptoms: progressive dyspnea, orthopnea, lightheadedness. Must educate patient on warning signs.

disposition.name: Follow-up plan
disposition.result: Repeat CRP in 1-2 weeks. Continue medications until CRP normalizes, then taper NSAIDs over 1-2 weeks. Continue colchicine 3 months total.
disposition.reasoning: CRP-guided therapy duration. Taper NSAIDs (don't stop abruptly) to reduce recurrence. Colchicine for full 3 months regardless of CRP.

disposition.name: Return to activity counseling (athletes)
disposition.result: NO competitive sports or strenuous exercise. Cardiology will clear for activity after: asymptomatic, CRP normalized, troponin normalized (if elevated), and echo shows resolved effusion. Minimum 3 months for myopericarditis.
disposition.reasoning: Premature return to athletics is dangerous, especially with myocardial involvement. European Society of Cardiology recommends 3 months minimum for myopericarditis.

actualDiagnosis.name: Acute Viral Pericarditis with Myopericarditis
keyFindings: Sharp pleuritic chest pain, worse supine, better leaning forward
Pericardial friction rub on auscultation
Recent viral prodrome (URI one week prior)
Diffuse ST elevation with PR depression on ECG
Elevated inflammatory markers (CRP, ESR)
Mildly elevated troponin (indicates myocardial involvement)
Small pericardial effusion on echo without tamponade
teachingPoints: Pericarditis pain: pleuritic (worse with inspiration), positional (worse supine, better leaning forward), often with friction rub
Pericardial friction rub: high-pitched, scratchy, up to 3 components - listen with patient leaning forward at end-expiration
ECG: diffuse ST elevation (concave up), PR depression, NO reciprocal changes - distinguishes from STEMI
Always check troponin - elevated troponin = myopericarditis which requires echo and activity restriction
Most pericarditis in young adults is viral/idiopathic and self-limited
Treatment: NSAIDs (ibuprofen or aspirin) PLUS colchicine - colchicine reduces recurrence by 50%
Activity restriction essential, especially with myocardial involvement - no athletics until CRP normalized
Consider other causes: autoimmune, uremic, TB, malignancy, post-MI (Dressler's) if not typical viral prodrome

closureScript.diagnosis: You have pericarditis - inflammation of the sac around your heart. Based on your recent cold, this is almost certainly viral pericarditis, which is the most common type in young healthy people like you. Your troponin is slightly elevated, which means there's a small amount of inflammation affecting the heart muscle itself - we call this myopericarditis.
closureScript.plan: Treatment is anti-inflammatory medications. I'm starting you on ibuprofen three times daily plus a medication called colchicine which significantly reduces the chance of this coming back. You'll need to take these for several weeks. Because your troponin is elevated, you'll need to avoid all strenuous exercise and athletics until your inflammation markers normalize - this is very important.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: Come back immediately if you develop shortness of breath, feel faint, notice your legs swelling, or if the pain gets much worse. These could indicate fluid building up around your heart. Also, absolutely no intense exercise until we clear you - exercising with myocarditis can be dangerous.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: Viral pericarditis usually resolves completely within 1-3 weeks. The main risk is recurrence - that's why we add colchicine. Most people make a full recovery with no long-term issues. We'll recheck your labs in 1-2 weeks to make sure the inflammation is resolving.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if there was hypotension and JVD?
whatIfScenarios.description: Pericarditis with large effusion, hypotension, JVD, and muffled heart sounds

chiefComplaint.original: Sharp chest pain that's worse when I lie down
chiefComplaint.modified: Chest pain, now I can barely breathe lying down, and I feel like I'm going to pass out

feedback.correct: BECK'S TRIAD = hypotension + JVD + muffled heart sounds = TAMPONADE. This is a LIFE-THREATENING emergency. Bedside echo confirms. Treatment: emergent pericardiocentesis. IV fluids as temporizing measure (preload dependent).
feedback.partial: Good recognition of tamponade. What's Beck's triad?
feedback.incorrect: Cardiac tamponade: fluid compresses the heart preventing filling. Beck's triad = hypotension, JVD, muffled heart sounds. Pulsus paradoxus >10 mmHg. Echo shows diastolic RV collapse. Emergent pericardiocentesis is life-saving.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Uncomplicated pericarditis = NSAIDs + colchicine, outpatient. Tamponade = emergent pericardiocentesis. JVD + hypotension should trigger immediate concern for tamponade.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient was on dialysis?
whatIfScenarios.description: Pericarditis in patient with ESRD, hasn't been to dialysis in a week

chiefComplaint.original: Sharp chest pain that's worse when I lie down
chiefComplaint.modified: Chest pain, and I haven't made it to dialysis in a week. I have kidney failure.

keyDiscriminators: May not have classic ECG changes

feedback.correct: Uremic pericarditis = indication for dialysis. Treatment is DIALYSIS (heparin-free to avoid hemorrhagic conversion), NOT NSAIDs. ECG may not show typical changes. Higher risk of hemorrhagic effusion and tamponade.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of uremic cause. How does treatment differ?
feedback.incorrect: Uremic pericarditis occurs when BUN >60 mg/dL. Treatment = DIALYSIS (use heparin-free). NSAIDs don't work and are contraindicated in renal failure anyway. Higher risk of hemorrhagic effusion → tamponade. May need daily dialysis until resolved.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Viral pericarditis = NSAIDs + colchicine. Uremic pericarditis = dialysis is the treatment. The cause determines the therapy entirely.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if this occurred 2 weeks after an MI?
whatIfScenarios.description: Chest pain with friction rub 2 weeks after acute MI

chiefComplaint.original: Sharp chest pain that's worse when I lie down
chiefComplaint.modified: I had a heart attack 2 weeks ago. Now I have new chest pain that's different - worse when I breathe.

feedback.correct: Dressler syndrome = autoimmune pericarditis 1-8 weeks post-MI. Must first rule out recurrent MI with ECG and troponins! Treatment: aspirin (preferred over other NSAIDs post-MI) + colchicine. Consider steroids if refractory.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of Dressler syndrome. What must you rule out first?
feedback.incorrect: Dressler syndrome (post-MI pericarditis): autoimmune, occurs 1-8 weeks post-MI. Features: fever, pleuritic chest pain, friction rub, elevated ESR/CRP. MUST rule out recurrent ischemia first. Treat with aspirin (not ibuprofen - aspirin preferred post-MI) + colchicine.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Idiopathic pericarditis = NSAIDs + colchicine. Dressler syndrome = aspirin + colchicine (aspirin preferred post-MI). Always rule out recurrent MI first in any post-MI chest pain.

female-45-bolivia.chiefComplaint: Fatigue and leg swelling for several months

female-45-bolivia.oldcartsNarrative: "Progressively short of breath for 6 months. Swelling in legs. Palpitations. Born in rural Bolivia - grew up in adobe house. Moved to US 20 years ago. Remember bugs that bit us at night - we called them 'vinchucas.' Many family members back home have heart problems."

historyQuestions.text: Where were you born?
historyQuestions.answer: Rural Bolivia, lived in adobe house until age 25. Very poor area.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Bolivia has highest Chagas prevalence. Adobe houses harbor triatomine bugs.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about the bugs.
historyQuestions.answer: 'Vinchucas' - kissing bugs. They lived in the mud walls and bit us at night. Everyone had them.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Triatomine bugs transmit Trypanosoma cruzi.

historyQuestions.text: Family heart disease?
historyQuestions.answer: My mother died of heart failure at 50. Two uncles have pacemakers. Very common in our village.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Family clustering reflects shared exposure, not genetics.

historyQuestions.text: What medications are you currently taking?
historyQuestions.answer: Just blood pressure medicine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medications can cause symptoms and affect treatment choices.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No known drug allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical for safe prescribing.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any other medical conditions?
historyQuestions.answer: High blood pressure. Otherwise healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Past medical history affects diagnosis and treatment.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

geographic_country_origin.answer: Rural Bolivia - endemic for Chagas. Adobe housing with triatomine bugs.
geographic_country_origin.relevance: Bolivia has highest Chagas prevalence worldwide. Adobe walls harbor vectors.

housing_childhood.answer: Adobe/mud brick house with thatched roof. Bugs in walls.
housing_childhood.relevance: Triatomine bugs ('kissing bugs') live in cracks of adobe walls.

physicalExam.finding: JVP elevated. S3 gallop. Displaced PMI. Irregular rhythm.
physicalExam.reasoning: Heart failure with possible arrhythmia.

physicalExam.finding: 2+ bilateral lower extremity edema.
physicalExam.reasoning: Right heart failure.

labs.name: Chagas serology (T. cruzi antibodies)
labs.result: IgG POSITIVE by two different methods
labs.reasoning: DIAGNOSTIC - two positive tests confirms chronic infection.

imaging.name: Echocardiogram

imaging.reasoning: Apical aneurysm is highly characteristic of Chagas.

imaging.name: Holter monitor
imaging.result: Frequent PVCs, runs of NSVT, right bundle branch block.
imaging.reasoning: Arrhythmias common and cause sudden death.

treatments.name: Heart failure therapy
treatments.result: ACEi, beta-blocker, diuretic, aldosterone antagonist
treatments.reasoning: Standard HF management.

treatments.name: Benznidazole
treatments.result: Consider antiparasitic therapy - may slow progression
treatments.reasoning: Evidence strongest for recent infection but may help chronic.

treatments.name: ICD evaluation
treatments.result: Given EF 25% and NSVT, likely candidate for ICD
treatments.reasoning: Sudden cardiac death is major cause of mortality.

consults.name: Infectious Disease
consults.result: Chagas expertise for antiparasitic decision
consults.reasoning: Specialized knowledge needed.

consults.name: Electrophysiology
consults.result: Arrhythmia management and ICD evaluation
consults.reasoning: High arrhythmia burden.

chf-exacerbation.name: Dorothy P.
chf-exacerbation.chiefComplaint: Can't breathe when I lie down
chf-exacerbation.variantDescription: Acute decompensated heart failure with medication optimization and transition to outpatient management

longitudinalDays.title: Day 0: Emergency Department
longitudinalDays.presentation: 68-year-old woman with history of HFrEF (EF 35%), HTN, and diabetes presents with 1 week of progressive shortness of breath, orthopnea (sleeps in recliner), and leg swelling. She admits to running out of her 'water pill' 2 weeks ago and eating more salty foods over the holidays.

historyQuestions.text: When did you start feeling short of breath?
historyQuestions.answer: Started about a week ago. At first just with walking, but now I can't even lie flat. I've been sleeping in my recliner for 3 nights.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Progressive dyspnea with orthopnea classic for CHF exacerbation

historyQuestions.text: How many pillows do you sleep on?
historyQuestions.answer: I can't use pillows anymore - I have to sit up in my recliner or I feel like I'm drowning.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Severe orthopnea indicates significant pulmonary congestion

historyQuestions.text: Do you wake up at night gasping for air?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes! It's terrifying. I wake up choking, have to sit up and catch my breath.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea is highly specific for heart failure

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your legs.
historyQuestions.answer: They're huge. My ankles look like tree trunks. Can barely get my shoes on.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Bilateral lower extremity edema from venous congestion

historyQuestions.text: What medications are you taking?
historyQuestions.answer: Lisinopril, carvedilol, I was on furosemide but I ran out 2 weeks ago. Also metformin for diabetes.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CRITICAL: Diuretic non-adherence is the most common cause of CHF exacerbation!

historyQuestions.text: How has your diet been?
historyQuestions.answer: Well, it was the holidays... I had ham, lots of cookies, probably didn't watch my salt like I should.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Dietary indiscretion (high sodium) is second most common exacerbation trigger

historyQuestions.text: Any chest pain?
historyQuestions.answer: No chest pain, just can't breathe.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Rules out ACS as precipitant, though ischemia can trigger CHF

historyQuestions.text: What could you do before this started?
historyQuestions.answer: I could walk around the grocery store, do my housework, go up one flight of stairs slowly. Now I can't even walk to the bathroom.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Establishes baseline functional status (NYHA Class II at baseline, now Class IV)

physicalExam.finding: Elderly woman in moderate respiratory distress. Speaking in short sentences. Sitting upright on stretcher, refuses to lie flat.
physicalExam.reasoning: Refusing to lie flat confirms significant orthopnea

physicalExam.finding: JVP elevated to angle of jaw (>15 cm H2O). Positive hepatojugular reflux.
physicalExam.reasoning: ELEVATED JVP is the most specific exam finding for volume overload in CHF

physicalExam.finding: PMI displaced laterally. S1/S2 present. S3 gallop heard at apex. Grade 2/6 holosystolic murmur at apex radiating to axilla.
physicalExam.reasoning: S3 GALLOP is specific for elevated filling pressures. Murmur suggests mitral regurgitation (functional from LV dilation).

physicalExam.finding: Crackles bilateral bases to mid-lung fields. No wheezes. Dullness at right base suggesting small effusion.
physicalExam.reasoning: Pulmonary edema causing crackles. Pleural effusion common in CHF.

physicalExam.finding: 3+ pitting edema bilateral lower extremities to mid-shin. Skin intact, no ulcers. Warm extremities.
physicalExam.reasoning: Significant peripheral edema from right-sided congestion. Warm = preserved perfusion (not in cardiogenic shock).

physicalExam.finding: Soft, non-tender. Liver palpable 3cm below costal margin, slightly tender. Positive hepatojugular reflux.
physicalExam.reasoning: Hepatomegaly from hepatic congestion (right heart failure)

availableImaging.name: Chest X-ray
availableImaging.result: Cardiomegaly. Bilateral pulmonary vascular congestion with cephalization. Bilateral pleural effusions, right greater than left. Kerley B lines present. No focal consolidation.

labs.name: BNP or NT-proBNP
labs.reasoning: BNP >400 strongly supports CHF diagnosis. Also useful for prognosis and response to treatment.

labs.name: Troponin
labs.reasoning: Rules out acute MI as precipitant. Mild elevations can occur in CHF from demand ischemia.

labs.name: TSH
labs.reasoning: Thyroid disease can cause or exacerbate heart failure. Check in new or worsening CHF.

labs.name: Iron studies
labs.reasoning: Iron deficiency common in CHF and independently contributes to symptoms. IV iron improves symptoms even without anemia.

labs.name: Echocardiogram
labs.result: EF 30-35% (reduced from 35% 6 months ago). Moderate MR. LV dilation. Elevated filling pressures.
labs.reasoning: Confirms HFrEF. Documents severity and any new findings (worse EF, worsening MR).

treatments.name: Supplemental oxygen
treatments.result: 4L NC applied, SpO2 improved to 94%
treatments.reasoning: Treat hypoxemia. May need BiPAP if not improving.

treatments.name: IV Furosemide 80mg
treatments.result: Given as IV push. Plan for continuous or intermittent dosing.
treatments.reasoning: IV diuretics are cornerstone of ADHF treatment. Start with dose ≥ home oral dose. Her usual dose was 40mg PO - give 80mg IV (2x conversion).

treatments.name: Fluid restriction 1.5L/day
treatments.result: Nursing notified of restriction.
treatments.reasoning: Fluid restriction in hyponatremic CHF patients. Na 134 indicates significant neurohormonal activation.

treatments.name: Low sodium diet (<2g/day)
treatments.result: Diet order placed.
treatments.reasoning: Dietary sodium restriction essential in all heart failure patients

treatments.name: Daily weights
treatments.result: Standing order for daily AM weights.
treatments.reasoning: MOST IMPORTANT MONITORING PARAMETER. Target 1-2 kg (2-4 lb) loss per day with diuresis.

treatments.name: Hold metformin
treatments.result: Held due to acute kidney injury.
treatments.reasoning: Metformin contraindicated in AKI. Creatinine 1.4 with likely baseline around 1.0.

disposition.name: Admit to telemetry
disposition.result: Admit to cardiology service with telemetry monitoring.
disposition.reasoning: Requires IV diuretics, monitoring, and medication optimization

decisionPoint.question: You've confirmed acute decompensated heart failure. What is your initial diuretic strategy?

options.title: IV Furosemide 80mg, then 40mg IV q12h, target 2-3L negative per day

feedback.message: CORRECT! IV dose should be ≥ home oral dose (she was on 40mg PO, so 80mg IV is appropriate). Target 1-2 kg/day weight loss or 2-3L negative fluid balance. Monitor creatinine and potassium closely.

options.title: Restart her home Furosemide 40mg PO
options.description: Just restart what she was on

feedback.message: INADEQUATE! In acute decompensation, IV diuretics are required. Oral furosemide is only ~50% bioavailable, and gut edema in CHF reduces absorption further. She needs IV push or drip.

options.title: IV Furosemide drip at 5mg/hr plus chlorothiazide

feedback.message: This is aggressive. While furosemide drips and sequential nephron blockade are valid strategies, they're typically reserved for diuretic resistance, not first-line. Start with intermittent IV dosing and escalate if needed. Watch for excessive diuresis and worsening renal function.

teachingPoints: BNP INTERPRETATION: BNP >400 = CHF very likely. BNP <100 = CHF unlikely. Grey zone 100-400 needs clinical correlation.
IV DIURETIC DOSING: Give IV dose ≥ equivalent to home oral dose. Oral lasix is ~50% bioavailable.
DAILY WEIGHTS: Most important monitoring parameter. 1 kg = 1 L of fluid. Target 1-2 kg loss daily.
DON'T CHASE CREATININE: Cr may rise with diuresis (hemoconcentration). Mild rise is acceptable if patient is decongesting.

longitudinalDays.title: Days 1-2: Active Diuresis
longitudinalDays.nurseReport: Good urine output overnight - 2.4L in last 12 hours. Patient reports sleeping flat for first time in a week! Crackles improved but still present at bases. Edema improved - now 2+ instead of 3+. Ate 50% of breakfast.

decisionPoint.question: She's losing weight and feels better, but creatinine rose from 1.4 to 1.6. What do you do?

options.title: Continue current diuretic dose - she's still congested
options.description: Mild creatinine rise is expected with diuresis. She still has crackles and edema.

feedback.message: CORRECT! Mild creatinine rise during CHF treatment is expected (hemoconcentration) and acceptable. The key is clinical improvement - she slept flat and is decongesting. Stop chasing the creatinine and treat the congestion.

options.title: Stop diuretics - the creatinine is rising!
options.description: Protect her kidneys from further injury

feedback.message: WRONG! This is the classic mistake. A small creatinine rise during decongestion is expected and acceptable. Stopping diuretics will lead to re-accumulation of fluid and worsening symptoms. The kidney injury from congestion (cardiorenal syndrome) is worse than mild hemoconcentration.

teachingPoints: CREATININE WILL RISE: Expect 0.2-0.5 mg/dL increase during aggressive diuresis. This is hemoconcentration, not ATN.
CONGESTION IS BAD FOR KIDNEYS: Venous congestion causes renal dysfunction (cardiorenal syndrome). Decongestion often IMPROVES renal function long-term.
POTASSIUM MONITORING: Check K daily during diuresis. Replace PRN. Add K-sparing diuretic if needed.
IMPROVEMENT MARKERS: Sleeping flat, weight loss, improved edema, decreased crackles, improved BNP.

longitudinalDays.title: Days 3-4: Euvolemia and Transition
longitudinalDays.nurseReport: Excellent progress! Weight is back to her baseline of 175 lbs. Sleeping flat, no orthopnea. Edema resolved. Lungs clear. Walking in hallway without dyspnea. Ready to transition to oral meds.

physicalExam.finding: JVP now 7-8 cm H2O (normal). No hepatojugular reflux.
physicalExam.reasoning: Normalized JVP indicates successful decongestion

physicalExam.finding: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No crackles.
physicalExam.reasoning: Pulmonary congestion resolved

physicalExam.finding: Trace pedal edema bilaterally (her baseline). No pitting.
physicalExam.reasoning: Peripheral edema resolved to baseline

current.change: Convert to 60mg PO BID (increased from home 40mg daily)

toAdd.rationale: Mortality benefit in HFrEF (RALES trial). Monitor K.
SGLT2i - mortality benefit in HFrEF (EMPEROR-Reduced). Can use even without diabetes.

decisionPoint.question: She's euvolemic and stable. Time to optimize her GDMT (guideline-directed medical therapy). What do you add?

options.title: Add spironolactone 25mg AND empagliflozin 10mg
options.description: Quadruple therapy: ACEi + BB + MRA + SGLT2i

feedback.message: PERFECT! The 'Fantastic Four' of HFrEF: ACEi/ARB/ARNI + Beta-blocker + MRA (spironolactone) + SGLT2i (empagliflozin/dapagliflozin). Each independently reduces mortality. She should be on all four.

options.title: Just increase her home furosemide and discharge
options.description: She's better, just make sure she takes her water pill

feedback.message: SUBOPTIMAL! Every hospitalization is an opportunity to optimize GDMT. She's not on spironolactone or SGLT2i - both have mortality benefits. Diuretics treat symptoms but don't change prognosis.

teachingPoints: GDMT = GUIDELINE-DIRECTED MEDICAL THERAPY: The four pillars of HFrEF treatment.
THE FANTASTIC FOUR: ACEi/ARB/ARNI + Beta-blocker + MRA + SGLT2i. Each reduces mortality ~20-25%.
SGLT2 INHIBITORS: Empagliflozin and dapagliflozin now approved for HFrEF even WITHOUT diabetes.
HOSPITALIZATION = OPTIMIZATION OPPORTUNITY: Don't just treat the exacerbation - optimize the regimen.

longitudinalDays.title: Day 5: Discharge

dischargeMedications.notes: Restarted now that Cr back to baseline

decisionPoint.question: What is the most important discharge instruction for this patient?

options.title: Weigh yourself EVERY morning. Call if you gain >3 lbs in a day or >5 lbs in a week.

feedback.message: PERFECT! Daily weights are the single most important self-monitoring tool in heart failure. Weight gain = fluid retention = call for help BEFORE you end up in the ED again. She should know her target weight (175 lbs) and when to call.

options.title: Take all your medications every day

feedback.message: Medication adherence is important, but daily weights are the EARLY WARNING SYSTEM. She needs to know when she's retaining fluid BEFORE she's short of breath. Weight gain precedes symptoms by days. Early intervention prevents hospitalization.

teachingPoints: TARGET WEIGHT: Every CHF patient needs to know their 'dry weight.' For Dorothy, it's 175 lbs.
DAILY WEIGHTS: Same time, same scale, after voiding, before eating. Record and report if up >3 lbs/day or >5 lbs/week.
ZONE MANAGEMENT: Green (weight stable) = continue meds. Yellow (up 2-3 lbs) = call clinic. Red (up >5 lbs or SOB) = ED.
7-DAY FOLLOW-UP: CHF patients discharged with medication changes need early follow-up to check labs and symptoms.

longitudinalDays.title: Month 1: Cardiology Follow-up
longitudinalDays.patientUpdate: Dorothy returns for 1-month follow-up. She's been weighing herself daily - weight stable at 174-176 lbs. Taking all medications. Tolerating empagliflozin and spironolactone well. K 4.4, Cr 1.2 (improved!). Walking her dog again. Sleeping flat. No ER visits.

decisionPoint.question: She's doing great. Her BP is 112/68 and HR 64. Time to uptitrate GDMT?

feedback.message: CORRECT! Carvedilol target dose for HFrEF is 25mg BID (or equivalent). Beta-blockers reduce mortality in a dose-dependent manner. She's stable, so uptitrate. Could also consider switching ACEi to ARNI (sacubitril/valsartan) for additional benefit.

options.title: Keep current doses - don't rock the boat
options.description: She's doing well, why change anything?

feedback.message: SUBOPTIMAL! 'Start low, go slow, but GO!' Beta-blockers and ACEi/ARB/ARNI have dose-dependent mortality benefits. The goal is target doses, not just any dose. She's stable - this is the perfect time to uptitrate.

caseConclusion.summary: 68-year-old woman with HFrEF presenting with acute decompensation due to medication non-adherence and dietary indiscretion. Successfully treated with IV diuresis and discharged on optimized quadruple therapy (ACEi + BB + MRA + SGLT2i) with comprehensive self-management education.
keyDecisions: Recognized volume overload with JVP, S3, crackles, edema
Initiated appropriate IV diuretic dosing (not under-treating)
Continued diuresis despite mild creatinine rise (don't chase the Cr)
Achieved euvolemia based on weight, exam, and symptom resolution
Optimized GDMT by adding spironolactone and SGLT2i
Educated patient on daily weights and action thresholds
learningObjectives: Assess volume status using JVP, S3, edema, weight, and BNP
Apply appropriate IV diuretic strategies for ADHF
Understand that mild creatinine rise during diuresis is acceptable
Optimize HFrEF regimen with 'Fantastic Four' GDMT
Develop patient-centered discharge plans with self-monitoring

chf-exacerbation.clinicalPearl: The most common reason for heart failure hospitalization is medication non-adherence, especially to diuretics. The best intervention to prevent readmission is patient education: daily weights, sodium restriction, and knowing when to call. Technology helps too - some patients benefit from remote weight monitoring programs.

questions.stem: An ECG shows ST elevation in leads II, III, and aVF with reciprocal ST depression in I and aVL. Which coronary artery is most likely occluded?

options.explanation: ST elevation in inferior leads (II, III, aVF) with reciprocal changes in lateral leads (I, aVL) localizes to an inferior STEMI, most commonly caused by RCA occlusion (~85% of cases). Check right-sided leads (V4R) to assess for RV involvement.
LAD occlusion causes anterior STEMI with ST elevation in V1-V4 (± V5-V6), not inferior leads.
LCx causes lateral STEMI (I, aVL, V5-V6). While LCx can cause inferior changes in ~15% of cases (dominant LCx), RCA is far more common.
Left main occlusion causes widespread ST depression with ST elevation in aVR — a pattern of diffuse subendocardial ischemia, not isolated inferior STEMI.
